title: Isolated liver chemo perfusion for hepatic metastases from uveal melanoma: a report of 38 cases
journal: Indian Journal of Thoracic and Cardiovascular Surgery
summary: This case series of 38 patients evaluates isolated liver chemoperfusion as a regional chemotherapy approach for hepatic metastases from uveal melanoma. The procedure demonstrated clinical benefit, with a median overall survival of 20.3 months and disease-free survival of 5.4 months in treated patients. However, the treatment carried significant morbidity, with complications including bleeding in 7 patients, hepatic artery thrombosis in 1 patient requiring emergency repair, and liver failure accounting for 2 of 3 procedure-related deaths. Despite these serious adverse events, the authors conclude that isolated liver chemoperfusion represents a safe method of regional chemotherapy that should be considered for patients with isolated hepatic metastases from uveal melanoma, suggesting the survival benefit justifies the procedural risks in appropriately selected candidates.

title: Update on near infrared imaging technology: indocyanine green and near infrared technology in the treatment of gynecologic cancers
journal: International journal of gynecological cancer : official journal of the International Gynecological Cancer Society
summary:
# Summary

This review examines near-infrared (NIR) imaging with indocyanine green (ICG) as an intraoperative visualization tool in gynecologic surgery, with particular relevance to endometriosis procedures. ICG is optimally excited at 805 nm wavelength and penetrates tissue to approximately 5 mm depth, superior to conventional fluorescein imaging, while demonstrating a favorable safety profile with rare adverse reactions and no effects on blood constituents or hemostasis. Key clinical applications include retrograde ureteric injection to prevent iatrogenic ureteric injury during endometriosis surgery—a critical complication that can lead to fistula formation, sepsis, and renal failure—as well as sentinel lymph node mapping with detection rates of 95.7–100%, substantially exceeding blue dye alone (64.9–78.6%). Additionally, ICG-guided NIR angiography accurately predicts flap perfusion and necrosis in radical gynecologic reconstruction with 100% sensitivity for microvascular complications, reducing major adverse outcomes compared to clinical judgment alone.

title: The Association of Intra-Abdominal Adhesions with Peritoneal Dialysis Catheter-Related Complications
journal: Clinical Journal of the American Society of Nephrology : CJASN
summary: This study examined how intra-abdominal adhesions affect peritoneal dialysis (PD) catheter outcomes in kidney failure patients. Patients with adhesions experienced significantly higher rates of adverse outcomes (17% versus 10%), with lower abdominal or pelvic adhesions showing a 1.80-fold increased risk compared to those without adhesions. Risk factors for developing adhesions included female sex, elevated body mass index, and prior abdominal surgery—particularly procedures like hysterectomy (67%), kidney transplant (71%), and appendectomy (64%)—which are highly relevant to endometriosis populations given the gynecologic surgical history association. Adhesions reduced the effective peritoneal surface area and increased catheter complications including tip migration, lumen occlusion, and omental wrapping, with 39% of patients without initial adhesions developing new adhesions after PD catheter insertion. Laparoscopic procedures enabled direct visualization and adhesiolysis, which reduced the risk of adverse outcomes compared to cases where adhesiolysis was not performed. These findings underscore that adhesions represent a major modifiable risk factor for PD catheter dysfunction, with laparoscopic intervention offering superior diagnostic and therapeutic capability over radiologic approaches alone.

title: Our Body Our Health (Jirkeena, Caafimaadkeena): Somali Women’s Narratives on Sexual Health
summary: This article examines the health consequences of female genital cutting (FGC) among Somali women, with particular focus on sexual and psychological outcomes. Female genital cutting causes multiple severe complications including sexual pain, reduced sexual pleasure, urinary and fungal infections, and mental health symptoms such as PTSD and anxiety. The severity of sexual dysfunction increases with FGC Type 3 (infibulation), which involves clitoral tissue removal and results in rigid scar tissue formation, pelvic floor hypertonicity, and recurrent infections that collectively drive increased sexual pain during intercourse. Psychological factors including catastrophizing and fear-avoidance beliefs further amplify pain perception in affected women. The study identifies deinfibulation—surgical opening of infibulated tissue—as an effective intervention for reducing sexual pain symptoms. Notably, resilience factors such as positive social support and effective coping skills can buffer against the negative impacts of chronic pain, suggesting that comprehensive clinical care for Somali women with FGC histories requires addressing both the physical complications and psychological dimensions of sexual dysfunction.

title: Radical Oophorectomy for Advanced Ovarian Cancer: A Feasibility Study from Tertiary Care Cancer Centre in Eastern India
journal: Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology of India
summary: This feasibility study from an Eastern Indian tertiary cancer center demonstrates that radical oophorectomy is an effective and secure surgical approach for treating advanced ovarian cancer with extensive infiltration into adjacent pelvic organs and peritoneum. The procedure is stratified into two types: Type 1 involves retrograde modified radical hysterectomy with bilateral adnexal removal, while Type 2 additionally incorporates total parietal and visceral pelvic peritonectomy along with en bloc resection of the rectosigmoid colon for cases with more extensive disease involvement. Among 16 patients with advanced ovarian malignancies included in the study, radical oophorectomy proved feasible and secure as a surgical technique. The staged approach allows tailoring of surgical extent based on the degree of tumor infiltration, improving both feasibility and safety outcomes. These findings support radical oophorectomy as a viable surgical option for optimizing tumor cytoreduction in advanced ovarian cancer cases with significant peritoneal and visceral involvement.

title: Associations of tubal ligation and hysterectomy with serum androgen and estrogen metabolites among postmenopausal women in the Women’s Health Initiative Observational Study
summary:
# Summary

This Women's Health Initiative study examined how hysterectomy and tubal ligation alter circulating sex steroid hormones and their metabolites in postmenopausal women. Hysterectomy was strongly associated with reduced adrenal androgens, including lower levels of testosterone, DHEA, DHEAS, androstenedione, and androsterone, while also showing elevated circulating estrogen metabolites such as 2-hydroxyestrone-3-methyl ether and 4-methoxyestrone. In contrast, tubal ligation showed no significant associations with circulating androgen or estrogen levels. The hormone alterations observed with hysterectomy may have clinical implications, as postmenopausal testosterone elevation is linked to increased ovarian and breast cancer risk, while shifts in the androgen-estrogen balance could promote pro-inflammatory responses. These findings suggest that hysterectomy-induced changes in hormone metabolism may contribute to altered disease risk profiles in postmenopausal women, including cardiovascular disease.

title: Comparing Thermal Damage Using Monopolar Hook Versus Harmonic Scalpel in Total Laparoscopic Hysterectomy; A Double-Blind Randomized Controlled Trial
journal: Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology of India
summary: This double-blind randomized controlled trial compared thermal damage profiles between monopolar hook and harmonic scalpel instruments during total laparoscopic hysterectomy for endometriosis. Monopolar hook use was associated with significantly higher thermal damage and injury, particularly in the right lateral operative field, whereas harmonic scalpel technology produced substantially reduced thermal damage across tissues during the procedure. Since thermal damage directly impairs wound healing processes, the reduced collateral thermal injury from harmonic scalpel use may translate to improved postoperative recovery and reduced complications. These findings suggest that harmonic scalpel represents a superior energy modality for laparoscopic hysterectomy in endometriosis patients, where minimizing thermal injury is important for optimal tissue healing. The instrument choice during minimally invasive gynecological surgery thus has meaningful implications for surgical outcomes and patient recovery profiles.

title: Excisional surgery versus ablative surgery for ovarian endometrioma
journal: The Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews
summary: This Cochrane systematic review compares excisional versus ablative surgical approaches for treating ovarian endometriomas, which are deposits of endometrial tissue causing pelvic pain and infertility. Laparoscopic excision (complete removal) significantly outperforms ablative surgery (drainage and thermal destruction) in reducing recurrence of dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and endometriomas within one to two years post-operatively, with ablative surgery showing substantially higher recurrence rates across all outcomes. After ablative surgery, approximately 49% of women experience dysmenorrhea recurrence and 58% experience dyspareunia recurrence, compared to only 10-34% and 4-23% respectively following excisional surgery; similarly, endometrioma recurrence occurs in 37% after ablation versus 5-17% after excision, with 32% requiring further surgery after ablation compared to 3-16% after excision. Both laparoscopic approaches offer considerable advantages over open surgery, including shorter hospital stays and faster recovery times. However, no meaningful difference was demonstrated between the two surgical techniques regarding spontaneous pregnancy rates, suggesting that pain relief rather than fertility improvement may be the primary benefit distinguishing excisional from ablative approaches.

title: SAHELI:  Study and Action on Hysterectomy: Evidence on women’s health through the life course in India.  Protocol for a mixed-methods study
summary: The SAHELI study protocol examines hysterectomy outcomes in Indian women across the lifespan, with particular attention to long-term health consequences and sociodemographic disparities. Hysterectomy, commonly performed for benign conditions including endometriosis, fibroids, and abnormal uterine bleeding in younger women, is associated with substantial cardiovascular and metabolic risks including increased incidence of cardiovascular disease, hypertension, high cholesterol, diabetes, and osteoporosis. When bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy accompanies hysterectomy, induced surgical menopause further elevates risks for cardiovascular disease, all-cause mortality, and additional comorbidities including depression and dementia. The study reveals important health equity concerns, as women with lower education, lower socioeconomic status, and rural residence demonstrate higher hysterectomy rates, while those with at least ten years of schooling are significantly less likely to undergo the procedure. These findings underscore the critical need to understand how early surgical menopause and hysterectomy influence women's health trajectories throughout their lives, particularly in resource-limited settings where access to alternative treatments may be limited.

title: Clinical efficacy and safety study of vNOTES for benign ovarian tumors in obese patients
summary: Vaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery (vNOTES) demonstrates superior clinical efficacy compared to multiport laparoscopic surgery (MPLS) for treating benign ovarian tumors in obese patients. By eliminating abdominal wall incisions, vNOTES significantly reduces operative time, anesthesia duration, postoperative pain, hospital stay, and the incidence of wound complications including infections and hernias—complications particularly problematic in obese patients whose increased abdominal wall thickness complicates traditional laparoscopic approaches. vNOTES patients experienced fewer postoperative complications overall, lower pain scores at 48 hours, and higher quality-of-life measures at three months post-operation, facilitating early mobilization and rapid recovery. The procedure successfully treated diverse benign ovarian pathologies including serous cystadenomas, endometriotic cysts, and teratomas without adjacent organ injury. With a short learning curve for teams experienced in minimally invasive surgery, vNOTES represents a feasible and reproducible approach that mitigates anesthesia risks while improving aesthetic outcomes in this high-risk surgical population.

title: A comprehensive review of urinary tract fistulas: the evolution of etiologies, surgical techniques, and contemporary outcomes
summary:
# Summary

Urinary tract fistulas (UTFs) represent a significant clinical problem with diverse etiologies varying by geographic region and clinical context, causing substantial morbidity through urinary incontinence, recurrent infections, and severely diminished quality of life. In developed nations, gynecologic surgery—particularly hysterectomy—is the predominant cause of vesicovaginal fistulas, while in developing countries, obstetric trauma from prolonged obstructed labor remains the leading etiology. Additional UTF types arise from specific injuries: endometriosis and pelvic inflammatory disease are associated with ureterovaginal fistulas, pelvic radiation with concurrent chemotherapy significantly increases overall fistula risk through endarteritis obliterans, and transvaginal surgeries can cause urethrovaginal fistulas. Modern surgical management has evolved substantially, with robot-assisted laparoscopic surgery now demonstrating superior outcomes including reduced blood loss, minimal postoperative complications, and low recurrence rates, leveraging three-dimensional visualization and precise instrument control compared to traditional open approaches. Adjunctive therapies such as hyperbaric oxygen therapy promote tissue healing through stem cell mobilization and growth factor upregulation, while conservative management with ureteral stent placement achieves success in approximately 65% of ureterovaginal fistula cases, providing treatment options across the clinical spectrum.

title: The Role of Douglasectomy Instead of Random Biopsies in the Surgical Treatment of Presumed FIGO Stage I Ovarian Cancer
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluates en bloc douglasectomy—removal of the pouch of Douglas peritoneum together with the uterus—as a technique to reduce peritoneal recurrence in presumed FIGO stage I ovarian cancer. Epithelial ovarian cancer cells exfoliate from primary tumors and can disseminate via peritoneal fluid circulation, with microscopic disease frequently implanting in the pouch of Douglas despite apparent early-stage disease at diagnosis. Patients undergoing douglasectomy (Group A) demonstrated significantly improved disease-free survival and absent pelvic recurrence compared to controls receiving random biopsies, with no recurrence in the pouch of Douglas specifically in the douglasectomy group versus four cases in the control group. However, the procedure was associated with higher operative time, increased postoperative complications including paralytic ileus and infection, and elevated preoperative CA-125 values reflecting more advanced disease burden in Group A. Despite these morbidities, overall survival did not differ significantly between groups, suggesting that aggressive pelvic peritoneal debulking may offer local disease control benefits without compromising long-term outcomes in early-stage ovarian cancer.

title: Comparison of Surgical Outcomes Between Single-Port Access Laparoscopic and Single-Site Robotic Surgery in Benign Gynecologic Diseases: A Single-Center Cohort Study
summary: This single-center cohort study compared single-site robotic surgery (SSRS) and single-port access laparoscopic surgery (SPALS) for treating benign gynecological diseases, with both techniques successfully avoiding conversion to open surgery. While SSRS offered technical advantages including three-dimensional visualization and wristed instruments that enhanced suturing precision and reduced technical difficulty, it was associated with significantly longer operation times (118 versus 57 minutes), delayed gas-passing recovery (39 versus 30 hours), and extended hospital stays compared to SPALS. SSRS was preferentially used in younger patients with lower parity and complex cases requiring fertility-preserving procedures such as myomectomy and ovarian cystectomy, and was notably associated with a higher incidence of severe intra-abdominal adhesions. However, the substantial cost differential—approximately six-fold higher for SSRS ($7,221 versus $1,170)—and lack of clear postoperative outcome superiority suggest that SPALS remains a cost-effective alternative for many benign gynecological conditions. The study indicates that patient selection and clinical complexity should guide surgical approach selection, with SSRS reserved for cases where technical precision is paramount.

title: Comparison between learning curves of robot-assisted and laparoscopic surgery in gynaecology: a systematic review
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review compares the learning curves of robot-assisted laparoscopic surgery (RLS) and traditional laparoscopic surgery (LPS) in gynecological procedures. Robotic-assisted approaches offer significant technical advantages including improved ergonomics, augmented visual acuity, nullified hand tremors, and smaller incisions, which collectively reduce morbidity, postoperative pain, and hospital stays for both benign and malignant gynecological conditions. Across complex procedures such as radical hysterectomy, bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, lymph node dissection, and sacrocolpopexy, surgeons using RLS demonstrate steeper and faster learning curves compared to those using LPS, suggesting greater procedural efficiency with increased experience. Three-dimensional visualization in laparoscopic surgery further enhances learning by improving speed and reducing performance errors. Prior laparoscopic experience significantly benefits surgical outcomes and facilitates the transfer of skills to robotic platforms, indicating that surgeons with foundational laparoscopic training achieve superior perioperative results in robotic-assisted procedures.

title: Risk factors for blood transfusion in patients undergoing hysterectomy for stage I endometrial cancer
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies critical preoperative and intraoperative risk factors for blood transfusion in endometrial cancer patients undergoing hysterectomy. Low preoperative hematocrit emerged as the strongest predictor (aOR 22.4), followed by abdominal surgical approach versus laparoscopy (aOR 6.36) and operative time exceeding 180 minutes (aOR 3.38). Additional risk factors included enlarged uteri, bleeding disorders, cardiac comorbidities, and dependent functional status. The analysis reveals that blood transfusion—while sometimes necessary—is independently associated with worse perioperative outcomes, including increased infections, sepsis, pneumonia, and significantly reduced five-year overall and progression-free survival. These adverse associations may result from transfusion-induced immunomodulation, inflammation, and oxidative stress that promote cancer stem cell enrichment and chemoresistance. The findings support preventive strategies including preoperative anemia optimization through iron supplementation, use of minimally invasive laparoscopic approaches, and implementation of restrictive transfusion protocols to improve endometrial cancer patient outcomes.

title: Salpingectomy versus tubal occlusion in laparoscopic sterilisation (SALSTER): a national register-based randomised non-inferiority trial
summary: High-grade serous ovarian carcinoma originates predominantly in the fimbriae of the Fallopian tubes, which has motivated the exploration of salpingectomy as a preventive intervention for epithelial ovarian cancer. The SALSTER trial compared laparoscopic salpingectomy with tubal occlusion for sterilization and found that salpingectomy was non-inferior to tubal occlusion regarding complication rates in the eight weeks following surgery, with an overall complication rate of 8.1%. However, salpingectomy procedures are associated with specific safety concerns, including increased perioperative bleeding and risk of complications, as well as potential effects such as earlier menopause onset. These findings suggest that while salpingectomy offers the theoretical benefit of reducing epithelial ovarian cancer incidence, the procedural risks must be carefully weighed against these protective effects when considering opportunistic salpingectomy during other gynecological operations. The trial demonstrates that both approaches are viable sterilization methods with comparable short-term safety profiles, though further investigation into long-term consequences of salpingectomy remains warranted.

title: A Personalized Approach to Adhesion Prevention in Single-Port Access Laparoscopic Surgery: A Randomized Prospective Study Evaluating the Efficacy of Adhesion Barriers and Patient-Specific Risk Factors
summary:
# Summary

Single-port access laparoscopic surgery has become increasingly popular in gynecology for procedures including hysterectomy, ovarian cystectomy, and myomectomy due to its cosmetic benefits, reduced postoperative pain, and improved recovery; however, the larger umbilical incision required (approximately 20-25 mm compared to 5-12 mm in conventional laparoscopy) significantly increases the risk of periumbilical adhesion formation. This prospective randomized trial evaluated whether Guardix SG, a temperature-sensitive anti-adhesive barrier, could reduce umbilical adhesions when applied at the incision site, and found that while the barrier showed a trend toward reducing adhesion rates (8% versus 13.3% in controls), the difference was not statistically significant. Pre-existing adhesions emerged as the most critical risk factor for postoperative adhesion development (odds ratio 6.909), whereas patient factors such as BMI, surgical method, and endometriosis diagnosis were not significantly associated with adhesion formation. The study suggests that a personalized risk stratification approach targeting patients with pre-existing adhesions for preventive measures may optimize the use of adhesion barriers, though anatomical factors such as the umbilicus's location on the linea alba and reduced gravitational pooling at the umbilical site may naturally limit adhesion formation in this region.

title: Minimally Invasive Rectal Surgery: Current Status and Future Perspectives in the Era of Digital Surgery
summary:
# Summary

This article examines minimally invasive surgical approaches for rectal cancer and inflammatory bowel disease, with emphasis on genetic pathways underlying colorectal cancer development and the advantages of laparoscopic techniques. Colorectal carcinogenesis progresses through well-characterized genetic pathways, including APC gene inactivation leading to unchecked cellular replication and adenoma formation, followed by additional mutations in K-RAS and p53 that drive progression to adenocarcinoma; alternatively, defects in DNA mismatch repair genes (hMLH1, hMSH2, hPMS1, hPMS2, hMSH6) characterize the hereditary nonpolyposis colorectal cancer pathway. Inflammatory bowel disease, particularly ulcerative colitis, substantially increases colorectal cancer risk through chronic inflammation. For early-stage rectal cancer, minimally invasive techniques including transanal endoscopic microsurgery (TEM) and transanal minimally invasive surgery (TAMIS) provide curative treatment while reducing surgical trauma and anastomotic complications. Laparoscopic and robotic-assisted approaches demonstrate significant benefits over open surgery, including reduced postoperative pain, shorter hospital stays, faster bowel function recovery, reduced blood loss, improved cosmetic outcomes, and fewer wound infections, while maintaining equivalent oncological outcomes. Total mesorectal excision remains the standard for rectal cancer, and prompt surgery following neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy improves overall and disease-free survival outcomes.

title: Efficacy of King’s Combined Uterine Suture in Managing Placenta Accreta: A Retrospective Analysis
journal: Medical Science Monitor: International Medical Journal of Experimental and Clinical Research
summary:
# Summary

Placenta accreta spectrum (PAS) represents a serious obstetric condition characterized by abnormal placental invasion into the myometrium, leading to significantly increased vascularization and a high risk of massive hemorrhage, hemorrhagic shock, and disseminated intravascular coagulation during delivery. This study evaluated King's combined uterine suture ligation surgery (KCUSLS), a conservative surgical technique that combines parauterine vascular ligation with longitudinal suturing of the lower uterine segment to achieve hemostasis through multiple mechanisms including ligation of bilateral uterine arteries and their collateral branches, vascular bed compression, and mechanical apposition of uterine walls. In a retrospective analysis of 49 pregnant women with PAS who underwent KCUSLS, the procedure successfully controlled intraoperative bleeding (average 507±162 mL) without requiring hysterectomy or causing ureteral injury, while avoiding the serious complications associated with alternative approaches such as aortic balloon occlusion. Notably, KCUSLS resulted in excellent neonatal outcomes with high Apgar scores (9.67±0.77) and no cases of asphyxia or neonatal death, demonstrating that this technique offers a safe and effective uterus-preserving alternative to hysterectomy for managing PAS while maintaining favorable maternal and fetal outcomes.

title: Exploring thermal balloon endometrial ablation with Foley’s catheter: Management for heavy menstrual bleeding
summary:
# Summary

Thermal balloon endometrial ablation (TBEA) using a Foley catheter is an effective minimally invasive treatment for heavy menstrual bleeding that works by thinning and reducing the endometrial lining. The procedure significantly improved clinical outcomes in patients, reducing bleeding days from 10.46 to 5.58 days, decreasing endometrial thickness from 9.24 to 4 mm, and lowering pain scores and menorrhagia severity scores within three months post-procedure. TBEA demonstrated an 88.6% success rate and improved quality of life metrics, with particular efficacy in women over 40 with regular menstrual cycles, while also reducing the incidence of irregular cycles from 62.3% to 29.8%. However, complications including infections (15.3% of patients) and balloon deflation (7.6%) occurred, emphasizing the importance of prophylactic antibiotics and proper procedural technique. This second-generation endometrial ablation approach offers a viable uterus-preserving alternative to hysterectomy for managing menorrhagia and dysfunctional uterine bleeding.

title: Rectal Injury Due to the Incorrect Insertion of a Vagi-Pipe Into the Rectum During Total Laparoscopic Hysterectomy: A Case Report
summary: This case report documents a rare but serious iatrogenic complication during total laparoscopic hysterectomy for endometriosis: rectal injury caused by incorrect insertion of a Vagi-Pipe (vaginal retraction device) into the rectum instead of the vagina. The incident resulted from multiple converging factors including the patient's nulliparity and resulting narrow vaginal orifice, muscle relaxation of the anal sphincter under general anesthesia, and resistance encountered during insertion that allowed the Vagi-Pipe tip to slip into the anus. Once positioned in the rectum, subsequent electrocautery dissection of the vaginal wall caused direct thermal and mechanical damage to the rectal mucosa, with the extent of thermal injury remaining difficult to assess intraoperatively. While laparoscopic hysterectomy carries known risks of pelvic organ injury (rectal injury incidence 1.5% in laparoscopic versus 0.3% in open surgery), this case highlights an unusual mechanism distinct from the typical rectal injuries associated with deep endometriosis-related invasion and adhesions. The patient required laparoscopic low anterior resection of the rectum without ileostomy to manage the injury, demonstrating the need for careful anatomical orientation and consideration of patient-specific anatomical variations during laparoscopic pelvic surgery.

title: Robotics-assisted surgery in gynecology: A single-center experience with the Hugo™ RAS system in India
summary: This single-center study evaluated the Hugo™ RAS robotic-assisted surgical system for treating diverse gynecological conditions, including endometriosis-related hysterectomies, demonstrating significant clinical advantages over conventional laparoscopic approaches. Twenty patients underwent procedures with the Hugo™ RAS system, which offers enhanced surgical precision through haptic feedback, 3D high-definition visualization, tremor filtration, and wristed instruments that provide six degrees of freedom—capabilities lacking in standard laparoscopic surgery. Patients experienced minimal blood loss (103.5 mL), no immediate postoperative complications, short hospital stays (2 days), and markedly reduced postoperative pain that decreased from 3.2 at 1 hour to 1.0 at 12 hours post-surgery. The system's four independent robotic arm carts with six hinges enable improved surgical positioning and maneuverability while reducing dependence on surgical assistants, with an efficient docking procedure and learning curve comparable to established platforms. These findings support robotic-assisted surgery as a viable, superior alternative to traditional laparoscopy for benign gynecological conditions including endometriosis, with particular advantages for complex cases such as obese patients where conventional approaches show higher morbidity and conversion rates.

title: Transvaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery for early-stage ovarian cancer and borderline ovarian tumors: a case series
summary: This case series evaluates transvaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery (vNOTES) as a minimally invasive approach for surgical staging of early-stage ovarian cancer and borderline ovarian tumors. Between October 2021 and August 2024, eleven patients underwent complete vNOTES staging procedures that included peritoneal washing, salpingo-oophorectomy, peritoneal biopsies, infracolic omentectomy, and total hysterectomy, with surgeons performing either bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy or fertility-sparing approaches based on clinical presentation. The technique successfully extracted tumor masses up to 6–7 cm without requiring morcelation, reduced postoperative morbidity through a single vaginal incision that minimized pain and hospitalization duration, and eliminated risks of port site metastases compared to conventional laparoscopy. Post-operative complications occurred in 27.3% of patients and were minor, including surgical site infection and cystitis, while adjuvant chemotherapy was administered in 36.4% of cases based on tumor staging. The findings demonstrate that vNOTES provides a feasible and safe alternative to conventional laparoscopic and robotic approaches for gynecological oncology, with particular advantages for patients with complex surgical histories or suspected intra-abdominal adhesions who require pelvic and paraaortic lymphadenectomy.

title: Long-term outcomes of bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy in women with personal history of breast cancer
summary:
# Summary

This study examined long-term health outcomes in breast cancer survivors undergoing bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (BSO), comparing surgical ovarian suppression with medical alternatives. BSO effectively reduced ovarian cancer risk by over 90% and, when combined with hysterectomy, was associated with reduced breast cancer and all-cause mortality in women aged 55 years and older. However, the procedure carried significant cardiovascular consequences, including increased risk of total cardiovascular disease, ischaemic heart disease, and angina across age groups. BSO was also associated with increased depression risk at any age and elevated second non-breast cancer risk in older women, though no increased contralateral breast cancer risk was observed. In contrast, medical ovarian function suppression using gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists combined with tamoxifen or aromatase inhibitors demonstrated superior outcomes, with significant reductions in all-cause mortality and disease-free survival without the cardiovascular penalties of surgical oophorectomy. These findings suggest that while BSO remains beneficial for ovarian cancer prevention in high-risk populations such as BRCA carriers, medical ovarian suppression may offer a safer alternative for hormone-positive breast cancer management in premenopausal women.

title: Effect of Patient Age on Decisional Regret After Laparoscopic Hysterectomy
summary: This study examined decisional regret following laparoscopic hysterectomy for benign gynecological conditions, with particular focus on patient age as a determinant of postoperative satisfaction. Patients aged 30 years or younger experienced substantially higher rates of both surgical regret (32.5% vs. 9.1%) and regret regarding loss of fertility (39.0% vs. 13.4%) compared to older patients, with age remaining a significant independent predictor even after multivariate adjustment. Beyond age, preoperative endometriosis diagnosis, persistent pelvic pain, and postoperative complications were all independently associated with increased regret in both categories. Notably, younger patients underwent hysterectomy more frequently for pelvic pain and were more likely to experience additional pelvic pain postoperatively as well as require further gynecologic surgery, suggesting that symptom relief may be inadequate in this population. Time elapsed since hysterectomy was associated with decreased fertility-related regret, indicating that psychological adjustment may improve over time. These findings suggest that careful patient counseling regarding symptom outcomes and permanent loss of fertility is particularly critical for younger surgical candidates, and alternative or adjunctive medical management should be strongly considered before proceeding with hysterectomy in this age group.

title: Conservative endometrioma surgery: The combined technique versus CO2-laser vaporization only (BLAST: Belgium LAser STudy): Clinical protocol for a multicenter randomized controlled trial
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease characterized by the presence of endometrium-like epithelium and stroma outside the uterus, affecting 2-10% of women of reproductive age and associated with significant pain and infertility, with endometriomas present in 17-44% of patients. This multicenter randomized controlled trial (BLAST study) compares two conservative laparoscopic surgical approaches for treating endometriomas: CO2-laser vaporization alone versus a combined technique (cystectomy stripping followed by CO2-laser ablation of the cyst surface). A key concern with endometrioma surgery is iatrogenic damage to ovarian reserve, as measured by serum anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) levels and antral follicle count (AFC), with traditional cystectomy showing greater reductions in AMH and ovarian volume compared to ablative techniques. Previous evidence suggests that CO2-laser vaporization and the combined technique may better preserve ovarian reserve than cystectomy alone, though CO2-laser vaporization is associated with higher recurrence rates. The combined technique appears to offer a compromise approach with limited deleterious effects on ovarian reserve while potentially reducing recurrence risk, making it an important therapeutic strategy to evaluate in this prospective study.

title: The impact of lymph node dissection on stage I ovarian endometrioid carcinoma: A US-China Comparative Analysis
summary: Ovarian endometrioid carcinoma (OEC) represents 10–15% of epithelial ovarian cancers and exhibits a favorable prognosis with 5-year survival rates exceeding 80%, particularly when diagnosed at stage I with distinctive clinical presentations including abdominal mass, pain, and distension. Lymph node dissection (LND) significantly improves outcomes in stage I OEC patients, with those undergoing the procedure achieving a 93.4% 5-year survival rate compared to 84.2% without dissection, and serving as an independent protective factor that reduces hazard ratios for overall mortality. The optimal survival benefit occurs when 29–34 lymph nodes are dissected, with consistent protective effects observed between 21–34 nodes in US data (SEER) and 29–36 nodes in Chinese data (NUWA), though benefits plateau after approximately 20 nodes with potential hazard ratio increases beyond 40 nodes. While LND improves progression-free and disease-free survival, it carries notable complications including lower limb lymphedema, lymphatic cysts, and pelvic infections that reduce quality of life, and extensive dissection may adversely affect immunotherapy efficacy. The study demonstrates that despite higher complication rates with extensive LND, the survival benefits in early-stage OEC remain substantial and consistent across patient subgroups, supporting its continued use in optimal staging and treatment planning while carefully balancing surgical morbidity.

title: Comparative analysis of laparoendoscopic single-site surgery and versus conventional laparoscopic surgery in adnexectomy: A systematic review and meta-analysis of surgical outcome
journal: Turkish Journal of Obstetrics and Gynecology
summary: This meta-analysis compared laparoendoscopic single-site (LESS) surgery with conventional laparoscopic surgery (CLS) for adnexectomy, examining both procedural outcomes and safety profiles. LESS demonstrated clinically meaningful advantages in reducing estimated blood loss and postoperative analgesic requirements compared to CLS, while also minimizing abdominal scarring and trocar-related complications—benefits that align with its single-incision approach. However, LESS was associated with longer operative times and significantly higher perioperative complication rates, with technical limitations including reduced triangulation, instrument interference, and visualization challenges contributing to these drawbacks. Pain scores at 6 and 24 hours postoperatively and hospital stay duration were comparable between the two approaches, suggesting similar acute recovery profiles despite the procedural differences. The findings indicate that while LESS offers cosmetic and tissue-preservation advantages for adnexectomy and related procedures (including cystectomy, endometrioma excision, and hysterectomy), the increased operative time and perioperative complications must be weighed against these benefits, particularly in complex cases involving dense adhesions, endometriosis, obesity, or prior pelvic surgery.

title: The environmental impact of multi-specialty robotic-assisted surgery: a waste audit analysis
summary:
# Summary

Robotic-assisted surgery for gynecological procedures, including endometriosis excision and hysterectomy, demonstrates improved clinical outcomes but generates substantially greater environmental impact compared to conventional approaches. Robotic hysterectomies produce 43.5% higher greenhouse gas emissions and 24.0% greater waste by weight than traditional laparoscopic surgery, with single-use items comprising 93.9% of total waste—particularly the da Vinci Xi instrument arm drape, which is the largest contributor across all robotic procedures. Endometriosis excision and hysterectomy cases each generate approximately 4.4-4.5 kilograms of waste, contributing to the healthcare industry's status as the second largest global waste generator, with serious implications for climate and patient health through increased greenhouse gas emissions and resultant global warming effects. Implementation of waste segregation strategies and remanufacturing of single-use medical instruments represent viable approaches to reduce both environmental impact and costs associated with robotic-assisted gynecological procedures.

title: Application of Surgical Adhesive Reduces Ureteroureterostomy Leakage
summary:
# Summary

Ureteral injuries are common iatrogenic complications of gynecologic, urologic, and colorectal surgery that can lead to serious sequelae including infection, sepsis, and kidney damage. Ureteroureterostomy is the standard surgical repair for proximal and middle ureteral injuries, but anastomotic leakage remains a significant postoperative risk that can result in urinoma, peritonitis, pyelonephritis, and potentially lethal sepsis. This study demonstrates that application of Dermabond surgical adhesive to the sutured ureteroureterostomy effectively prevents anastomotic leakage across a range of physiologic and supraphysiologic perfusion pressures, even where sutures alone proved inadequate. Surgical adhesive reinforcement preserves anastomotic integrity while permitting more efficient suturing techniques that minimize excessive tissue trauma and preserve periureteral blood supply critical for healing. The combination of suture and adhesive represents an evidence-based adjunctive approach to reduce postoperative complications and potentially shorten hospital stays following ureteral reconstruction.

title: Laparoendoscopic single‐site surgery versus conventional laparoscopic surgery for adnexal tumors: A comparison of surgical outcomes and postoperative pain outcomes
journal: The Kaohsiung Journal of Medical Sciences
summary: This study compared laparoendoscopic single-site surgery (LESS) with conventional laparoscopic surgery (CLS) for treating adnexal tumors, demonstrating that LESS is a feasible surgical approach with comparable clinical outcomes. Both surgical techniques successfully managed adnexal masses, with cystectomy being the most frequently performed procedure across both groups. Pathological examination revealed similar tumor characteristics between groups, with mucinous cystadenoma being the most common finding. Notably, all LESS procedures were completed without conversion to conventional laparoscopic surgery, indicating technical success and safety of the single-site approach. Postoperative pain intensity, measured by visual analog scale, showed no significant differences between LESS and CLS groups, suggesting that the minimally invasive single-site approach does not compromise pain management outcomes. These findings support LESS as a viable alternative to conventional laparoscopic surgery for adnexal tumor management, offering potential advantages in surgical approach while maintaining equivalent clinical and postoperative pain outcomes.

title: Subcutaneous Emphysema and Pneumomediastinum Secondary to Dental Extraction: A Case Report and Literature Review
journal: The Kaohsiung Journal of Medical Sciences
summary: This case report documents a rare but serious complication of dental extraction: the development of subcutaneous emphysema and pneumomediastinum following molar extraction. The use of high-speed dental drills can introduce air into soft tissue planes, creating pathways for air dissection into subcutaneous spaces and the mediastinum. The patient presented with acute dyspnea accompanied by right cheek and neck swelling during the extraction procedure, representing the clinical manifestation of air tracking through tissue compartments. While subcutaneous emphysema and pneumomediastinum are uncommon complications of dental procedures, this case highlights the potential for air to rapidly dissect through anatomical planes when high-speed instrumentation is used during extraction. Recognition of these clinical signs during dental work is important for prompt diagnosis and management to prevent progression to more severe respiratory compromise.

title: Gastrointestinal function outcomes following radical and conservative colorectal surgery for deep endometriosis: A systematic review and meta‐analysis
summary:
# Summary

Deep colorectal endometriosis causes significant gastrointestinal dysfunction, with up to 70% of patients experiencing defecation pain and over 50% suffering from cyclic constipation, creating symptoms similar to lower anterior resection syndrome even without surgery. When surgical intervention is necessary, the choice of approach substantially impacts postoperative outcomes: segmental resection, while sometimes required for extensive disease, associates with higher rates of postoperative constipation, increased daily stool frequency, and reduced gastrointestinal quality of life compared to conservative surgical techniques like rectal shaving. The mechanisms underlying surgical morbidity include damage to intestinal autonomous innervation, colorectal stenosis, and altered colonic water absorption resulting from rectal shortening. Conservative surgery preserves better functional outcomes and quality of life without significantly increasing rates of gas or stool incontinence or defecation pain compared to segmental approaches. Despite successful disease removal, patients frequently experience persistent digestive complaints postoperatively, and baseline severe constipation before surgery emerges as a relevant prognostic factor for postoperative functional status. These findings emphasize the importance of selecting the least invasive surgical technique appropriate for individual disease extent while considering surgeon experience as an influential factor in complication rates.

title: Postoperative outcomes in minimally invasive total versus supracervical hysterectomy for endometriosis: a NSQIP study
summary: Endometriosis causes multiple debilitating symptoms including dysmenorrhea, chronic pain, dyspareunia, and infertility, and surgical resection, particularly hysterectomy, provides definitive treatment for women with symptoms refractory to medical therapy. This comparative study of minimally invasive hysterectomy approaches in endometriosis patients found that laparoscopic supracervical hysterectomy (LSCH) was associated with superior postoperative outcomes compared to total laparoscopic hysterectomy (TLH), including shorter surgical times, reduced blood loss, and significantly lower complication rates. Total laparoscopic hysterectomy was independently associated with higher rates of both major (3.7% vs. 1.5%) and minor complications (5.4% vs. 2.8%), including increased urinary tract infections and postoperative fever, while LSCH demonstrated independently reduced odds of complications. However, LSCH carries distinct trade-offs, including persistent cyclic vaginal bleeding in 5-10% of patients due to retained endometrial tissue in the cervical stump, which may theoretically increase endometriosis recurrence risk and necessitate subsequent trachelectomy in some cases. These findings suggest that surgical technique selection should balance the lower perioperative morbidity of supracervical hysterectomy against the completeness of disease eradication achieved with total hysterectomy in endometriosis patients.

title: Impact of Prior Abdominal Surgery on Total Laparoscopic Hysterectomy vs Total Abdominal Hysterectomy: A Retrospective Cohort Study
summary:
# Summary

Total laparoscopic hysterectomy (TLH) is effective for treating endometriosis, abnormal uterine bleeding, and fibroids while significantly reducing intraoperative blood loss, postoperative pain, and hospital stay compared to total abdominal hysterectomy (TAH). Prior abdominal surgeries, particularly cesarean sections, appendectomy, and cholecystectomy, generate dense adhesions that increase surgical complexity and risk of bladder and bowel injury; however, TLH remains a viable and preferable option for most patients with prior surgical history due to its minimally invasive nature. Despite adhesions and altered anatomy obscuring surgical landmarks, TLH was completed safely without significantly increasing operative time or overall complication rates, though severe adhesions occasionally necessitated conversion to open surgery. Early ambulation and reduced perioperative morbidity with TLH contribute to faster patient recovery and lower healthcare costs compared to TAH's longer recovery times and higher risk of wound infection. The study emphasizes that surgical expertise, meticulous dissection techniques, precise intraoperative visualization, and preoperative imaging with MRI or 3D ultrasound are essential for optimizing outcomes and preventing complications in this technically demanding procedure.

title: Adoption of vaginally assisted natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery for hysterectomy: A single tertiary experience
journal: The Australian & New Zealand Journal of Obstetrics & Gynaecology
summary:
# Summary

This case series evaluates vaginally assisted natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery (VANH) for hysterectomy in a tertiary center, comparing its outcomes to traditional vaginal and laparoscopic approaches. VANH demonstrated significant advantages including shorter operative time, reduced length of stay (median 1.4 days), and lower analgesia requirements compared to laparoscopic hysterectomy. The majority of procedures (60%) were performed for complex hyperplasia with atypia, with concurrent bilateral adnexectomy in 65% of cases, and all specimens were removed en bloc with successful cystoscopic confirmation. However, endometriosis emerged as a relative contraindication due to increased risks of adhesions, obliterated pouch of Douglas, and conversion to laparoscopy, with four of five conversions occurring in morbidly obese patients. The authors emphasize that VANH requires a substantial learning curve with dedicated training and mentoring, careful case selection (small uteri, low BMI, parous patients), and preoperative vaginal and rectal examination to identify unexpected pelvic pathology, though the technique offers potential advantages for en bloc removal of larger uteri and improved training exposure for gynecologic trainees.

title: Percutaneous transluminal angioplasty with stenting for the treatment of lower limb arteriosclerosis obliterans
summary:
# Summary

Lower limb arteriosclerosis obliterans (ASO), caused by atherosclerotic plaque buildup, reduces blood flow to the lower limbs resulting in pain and functional impairment. Percutaneous transluminal angioplasty with stenting (PTAS) treats this condition by enhancing blood circulation and vascular patency more effectively than angioplasty alone. PTAS significantly improves clinical outcomes including ankle-brachial index, claudication distance, and quality of life scores, while stents prevent restenosis and reduce vessel elastic recoil and neointimal hyperplasia—complications that commonly follow traditional angioplasty. Notably, PTAS substantially reduces the need for target lesion revascularization, demonstrating superior long-term efficacy compared to PTA without stenting.

title: Safety and effectiveness of office hysteroscopic metroplasty with and without uterine stent placement in infertile population: a 10-year retrospective study
summary:
# Summary

Septate uterus, a congenital uterine malformation, significantly impairs fertility and pregnancy outcomes by reducing expression of key implantation factors like Hox-A10 and vascular endothelial growth factor receptor, coupled with altered endometrial tissue composition and poor septal vascularization. Office-based hysteroscopic septum resection with ultrasound guidance effectively treats infertility and recurrent pregnancy loss associated with septate uterus, improving live birth rates approximately sevenfold while reducing miscarriage rates by 64% and malpresentation rates. Ultrasound guidance during resection substantially improves septal resection accuracy and prevents serious intraoperative complications including uterine perforation, excessive bleeding, and infections. While intrauterine balloon stent placement after septoplasty can help prevent postoperative adhesions, live birth rates improve comparably with or without stent placement, suggesting stents may not be universally necessary. This office-based approach offers additional advantages including faster booking times, avoidance of general anesthesia, and reduced operative costs compared to laparoscopic procedures, establishing hysteroscopic metroplasty with ultrasound guidance as a safe and effective first-line treatment for improving fertility outcomes in infertile women with septate uterus.

title: Minimally Invasive Myomectomy with Temporary Bilateral Uterine Artery Blockage at Anterior Cul-de-Sac
journal: JSLS : Journal of the Society of Laparoscopic & Robotic Surgeons
summary: Minimally invasive myomectomy combined with temporary bilateral uterine artery blockade represents an effective surgical strategy for managing complicated uterine fibroids while minimizing hemorrhagic complications. Bilateral uterine artery blockade substantially reduces intraoperative bleeding, resulting in significantly lower transfusion requirements compared to standard approaches, with transfusion rates of only 3% versus 17% in conventional surgery. This technique also decreases both intraoperative and postoperative complication rates, with intraoperative complications occurring in 0% of minimally invasive cases versus 3% in open procedures, and postoperative complications reduced from 9% to 3%. Patients undergoing minimally invasive myomectomy with arterial blockade additionally benefit from substantially shortened hospital stays, with 70% of patients discharged within one day compared to only 29% of open surgery patients. Temporary bilateral uterine artery blockade enables the safe performance of complex myomectomy procedures through minimally invasive approaches, making it a valuable technique for fibroid management that improves both operative safety and patient recovery outcomes.

title: Safe Implementation of Robotic Surgery for Gynecologic Diseases at a Tertiary Center: Retrospective Analysis of 149 Cases and Review of the Literature
summary: This retrospective analysis of 149 robotic surgical cases demonstrates the safe and effective implementation of robotic-assisted hysterectomy for multiple gynecologic conditions, including endometriosis, adenomyosis, leiomyoma, and endometrial cancer. Robotic platforms offer distinct technical advantages including three-dimensional visualization and wristed instruments, enabling surgeons to achieve a shorter learning curve compared to traditional approaches. A structured simulation program significantly accelerated surgical training by reducing setup times and stabilizing operative times within the first 21 procedures, with experienced surgeons (Groups B and C) demonstrating substantially lower setup times than the initial cohort. The study reported excellent safety outcomes with no intraoperative complications, blood transfusions, conversions to open surgery, or organ injuries, while also achieving lymph node retrieval comparable to laparoscopic techniques during pelvic lymphadenectomy. For endometriosis cases specifically, robotic hysterectomy provided effective treatment with favorable morbidity profiles, though cases with adhesions attributable to endometriosis were more prevalent in later surgical cases. These findings support the clinical adoption of robotic surgery for benign gynecologic diseases when accompanied by appropriate surgical training and institutional support protocols.

title: On-demand robotic surgery for hysterectomies: A combination of the best of robotic and laparoscopic approach
summary: Robot-assisted hysterectomy using the Dexter robotic system effectively treats multiple gynecological conditions including endometriosis, endometrial cancer, and uterine fibroids. The Dexter system uniquely combines robotic precision with laparoscopic flexibility, allowing surgeons to seamlessly switch between robotic and standard laparoscopic interfaces during surgery while maintaining sterility and complete access to the operative field. In a cohort of 24 patients, the system was used for hysterectomies often paired with complex ancillary procedures such as lymphadenectomy and deep infiltrating endometriosis surgery, with operative times and blood loss varying by diagnosis—endometriosis cases requiring longer operative times (186.2 minutes) and greater blood loss (190 milliliters) compared to uterine fibroids. While minimally invasive hysterectomy generally reduces intraoperative bleeding, postoperative pain, and complications compared to open approaches, this series documented vaginal dome infections in 12.5% of patients and trocar hernias as postoperative grade III complications. The on-demand design of the Dexter system provides a practical solution for complex gynecological surgery by combining the ergonomic advantages of robotic surgery with the accessibility and instrument variety of conventional laparoscopy.

title: Outcomes of Total Laparoscopic Hysterectomy: A Single-Surgeon Experience at a Tertiary Care Hospital in North India
summary:
# Summary

This single-surgeon retrospective study of 150 total laparoscopic hysterectomy (TLH) procedures demonstrates that TLH is a safe and effective minimally invasive surgical approach for benign gynecological conditions, achieving significant reductions in blood loss, hospital stay, operative time, and postoperative morbidity compared to traditional open hysterectomy. The most common indications treated were adenomyosis (26%), leiomyoma (20.67%), and combined pathologies, with additional cases involving endometriosis, endometrial hyperplasia, and uterovaginal prolapse. Serious complications were remarkably rare, with no cases of ureteric injury, vesicovaginal fistula, peritonitis, or thromboembolic events; only one conversion to laparotomy (0.67%) and minimal organ injuries occurred, with postoperative infection rates below 2%. Surgeon experience significantly improved outcomes, reducing both operative time and complication rates, while the use of advanced energy sources further enhanced hemostasis and efficiency. These findings support TLH as a superior minimally invasive alternative to traditional abdominal hysterectomy for appropriately selected patients with benign uterine pathology.

title: Sutureless “Slim-Mesh” Technique for the Repair of Abdominal-Wall Hernias in the Obese Population
journal: JSLS : Journal of the Society of Laparoscopic & Robotic Surgeons
summary: The sutureless "Slim-Mesh" laparoscopic technique was developed to safely repair ventral hernias, including large, giant, massive, and multiple widely-spaced hernias in obese and superobese patients. This innovative approach aims to reduce surgical time and minimize intra- and postoperative complications compared to conventional repair methods. In a prospective study of 64 obese/superobese patients between 2009 and 2023, the technique demonstrated effectiveness for ventral hernia repair with relatively low morbidity. Late postoperative complications were minimal, occurring in only 6 patients total, including four hernia recurrences (6%), two trocar-site hernias, and one case of chronic abdominal-wall pain. The Slim-Mesh technique represents a significant advancement for complex hernia repair in the challenging population of obese patients, where traditional surgical approaches may be more technically demanding and associated with higher complication rates. This sutureless laparoscopic approach offers obese patients a safer alternative for managing ventral hernias with improved perioperative outcomes.

title: No differences in IVF pregnancy outcomes following hysteroscopic polypectomy using a manual hysteroscopic tissue removal device versus conventional resection
summary: Endometrial polyps are common benign intrauterine lesions found in up to 35% of infertile women that impair fertility through multiple mechanisms including distortion of uterine cavity shape, chronic endometritis, reduced uterine receptivity, and altered cytokine and matrix metalloproteinase levels. Hysteroscopic polypectomy removes these polyps and may improve both natural conception and assisted reproductive technique outcomes, with manual hysteroscopic tissue removal (mHTR) devices offering high efficiency, safety, and complete resection rates while facilitating endometrial healing and reducing time to pregnancy. This study compared manual mHTR polypectomy to traditional mechanical resection and found no significant differences in in vitro fertilization pregnancy outcomes, with comparable positive pregnancy rates (80.2% vs. 79.5%), clinical pregnancy rates (70.3% vs. 74.4%), and miscarriage rates (11.5% vs. 12.1%) between the two techniques. The timing of embryo transfer after polypectomy did not significantly affect outcomes, as similar pregnancy and miscarriage rates were observed whether transfer occurred in the first menstrual cycle or after multiple cycles following procedure. These findings suggest that both manual mHTR and conventional mechanical resection are equally effective approaches for managing endometrial polyps in infertile women undergoing IVF.

title: Circular staplers and anastomotic leakage in colorectal surgery: meta-analysis
summary: This meta-analysis examines how circular stapler design features influence anastomotic leakage rates in colorectal surgery. The study found that smaller diameter staplers (28-29 mm) produce significantly lower leakage rates compared to larger 31-33 mm staplers, while powered stapler technology reduces leakage by approximately half compared to manual devices. The single-stapling technique proved superior to the double-stapling technique, which creates structural weaknesses including "dog-ears" and reduced bursting pressure. Powered staplers improve outcomes by stabilizing the suture line and increasing leak pressure, while patient factors including diabetes, smoking, advanced age, and high ASA scores independently increase leakage risk. Although technically focused on colorectal surgery, these findings on stapler technology and anastomotic integrity have relevant implications for gynecological laparoscopic procedures involving tissue anastomosis.

title: Sexual function and pelvic floor function five years after hysterectomy
summary:
# Summary

This five-year follow-up study of hysterectomy patients demonstrates substantial improvements in pelvic floor function, with over 50% reduction in pelvic floor dysfunction symptoms and decreased distress from urinary incontinence, prolapse, and bowel symptoms. However, sexual function outcomes diverged dramatically based on baseline sexual activity status: women who were sexually active before surgery experienced significant declines across all sexual domains (desire, arousal, lubrication, orgasm, and satisfaction), while non-sexually active women reported improvements, with nearly 24% transitioning to sexual activity. The decline in sexual function among previously active women occurred independently of surgical route, body mass index, and concomitant bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy. Importantly, pelvic floor dysfunction and associated incontinence showed strong associations with sexual dysfunction, and higher BMI at five years correlated with worse pelvic floor outcomes, suggesting that maintaining pelvic floor health and body weight may help preserve sexual function after hysterectomy for benign conditions including endometriosis.

title: Why not vaginal?—Nationwide trends and surgical outcomes in low‐risk hysterectomies: A retrospective cohort study
summary: This nationwide cohort study compared surgical approaches for low-risk hysterectomies indicated for abnormal uterine bleeding, uterine fibroids, or cervical dysplasia. Vaginal hysterectomy emerged as the most cost-effective and efficient approach, with the shortest operative time (67 minutes), lowest complication rates, briefest hospitalization, and lowest total costs. Robot-assisted hysterectomy offered reduced intraoperative complications and fewer conversions compared to vaginal approaches but required longer operative times and substantially higher costs (71% more expensive than vaginal). Laparoscopic hysterectomy showed increased conversion rates and longer operative times without clear safety advantages. Abdominal hysterectomy had the highest rates of postoperative complications and longest hospitalizations despite lower perioperative costs. The study notes that endometriosis cases are rarely performed vaginally and are considered technically challenging, suggesting that approach selection should account for specific clinical indications beyond low-risk categories.

title: Laparoscopic radical hysterectomy and pelvic lymph node dissection for early cervical cancer effectively improves surgical efficacy
summary: This study demonstrates that laparoscopic radical hysterectomy without uterine manipulation significantly improves surgical outcomes in early-stage cervical cancer treatment compared to conventional approaches. The technique reduced operative time, intraoperative blood loss, postoperative pain, and hospital stay length while promoting faster return of bowel function. Notably, the procedure achieved superior lymph node dissection rates, which multivariate analysis identified as an independent prognostic factor for cervical cancer outcomes. Tumor markers including CA125, CA199, CEA, and SCC were all substantially decreased in patients undergoing this approach. Additionally, the modified technique substantially reduced postoperative complications and resulted in improved quality of life, supporting laparoscopic radical hysterectomy combined with pelvic lymph node dissection as an effective surgical strategy for early cervical cancer management.

title: Gasless vNOTES vs. traditional vNOTES for benign gynecological disease: a randomized controlled clinical trial
summary: This randomized controlled trial compared gasless vNOTES (G-vNOTES) with traditional CO2-insufflated vNOTES (T-vNOTES) for benign gynecological procedures, addressing the known complications of CO2 absorption during laparoscopy. CO2 insufflation causes hypercapnia, acidosis, and postoperative pain while inducing cardiopulmonary complications including tachycardia, arrhythmias, and pulmonary edema; pneumoperitoneum further increases peak airway pressure, decreases cardiac output, and reduces pulmonary compliance. Although G-vNOTES maintained lower airway pressures and reduced certain anesthetic risks, it demonstrated a significantly higher conversion rate (16.95% versus 5.26%) compared to T-vNOTES, attributed to poor surgical field exposure in the gasless approach. The findings suggest that while eliminating CO2 absorption mitigates cardiopulmonary and postoperative pain complications, the technical limitations of gasless vNOTES may offset these physiological advantages for routine gynecological hysterectomy cases.

title: The Feasibility and Safety of Robot-Assisted Vaginal Natural Orifice Transluminal Endoscopic Surgery (RA-vNOTES) for Gynecologic Disease: 298-Case Series
summary:
# Robot-Assisted Vaginal Natural Orifice Transluminal Endoscopic Surgery for Gynecologic Disease

This 298-case series demonstrates that robot-assisted vaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery (RA-vNOTES) is a safe and feasible minimally invasive approach for treating multiple gynecologic conditions, with endometriosis as the primary indication in 43.62% of cases. The Da Vinci Xi robotic platform enhances surgical capabilities by providing superior three-dimensional visualization, reduced surgeon tremor and fatigue, and improved suturing precision through wristed instruments compared to traditional laparoscopy, while the vaginal approach eliminates visible abdominal incisions and improves cosmetic outcomes. The procedure effectively addressed diverse pathologies including endometrial and cervical cancers, benign conditions like uterine fibroids and pelvic organ prolapse, and complex cases with stages I-IV endometriosis, with endometriosis resection performed in 64.43% of patients resulting in significant postoperative pain reduction. Safety outcomes were favorable, with 69.13% of patients discharged within 24 hours and low rates of major complications; notably, the implementation of indocyanine green-stented ureteral identification substantially reduced ureteral injury risk, a critical concern in endometriosis surgery. The most common postoperative complication was urinary tract infection, while intraoperative injuries (bladder, bowel) occurred in fewer than 1% of cases, and deep surgical site infections affected only 0.34% of patients. RA-vNOTES thus represents a less invasive alternative to traditional laparoscopy for complex gynecologic procedures while maintaining safety comparable to or better than conventional approaches.

title: Comparison of Laparoscopic and Robotic Intraoperative Adverse Events in Benign Gynecological Procedures and the Correlation of the Adverse Events With Postoperative Outcomes and Risk Analysis
summary:
# Summary

This comparative study evaluated intraoperative adverse events (iAEs) and postoperative complications in laparoscopic versus robotic gynecological procedures, with particular attention to endometriosis cases. Robotic surgery demonstrated superior safety outcomes, with zero intraoperative adverse events compared to 7.5% in the laparoscopic group, primarily due to enhanced three-dimensional vision and precision that enabled better hemorrhage control and reduced organ injury. The severity of intraoperative complications strongly correlated with postoperative complication grades, with higher iAE grades associated with three-fold increases in hospital stay duration and significantly elevated postoperative morbidity. Endometriosis, combined with previous abdominal surgery, elevated ASA classification, advanced age, preoperative anemia, and obesity emerged as independent risk factors for major postoperative complications. Surgical expertise and high case volume substantially mitigated complication rates across both approaches, while detailed preoperative risk assessment is recommended to optimize patient selection and reduce both intraoperative and postoperative complications in benign gynecological procedures.

title: A comparative study of prone split-leg position and lithotomy position in posterior uterine myomectomy by transvaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery
summary:
# Summary

This study compared the prone split-leg position (PSP) with the traditional lithotomy position (LP) for transvaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery (vNOTES) in posterior uterine myomectomy. The prone split-leg position significantly reduced operative time (93.39 versus 112.67 minutes) while enhancing surgical visibility and manipulation space, thereby reducing the complexity and difficulty of accessing posterior uterine fibroids through the posterior vaginal fornix approach. Although PSP required longer preoperative preparation time, it reduced the number of required surgical assistants and improved ergonomics with less physical and mental stress on the operating team compared to lithotomy positioning. The vNOTES technique itself offers additional advantages including lower postoperative pain, reduced analgesic requirements, shorter hospital stays, and prevention of incision-related complications inherent to traditional laparoscopy. These findings demonstrate that the prone split-leg position substantially improves the efficiency, safety, and feasibility of vaginal approach myomectomy, though patient suitability must be assessed as the position may not suit those with obesity or severe spinal deformities.

title: Helium Plasma-Driven Radiofrequency for Skin Contraction: Clinical Use, Safety Recommendations, and Results in High-Definition Body Contouring Surgery
summary:
# Summary

This article examines the clinical application of helium plasma-driven radiofrequency technology (J-Plasma/RFHP) for skin contraction in high-definition body contouring surgery. The RFHP device rapidly achieves temperatures exceeding 85°C, which denatures collagen proteins and activates fibroblasts to produce immediate and sustained tissue retraction, with collagen fiber length reduced by 40-50%. When combined with high-definition liposculpture, RFHP achieves superior skin tightening compared to traditional liposuction alone, with reported surface area reductions of 28.5% versus 10.3% for conventional techniques. Beyond cosmetic applications, the technology demonstrates significant advantages for endometriosis management by preventing vaginal dehiscence and intraabdominal adhesions while treating chronic pelvic pain in gynecological procedures. However, RFHP use is associated with notable complications including postoperative pain, rare cases of pneumoperitoneum, skin overcorrection, and extended ecchymosis, with management protocols employing NSAIDs, serial massage, ultrasound, and physical therapy for adverse outcomes. The authors recommend specific perioperative protocols including pregabalin for neuropathic pain prevention and identify pregnancy and lactation as contraindications for RFHP treatment.

title: Incidence and risk factors of postpartum urinary retention following cesarean section: a retrospective nationwide inpatient sample database study
summary: This retrospective nationwide study identified multiple demographic, clinical, and procedural risk factors for postpartum urinary retention following cesarean section. Advanced maternal age (25–34 years and ≥35 years), Asian and Pacific Islander ethnicity, and the presence of comorbidities significantly increased retention risk, with the effect amplified by neurological conditions, fluid-electrolyte disorders, and paralysis. Direct surgical injuries to the bladder or ureteral structures showed the strongest association with urinary retention (OR=6.12), while bladder catheter placement (OR=22.57) and postoperative complications including severe infections, acute renal failure, and hemorrhage substantially elevated risk. Protective factors included low cervical cesarean technique, prior cesarean delivery, obesity, and hypertension, though the mechanism underlying these protective effects remains unclear. Patients experiencing postpartum urinary retention demonstrated significantly longer hospital stays, higher costs, and increased morbidity, with untreated retention potentially causing parasympathetic nerve damage and rare but life-threatening bladder rupture. These findings highlight the need for careful patient selection and perioperative monitoring to reduce retention incidence and associated maternal morbidity.

title: Ventral Rectopexy: An International Expert Panel Consensus and Review of Contemporary Literature
journal: Diseases of the Colon and Rectum
summary:
# Summary

Ventral rectopexy (VR) is a minimally invasive surgical procedure that effectively treats full-thickness external rectal prolapse, fecal incontinence, obstructed defecation, and multicompartment pelvic prolapse. The laparoscopic approach offers significant advantages over open surgery, including reduced postoperative pain and faster patient recovery, with emerging evidence suggesting robotic-assisted VR may further improve bowel function recovery. However, VR is contraindicated or relatively contraindicated in patients with active pelvic malignancy, pregnancy, fistulizing rectovaginal disease, pelvic sepsis, inflammatory bowel disease, and significant pelvic endometriosis. Technical considerations include avoiding permanent sutures due to prosthesis complications and careful attention to fixation methods, as sacral promontory fixation carries risk of discitis. These consensus findings establish VR as a safe and effective minimally invasive option for appropriately selected patients with pelvic floor disorders.

title: Prevention and Treatment of Intraoperative Complications During Gynecological Laparoscopic Surgery: Practical Tips and Tricks—A Narrative Review
summary:
# Summary

Laparoscopic gynecological surgery for endometriosis carries significant intraoperative and postoperative risks, with bowel injuries and vascular injuries being the most common and potentially serious complications. Bowel injuries result primarily from thermal damage, trocar placement errors, and Veress needle insertion, while vascular injuries, though rarer, carry high mortality rates and occur mainly during trocar insertion or adhesiolysis in complex cases. Multiple risk factors increase injury likelihood, including patient age over 38 years, surgery duration exceeding 94-99 minutes, elevated hemoglobin loss, higher ASA status, and pathological complexity such as deep infiltrating endometriosis and frozen pelvis. Prevention strategies are well-established and include accurate trocar placement with visual confirmation, cold dissection techniques, careful energy device use, preoperative imaging, and prophylactic measures like Foley catheterization for high-risk urinary tract injury cases. Surgical training, particularly through virtual reality simulators, significantly reduces technical errors and improves precision. Early recognition and appropriate management—whether through immediate laparoscopic or open repair for bowel injuries or hemorrhage control for vascular injuries—are critical to minimizing morbidity in these complex gynecological procedures.

title: Stapled versus handsewn methods for colorectal anastomosis surgery
journal: The Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews
summary: This Cochrane systematic review compared stapled and handsewn techniques for colorectal anastomosis across multiple outcomes in large patient populations. Stapled anastomosis was associated with significantly shorter operative time (7.6 minutes faster) and a higher rate of stricture formation compared to handsewn methods, which required longer operative duration. Importantly, stapled anastomosis was associated with a significantly increased need for reoperation, though mortality, overall anastomotic dehiscence, and other major complications including clinical dehiscence, hemorrhage, and wound infection showed no significant differences between the two techniques. Hospital stay and radiological evidence of dehiscence were comparable between approaches. The findings suggest that while stapled anastomosis offers a procedural time advantage, the increased stricture rate and reoperation requirement warrant careful consideration when selecting surgical technique for colorectal anastomosis.

title: Intraoperative and Postoperative Outcomes in Patients Undergoing Total Laparoscopic Hysterectomy for Benign Conditions With Drain Placement
summary:
# Summary
