This study examined intraoperative and postoperative outcomes in 415 patients undergoing total laparoscopic hysterectomy for benign conditions, comparing those who received surgical drains with those who did not. While laparoscopic hysterectomy offers significant advantages over abdominal approaches, including reduced hospitalization time, faster recovery, decreased pain, and lower wound infection rates, the analysis revealed that drain placement was associated with several adverse outcomes. Patients receiving drains experienced significantly longer operation times, increased length of hospital stay, higher complication rates, greater transfusion requirements, and lower postoperative hemoglobin levels, suggesting that drain application may serve as a predictive marker for intraoperative bleeding complications. Hysterectomy effectively treats multiple benign conditions including uterine fibroids, abnormal uterine bleeding, and chronic pelvic pain, while the study documented overall low complication rates from the laparoscopic approach itself, including cuff infections (0.72%), bladder injuries (1.20%), and other visceral injuries. These findings suggest that while drains are intended to prevent abscess formation and potentially reduce postoperative pain through gas evacuation, their use should be carefully considered given the association with prolonged operative times and increased morbidity.

title: Artificial intelligence for instance segmentation of MRI: advancing efficiency and safety in laparoscopic myomectomy of broad ligament fibroids
summary: Broad ligament fibroids present significant surgical challenges due to their intimate association with uterine vascular arcades and ureteric compression, creating high risks of ureteric injury and uterine vessel damage during myomectomy. While laparoscopic myomectomy offers advantages over open surgery including reduced postoperative pain and faster recovery, the procedure remains complicated by high intraoperative bleeding and prolonged operative times, particularly when inadequate fibroid localization forces conversions to laparotomy. To address these challenges, researchers developed an artificial intelligence system using deep learning models (modified U-Net with attention mechanisms and optimized Mask-RCNN) to segment fibroids, uterine walls, and surrounding anatomy from preoperative MRI with millimeter-level precision. In a clinical trial comparing AI-assisted versus conventional laparoscopic myomectomy, the AI-guided approach significantly reduced operative time, intraoperative blood loss, and improved postoperative recovery, with patients achieving first flatus within 24 hours at substantially higher rates. The AI system enables surgeons to discern critical anatomical relationships preoperatively through 3D reconstruction, thereby minimizing intraoperative exploration time and reducing iatrogenic complications. This multidisciplinary AI technology directly addresses the operative time-blood loss paradigm in broad ligament myomectomy, advancing both surgical safety and efficiency.

title: Peri- and Postoperative Complications in Abdominal, Vaginal Extraperitoneal, and Vaginal Intraperitoneal Colpopexy
summary: This study compared perioperative outcomes and complications across three surgical approaches for treating symptomatic pelvic organ prolapse: abdominal, vaginal extraperitoneal, and vaginal intraperitoneal sacral colpopexy. The abdominal approach demonstrated significantly longer operative times, extended hospital stays, and higher rates of bleeding requiring transfusion, as well as increased overall morbidity and mortality. The vaginal extraperitoneal approach, used predominantly in older patients with prior pelvic operations, showed elevated rates of postoperative urinary retention associated with advanced age. Endometriosis was more frequently associated with the vaginal intraperitoneal approach, while serious urinary tract complications such as ureteral fistula occurred exclusively with the abdominal method. These findings suggest that minimally invasive vaginal approaches offer significant advantages over open abdominal sacral colpopexy for pelvic organ prolapse repair, with reduced hospitalization and transfusion requirements, though patient age and surgical history influence complication profiles across approaches.

title: Comparison of laparoscopic vs. robotic sentinel lymph node mapping and biopsy in  endometrial cancer
summary: Sentinel lymph node biopsy has become standard for staging clinically confined endometrial cancer, with indigo-cyanine green dye significantly improving detection success. This study compared robotic-assisted laparoscopy (RAL) with standard laparoscopy for sentinel lymph node mapping, finding that while both approaches achieved similar bilateral biopsy success rates (60.9% vs. 69.2%), RAL demonstrated improved outcomes with surgeon experience despite requiring longer operative time. Patient factors substantially impacted success, with increasing body mass index and age significantly reducing bilateral sentinel lymph node detection, likely through obesity-mediated impairment of pelvic lymphatic access and age-related lymphatic deterioration. RAL showed a learning curve effect, with lower success during initial implementation but improving substantially with surgeon experience. Across successfully mapped cases, sentinel lymph node biopsy identified metastatic disease in 11.5% of patients, including isolated tumor cells, micrometastases, and macrometastases, with no adverse oncological outcomes compared to systematic lymphadenectomy.

title: Laparoscopic and Robot-Assisted Laparoscopic Management of Iatrogenic Ureteral Strictures: Preliminary Experience
summary:
# Summary

Iatrogenic ureteral strictures represent a significant complication of urological and surgical procedures, including ureterorenoscopy with laser lithotripsy and pancreaticoduodenectomy, occurring in approximately 3.5% of cases following endoscopic stone treatment. Both laparoscopic ureteroureterostomy and robot-assisted approaches effectively treat lumbar and pelvic ureteral strictures, with success rates exceeding 90% and preservation of renal function in nearly 89% of patients at median 38-month follow-up. Robotic platforms enhance surgical precision and intracorporeal suturing capability while reducing operative time, blood loss, hospitalization duration, and inflammatory response markers compared to open approaches, though stricture recurrence can occur up to one year postoperatively. Key technical challenges include managing fibrosis, scarring, and obliterated surgical planes, with adequate blood supply preservation being critical to prevent ureteral necrosis and recurrent strictures. When performed by experienced surgeons in specialized centers, minimally invasive ureteral reconstruction significantly reduces postoperative morbidity while achieving durable functional outcomes and patent ureter restoration.

title: Efficacy of Quadratus Lumborum Block for Postoperative Pain Management in Single-Port Total Laparoscopic Hysterectomy: A Randomized Observer-Blinded Controlled Trial
summary:
# Summary

This randomized controlled trial evaluated the quadratus lumborum block (QLB) for postoperative pain management following single-port total laparoscopic hysterectomy. While QLB successfully reduced overall opioid consumption and postoperative pain as part of multimodal analgesia regimens employing regional anesthesia techniques, the block demonstrated limited efficacy in this specific surgical context. Single-port laparoscopic hysterectomy inherently causes minimal somatic pain due to reduced port-site trauma, whereas the procedure predominantly induces visceral pain transmitted through the hypogastric plexus and pelvic splanchnic nerves—pain pathways that QLB's coverage of thoracic paravertebral nerves (T8/9-L1/3) cannot reliably block. Notably, QLB was associated with increased postoperative nausea and vomiting (25.9% versus 3.4% in controls), a potentially serious adverse effect that may relate to local anesthetic systemic absorption. The findings suggest that QLB may be more appropriately deployed in multiport laparoscopic or open abdominal hysterectomies where somatic pain comprises a larger proportion of postoperative discomfort, rather than in single-port procedures where visceral pain dominates.

title: Intergroup statement: opportunistic salpingectomy—molecular pathology, clinical outcomes and implications for practice (German Ovarian Cancer Commission, the North-Eastern German Society of Gynecologic Oncology (NOGGO), AGO Austria and AGO Swiss)
summary:
# Summary

This intergroup consensus statement examines the molecular pathology and clinical implications of opportunistic salpingectomy for ovarian cancer prevention. The fallopian tube has emerged as the primary origin site for high-grade serous tubal ovarian carcinoma (HGSTOC), with serous intraepithelial carcinomas (STICs) containing TP53 mutations representing precursor lesions that can progress to invasive disease and peritoneal carcinomatosis. Bilateral salpingectomy reduces ovarian cancer risk by approximately 42-50% and prevents HGSTOC development without significant morbidity, making it an attractive opportunistic intervention during gynecologic surgery, particularly in BRCA1/2 mutation carriers where risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy remains the gold standard. Additional risk-reduction strategies including tubal ligation, hysterectomy, and combined adnexectomy provide varying degrees of ovarian cancer prevention, though early menopause from oophorectomy may carry cardiovascular and bone health consequences without hormone replacement therapy. While STICs are found in up to 10% of risk-reduction specimens and approximately 50% of existing HGSTOCs, not all lesions progress to malignancy, suggesting complex molecular pathways in tumorigenesis. The molecular data supporting tubal epithelial origin of HGSTOC, combined with the clinical efficacy and safety profile of salpingectomy, support its incorporation into practice for appropriate patient populations.

title: Day case total laparoscopic hysterectomy in a low resource setting: A descriptive analysis
summary:
# Summary

Total laparoscopic hysterectomy (TLH) represents a minimally invasive surgical approach that significantly reduces patient morbidity, intraoperative blood loss, postoperative analgesic requirements, and infection rates compared to traditional abdominal surgery, while enabling shorter hospitalization and day-case discharge. In this low-resource Caribbean setting, uterine leiomyoma was the most common indication for TLH (45%), followed by endometrial cancer (17.5%), with the majority of procedures involving bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy. Implementation of Enhanced Recovery After Surgery (ERAS) protocols, including early catheter removal, multimodal non-opioid analgesia, early ambulation, and optimized intraoperative ventilation strategies targeting lower end-tidal CO₂, successfully facilitated same-day discharge in most patients. Key perioperative strategies such as pressure-controlled ventilation, quantitative neuromuscular monitoring with rocuronium/sugammadex reversal, and maintenance of pneumoperitoneum at 8-10 mmHg mitigated the physiological challenges of steep Trendelenburg positioning and pneumoperitoneum. Day-case TLH proved safe and feasible for carefully selected patients with both benign and malignant gynecological conditions, delivering substantial benefits including reduced healthcare costs, improved theatre efficiency, and enhanced patient satisfaction in resource-limited settings.

title: Perioperative enhanced recovery after surgery (ERAS) for non‐malignant gynaecological conditions
journal: The Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews
summary:
# Summary

Surgical stress triggers inflammatory and hormonal responses that impair postoperative recovery through multiple pathways. Sympathetic nervous system activation produces catecholamines, which inhibit insulin-producing beta cells and cause insulin resistance, leading to hyperglycemia that negatively impacts wound healing and increases infection risk. Enhanced Recovery After Surgery (ERAS) protocols comprehensively address these complications through multiple interventions: minimally invasive surgical approaches reduce tissue trauma, carbohydrate loading decreases insulin resistance, early mobilization restores bowel function and prevents thrombosis, multimodal analgesia reduces opioid-related complications, and temperature maintenance with antimicrobial prophylaxis prevent infections. Applied to laparoscopic hysterectomy for endometriosis and other non-malignant gynecological conditions, ERAS protocols significantly reduce hospital stay, postoperative pain, perioperative complications, and readmissions, offering both clinical and economic benefits for managing high-cost conditions like endometriosis.

title: Effect of internal iliac artery ligation on sonographic blood changes in the uterine arteries: A case–control study in women with postpartum hemorrhage in sub-Saharan Africa
journal: The Journal of International Medical Research
summary:
# Summary

Internal iliac artery ligation (IIAL) is an effective uterine-preserving surgical intervention for treating postpartum hemorrhage by reducing pelvic blood flow by approximately 49% and diminishing blood flow to the uterus, particularly in cases of uterine atony and placental invasion anomalies. Despite these reductions in overall pelvic perfusion, sonographic analysis revealed that IIAL does not impair uterine blood perfusion or significantly alter key Doppler parameters including pulsatility index, resistance index, or maximal-to-minimal diastolic flow in the uterine arteries. This apparent paradox is explained by pelvic collateral circulation and recanalization of the internal iliac artery, which maintain near-normal perfusion pressure to the uterus and preserve its viability. IIAL successfully avoids the need for hysterectomy in women with severe postpartum hemorrhage and represents an important alternative to more destructive surgical approaches, positioning it as a key uterine-sparing strategy alongside other interventions such as selective uterine devascularization and uterine artery embolization.

title: Unveiling the real benefits of robot-assisted surgery in gynaecology: from telesurgery to image-guided surgery and artificial intelligence
summary: Robotic-assisted surgery has emerged as a valuable tool in gynecological oncology and benign conditions, with established applications in endometrial and ovarian cancer management, particularly in obese patients, and in complex cases of deep and pelvic endometriosis. Advanced imaging-guided surgical techniques, including three-dimensional reconstructions, virtual and augmented reality, and intraoperative ultrasound, significantly enhance surgical safety and quality by enabling better visualization of anatomical structures, sentinel lymph node identification, and assessment of endometriotic bowel infiltration. The integration of artificial intelligence technologies, particularly computer vision analysis of surgical workflows and large language models providing real-time clinical decision support, reduces surgical complications and improves operating room efficiency. Telesurgery platforms show promise for democratizing access to advanced surgical care in underserved and rural areas, while hybrid operating rooms equipped with integrated intraoperative imaging systems enhance real-time surgical guidance. Deep learning algorithms further support automatic image analysis and interpretation, contributing to improved clinical outcomes. These technological advances collectively represent the transition toward digital surgery, positioning robotic and image-guided approaches as fundamental components of modern minimally invasive gynecological practice.

title: Do we need a preventive stoma in surgery for colorectal endometriosis? A retrospective series of 97 patients treated at an expert centre
summary: Colorectal endometriosis, occurring in 5.3-12% of patients with deep endometriosis, is a benign disorder characterized by endometrial-like tissue outside the uterus that triggers chronic inflammatory reactions. This study of 97 patients evaluated surgical approaches including segmental resection, disc excision, and rectal shaving, finding that laparoscopic surgery performed by expert surgeons in high-volume centers significantly reduces postoperative morbidity without routine preventive stoma formation. While rectovaginal fistula remains the most serious complication affecting quality of life and fertility, occurring in 3.09% of cases, the study demonstrated that careful surgical technique and multidisciplinary management effectively minimize this risk even in conservative approaches that preserve rectal tissue. Larger endometriotic nodules (≥30 mm) and concomitant vaginal suturing were associated with increased complications, though preventive stomas did not conclusively prevent fistula development. The findings support that surgeon expertise and high-volume center protocols are critical factors determining favorable outcomes in colorectal endometriosis surgery, with conservative techniques preferred when feasible to optimize functional outcomes.

title: Sexual quality of life after hysterectomy performed by conventional laparoscopy versus Vaginal Natural Orifice Transluminal Endoscopic Surgery (vNOTES) in benign gynaecology
summary: Adenomyosis significantly impairs sexual function through reduced arousal and orgasm capacity, conditions substantially improved by hysterectomy. This study compared vaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery (vNOTES) with conventional laparoscopic hysterectomy (TLH) for treating benign gynecological conditions, finding that vNOTES produced superior postoperative sexual quality of life outcomes including higher arousal, orgasm, and overall satisfaction scores compared to TLH. The vNOTES approach facilitated faster recovery with shorter hospital stays, reduced analgesic requirements, and earlier resumption of sexual activity, with 75% of vNOTES patients versus 43% of TLH patients returning to sexual activity within three months. Additionally, vNOTES prevented postoperative complications such as dyspareunia and post-coital bleeding through superior vaginal scar healing. Although vNOTES resulted in slightly higher total blood loss without clinical consequences, it is contraindicated in cases of endometriosis at the pouch of Douglas, whereas traditional laparoscopy carries increased risks of urinary and digestive tract injury. Overall, vNOTES emerges as the preferred minimally invasive approach for hysterectomy in appropriate candidates seeking optimal sexual function restoration.

title: An innovative and effective approach of cervical canal penetration in patients diagnosed with type II vaginal atresia with adequate uterine corpus development: A case series
summary: Type II vaginal atresia, often accompanied by cervical dysplasia and a functional endometrium, presents clinically with absent menarche, periodic abdominal pain, and pelvic masses including hematometra and sometimes coexisting endometriosis. The authors describe a laparoscopic and ultrasound-guided cervical canal penetration technique that successfully treats this condition while preserving uterine and cervical integrity and avoiding damage to adjacent organs like the bladder and rectum. The procedure involves porcine small intestine submucosal grafts that promote host cell proliferation, tissue remodeling, and vascular regeneration at the surgical site. Postoperatively, patients received gonadotropin-releasing hormone antagonist treatment to suppress endometrial function, and all patients achieved regular menstruation with resolution of periodic abdominal pain. Postoperative complications included vaginal adhesions and cervical adhesion formation, necessitating additional dilation procedures in some cases. This minimally invasive approach offers advantages over traditional vaginal reconstruction methods by enabling concurrent diagnosis and treatment of pelvic endometriosis while maintaining anatomical integrity.

title: Comparison of rapid recovery outcomes between vNOTES hysterectomy and laparoscopic hysterectomy: a prospective study
summary: Vaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic hysterectomy (vNOTEH) demonstrates superior rapid recovery outcomes compared to conventional laparoscopic hysterectomy (LH) across multiple clinical parameters. vNOTEH significantly reduces operation time, time to first anal exhaust, postoperative hospitalization duration, and return-to-work time, with employed patients resuming work approximately one month earlier than those undergoing LH. The vNOTES approach facilitates faster gastrointestinal and bowel function recovery, as evidenced by shorter time to first anal exhaust, which serves as a key marker of postoperative intestinal recovery and correlates with earlier return to work. Additionally, vNOTEH patients report substantially higher cosmetic satisfaction scores compared to LH recipients, reflecting the minimally invasive nature of this third-generation surgical technique. No conversions to alternative surgical approaches or readmissions occurred in the vNOTEH group, demonstrating the safety and efficacy of this approach. These findings establish vNOTEH as a promising alternative for hysterectomy in women requiring gynecological surgery, offering accelerated functional recovery and improved patient satisfaction while maintaining surgical safety.

title: Applicability of ultrasound-guided puncture and laparoscopic cystectomy for ovarian endometriosis cyst: a non-randomized trial
summary: Ovarian endometriosis cysts, arising from ectopic endometrium, are strongly associated with pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and infertility. This trial compared two surgical approaches: laparoscopic cystectomy (LC) and ultrasound-guided puncture (UGP). While LC achieved superior symptom relief and cyst elimination, reducing pain scores and cancer antigen 125 levels more effectively, it significantly impaired ovarian reserve by decreasing anti-Müllerian hormone levels. In contrast, UGP, being minimally invasive and not requiring general anesthesia, preserved ovarian reserve and even increased AMH concentrations through mechanical stress reduction and mitigation of inflammatory cytokines. The optimal treatment choice depends on clinical presentation: LC is recommended for patients with multiple or bilateral cysts and severe pain symptoms, whereas UGP is better suited for those with fewer cysts and milder symptoms who wish to preserve reproductive function.

title: Risk of major gynecologic surgery before age 40 among daughters of young mothers
summary: This study demonstrates that daughters of young mothers, particularly those under age 20, face significantly elevated risk of requiring major gynecologic surgery including bilateral oophorectomy or hysterectomy before age 40, with relative risks of 1.74 and 1.35 for maternal ages under 20 and 20-24 years respectively. Beyond maternal age, multiple socioeconomic and reproductive factors independently contribute to this surgical risk, including lower childhood income, maternal education below bachelor's degree, and giving birth before age 25. The mechanisms underlying these associations appear multifactorial, potentially reflecting both inherited genetic predisposition and adverse socioeconomic circumstances that influence gynecologic health outcomes. Notably, daughters of younger mothers did not demonstrate reduced childbearing capacity, suggesting the surgical interventions reflect pathology management rather than fertility problems. These findings indicate that young maternal age serves as a marker for increased gynecologic disease burden in offspring, though the precise biological and environmental mechanisms connecting maternal age to daughter's gynecologic health warrant further investigation.

title: Role of transumbilical laparoendoscopic single-site surgery in the implementation ERAS in gynecology oncology: a retrospective study
summary: This retrospective study demonstrates that transumbilical laparoendoscopic single-site surgery (TU-LESS) combined with enhanced recovery after surgery (ERAS) protocols provides significant benefits for gynecological oncology patients, successfully treating 315 cases of cervical, endometrial, and ovarian cancers. TU-LESS substantially reduces postoperative pain, hospital stay duration, and incisional complications compared to conventional laparoscopy, while minimally invasive approaches mitigate immunological stress from surgical trauma. ERAS protocols further minimize postoperative complications and reduce length of stay through evidence-based perioperative management strategies, including propofol anesthesia, 5-HT receptor antagonists combined with dexamethasone for nausea prevention, and low molecular weight heparin prophylaxis against venous thromboembolism. While the study documents various intraoperative complications (vascular, gastrointestinal, and urinary injuries) and postoperative adverse events (infections, hemorrhage, and thrombosis), TU-LESS enables faster recovery with improved surgical outcomes when surgeon proficiency is established. However, single-site laparoscopy carries a recognized elevated hernia risk at the umbilical incision site. Overall, the integration of TU-LESS with ERAS protocols represents an effective minimally invasive approach that enhances patient recovery in gynecological malignancy treatment.

title: Postoperative abdominopelvic adhesion and umbilical wound validation after single-port laparoscopy or two-port laparoscopy for gynecological surgery: a comparison with conventional laparoscopy
summary:
# Summary

This study compared postoperative adhesion formation and wound complications across single-port, two-port, and conventional laparoscopy for gynecological surgery, with particular relevance to endometriosis cases. The key finding was that single-port laparoscopy paradoxically increases intraperitoneal umbilical adhesions despite reducing overall tissue trauma, with adhesion incidence rising as the number of ports decreased and correlating with umbilical wound size. Surgical trauma triggers peritoneal damage, which initiates an inflammatory response promoting procoagulatory reactions and fibrin persistence—the critical determinant of adhesion development. While laparoscopy generally produces less scarring than open surgery, single-port approaches carry elevated risks for umbilical hernias and port site adhesions, complications that can lead to serious sequelae including chronic pain, infertility, and bowel obstruction. Perioperative inflammatory markers, particularly preoperative white blood cell counts and postoperative inflammatory status, emerged as significant predictive factors for adhesion severity. These findings suggest that while single-port laparoscopy offers cosmetic and tissue-sparing advantages, the concentrated umbilical trauma may necessitate careful patient selection, particularly in endometriosis cases where baseline pelvic adhesions are prevalent.

title: Safety and Efficacy of Robotic Hysterectomy Using an Indigenous Robotic System: A Retrospective Study
summary: This retrospective study demonstrates that robotic hysterectomy using the indigenous SSI Mantra robotic system safely and effectively treats multiple benign gynecological conditions, including endometriosis, fibroids, adenomyosis, and abnormal uterine bleeding. The robotic approach significantly outperforms traditional open laparotomy, which is associated with organ injury, wound dehiscence, and hemorrhage, while offering advantages over laparoscopic hysterectomy through superior ergonomics, a shorter learning curve, improved visualization with seven degrees of freedom, and tremor-free precision movements. Among 15 patients treated with this minimally invasive technique, robotic hysterectomy achieved minimal blood loss without transfusions, low postoperative pain scores (mean VAS 3.86), reduced hospital stay duration, and no conversions to open surgery or major complications. The procedure proved particularly beneficial for complex cases involving large fibroids, obese patients, and those with prior pelvic surgery, populations for whom vaginal hysterectomy is contraindicated and laparoscopic approaches are challenging. Notably, operative time and docking time improved substantially with surgeon experience, decreasing from 25 to 5 minutes respectively across cases. The SSI Mantra system's indigenous design provides cost-effectiveness while maintaining the clinical advantages of robotic-assisted surgery, making it a valuable alternative for gynecological procedures worldwide.

title: Post-Market Clinical Follow-Up Study of the LevaLap 1.0 during Laparoscopic Access
journal: JSLS : Journal of the Society of Laparoscopic & Robotic Surgeons
summary: This post-market clinical study evaluated the LevaLap 1.0, a surgical access device designed to improve abdominal entry during gynecologic laparoscopic procedures. The device demonstrated exceptional performance in facilitating peritoneal access, achieving successful entry on the first attempt in 96.8% of cases and within two attempts in 99.4% of cases when used with Veress needle insufflation. Surgeons reported substantial subjective improvements with the device, including easier access (59.5%), increased confidence (68.3%), enhanced access control (67.1%), and improved access efficiency. The LevaLap 1.0 appeared to provide particular benefit for stabilizing and optimizing abdominal entry, addressing a critical technical aspect of laparoscopic surgery initiation. Adverse events were minimal, with only one minor device-related event reported (localized skin redness at the application site). These findings suggest the LevaLap 1.0 is a safe and effective tool for improving laparoscopic access success and surgeon confidence during gynecologic procedures, including those for endometriosis management.

title: Does Ultrasound-Guided Quadratus Lumborum Block Improve Pain after Hysterectomy
journal: JSLS : Journal of the Society of Laparoscopic & Robotic Surgeons
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluated whether ultrasound-guided quadratus lumborum (QLB) block, a regional anesthesia technique, could improve postoperative pain control following minimally invasive hysterectomy for endometriosis. Patients undergoing laparoscopic hysterectomy were randomized to receive either a QLB or no QLB as an adjunct to standard pain management. The analysis found no significant difference in postoperative pain scores between the QLB and no-QLB groups at any time point measured, nor were there significant differences in opioid, acetaminophen, or ibuprofen usage between groups. These findings suggest that the QLB does not provide meaningful additional benefit for pain reduction in patients undergoing minimally invasive hysterectomy, indicating that standard multimodal analgesia may be sufficient for postoperative pain management in this surgical population.

title: Articulating Tissue Sealing Device for Advanced Laparoscopic Pelvic Surgery
journal: Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology of India
summary: This case report demonstrates the clinical utility of the ENSEAL G2 articulating tissue sealer in advanced laparoscopic pelvic surgery for endometriosis management. The device was successfully employed for multiple surgical procedures including adhesiolysis, omentectomy, hysterectomy, and salpingo-oophorectomy in a patient with endometrial cancer and abnormal perimenopausal bleeding. The articulating feature of the ENSEAL G2 provides significant advantages over conventional straight-stick vessel sealers by enabling superior tissue sealing and reducing inadequate ligation of the uterine artery, which commonly causes hemorrhage during pelvic procedures. The device's articulation allows improved access to contralateral structures and is particularly effective for larger tissue dissection, though ultrasonic instruments remain preferable for fine dissection tasks such as bladder dissection. By improving hemostatic control and reducing patient morbidity, the ENSEAL G2 articulating tissue sealer represents a valuable technological advancement for advanced laparoscopic treatment of endometriosis and related pelvic pathology.

title: Cystopexy raises the post-operative complication rate during laparoscopic hysterectomy for uterine prolapse
summary: This study examined whether adding cystopexy to laparoscopic colposuspension or colposacropexy procedures improves outcomes in women undergoing surgery for pelvic organ prolapse. The key finding is that cystopexy significantly increased postoperative complications, with Group B (procedures including cystopexy) experiencing a 16% complication rate compared to 2.4% in Group A, including cases of postoperative anemia requiring transfusion and pulmonary embolism. Notably, despite the higher complication burden, cystopexy did not reduce prolapse recurrence at 24-month follow-up compared to surgery without cystopexy. While cystopexy may theoretically prevent stress incontinence and address anterior compartment defects, the substantially increased perioperative morbidity without demonstrated benefit for recurrence prevention suggests that routine addition of cystopexy to central compartment prolapse surgery warrants reconsideration. The study demonstrates that laparoscopic approaches remain advantageous for minimizing ureteral injury through superior visualization, even when complications increase with additional procedures.

title: Comparison of barbed suture and conventional suture in laparoendoscopic single-site myomectomy
summary: This study compares barbed versus conventional sutures in laparoendoscopic single-site myomectomy (LESS-M), a minimally invasive approach for treating uterine fibroids that cause menorrhagia, pelvic pain, and infertility. Barbed sutures, specifically the V-Loc device, employ unidirectional barbs and loop ends that eliminate the need for surgical knots, thereby facilitating uterine wall closure and reducing suturing time. The investigation demonstrated that LESS-M performed with barbed sutures significantly reduced operative time, intraoperative blood loss, and postoperative hemoglobin decline compared to conventional suture techniques. Laparoscopic myomectomy generally provides advantages over open surgery including shorter hospitalization, lower perioperative complications, and reduced postoperative pain. These findings suggest that barbed sutures represent a superior technical refinement for LESS-M, enhancing surgical efficiency and reducing patient morbidity during the treatment of symptomatic uterine myomas.

title: Effect of bilateral uterine artery ligation on blood loss during total laparoscopic hysterectomy
summary: This study evaluated the efficacy of bilateral uterine artery ligation (BUAL) during total laparoscopic hysterectomy (TLH) for treating benign uterine conditions including abnormal uterine bleeding, leiomyoma, and adenomyosis. The key finding was that BUAL significantly reduced intraoperative blood loss (103.7 mL) compared to conventional TLH (128.7 mL), achieved by ligating uterine arteries at their origin to induce temporary uterine ischemia and secure hemostasis while reducing ureteral injury risk. However, this hemostatic benefit came at the cost of slightly longer operative time in the BUAL group, though the posterior approach to artery ligation proved more time-efficient. Overall, TLH demonstrated substantial advantages over traditional abdominal hysterectomy, including reduced postoperative complications, shorter hospital stays, decreased analgesic requirements, lower infection and ileus rates, and improved patient recovery and quality of life. The study confirms that when performed by experienced surgeons using modern vessel-sealing technologies, TLH with strategic vascular control represents an effective minimally invasive approach for benign gynecologic pathology, with operative time correlating significantly with both blood loss and hemoglobin preservation.

title: A Randomized Controlled Trial Comparing the Efficacy of Bupivacaine Injection in Vaginal Vault and Paracervical Region Versus Vaginal Vault Infiltration with Bupivacaine After Total Laparoscopic Hysterectomy
journal: Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology of India
summary: This randomized controlled trial evaluated local anesthetic strategies for managing postoperative pain following total laparoscopic hysterectomy, comparing paracervical block combined with vaginal vault infiltration (bupivacaine) versus vault infiltration alone. The combined paracervical block and vault infiltration approach significantly reduced postoperative pain scores at 1, 2, and 6 hours post-operatively, with mean visual analog scale (VAS) scores of 1.2, 2.07, and 3.13 respectively, compared to 3.67, 6.13, and 7.93 in the vault infiltration-only group. Notably, no patients receiving the combined treatment required rescue analgesia, while 13.3% of the vault infiltration-only group required additional opioid analgesia at 1 hour post-surgery. The superior efficacy of the combined approach likely stems from enhanced anesthetic coverage throughout the operative period, as the paracervical block compensates for bupivacaine's relatively short half-life of 2.7 hours. This multimodal local anesthetic strategy resulted in reduced opioid requirements and facilitated earlier return to daily activities with potentially shorter hospital stays, offering a minimally invasive pain management alternative to systemic analgesics following laparoscopic hysterectomy.

title: Conversion to laparotomy during laparoscopic hysterectomy: a meta-analysis of prevalence and key risk factors
summary:
# Summary

This meta-analysis examines conversion rates from laparoscopic to open hysterectomy and identifies critical risk factors influencing surgical outcomes. While laparoscopic hysterectomy offers substantial benefits over traditional open surgery—including reduced postoperative pain, shorter recovery times, decreased blood loss, and fewer overall complications—conversion to laparotomy negates these advantages and increases morbidity, extends hospitalization, and delays recovery. The analysis identified several strong predictors of conversion: a history of adhesions (threefold increased risk), higher BMI, dense pelvic adhesions, excessive intraoperative bleeding, unanticipated large uterine size, and difficult anatomical visualization, with malignant conditions showing significantly higher conversion rates (11%) compared to benign conditions. Conversely, experienced and high-volume surgeons substantially reduce conversion risk, as does adoption of robotic-assisted laparoscopic hysterectomy, which enhances visualization, dexterity, and precision to overcome challenges posed by obesity and adhesions. Notably, endometriosis-related adhesions prominently contribute to conversion risk by obscuring normal anatomic planes, increasing operative time, and elevating inadvertent organ injury risk, making surgeon expertise particularly valuable in managing these complex cases.

title: E-Posters
summary:
# Article Summary

This compilation of spine surgery research presents multiple surgical approaches across different spinal regions, though notably lacks direct endometriosis-specific findings despite the stated cluster theme. Key findings include: distal junctional problems following spinal fusion are significantly associated with smaller postoperative kyphosis parameters, larger thoracic kyphosis changes, and residual vertebral slippage, with risk substantially reduced when the lowest instrumented vertebra is positioned at the fractional lumbar or lower endplate. Cervical disc arthroplasty using the C-ESP prosthesis effectively treats cervical radiculopathy and produces sustained improvements in neck and arm pain, disability indices, and quality-of-life measures through 8-year follow-up. Minimally invasive endoscopy-assisted anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (MOEA-ACDF) reduces operative blood loss compared to traditional ACDF while preserving cervical range of motion, whereas standard ACDF significantly restricts motion. In lumbar pathology, Coflex dynamic stabilization demonstrates long-term pain relief and reduced adjacent segment degeneration compared to posterior lumbar interbody fusion (PLIF), while maintaining surgical segment mobility. Additionally, standardized opioid prescribing protocols effectively reduce postoperative opioid consumption and prolonged use following spinal surgery.

title: Every minute counts: a network meta-analysis comparing the effect of prophylactic endovascular procedures in abnormal placentation
journal: World Journal of Emergency Surgery : WJES
summary:
# Summary

This network meta-analysis evaluates prophylactic endovascular interventions for managing abnormal placentation and preventing postpartum hemorrhage. Placental disorders, particularly placenta previa and placenta accreta spectrum disorders, are major causes of massive postpartum hemorrhage and account for over half of peripartum hysterectomies performed. The study demonstrates that prophylactic endovascular techniques—including resuscitative endovascular balloon occlusion of the aorta (REBOA), uterine artery embolization (UAE), and aortic balloon occlusion—significantly reduce intraoperative blood loss, peripartum hysterectomy rates, blood transfusions, and operative time compared to standard care. REBOA showed particularly strong efficacy, reducing massive transfusion rates, surgery-related complications, intensive care unit admissions, and red blood cell transfusions. Patients with placenta previa who received no prophylactic endovascular procedure faced a fourfold to fivefold increased risk of peripartum hysterectomy, underscoring the protective value of these interventions in abnormal placentation management.

title: A core outcome set of measurement instruments for assessing effectiveness and efficacy of perioperative pain management: results of the international IMI-PainCare PROMPT Delphi consensus process☆
summary:
# Summary

This study presents an international consensus-derived core outcome set (COS) of patient-reported outcome measures (PROMs) designed to standardize the assessment of perioperative pain management effectiveness across multiple surgical types, including endometriosis-related procedures. An international multiprofessional stakeholder panel developed the final COS through a rigorous Delphi process, identifying key measurement domains that reflect the biopsychosocial complexity of pain: pain intensity (with subdomains at rest and during activity), physical function, self-efficacy, and adverse events. The COS emphasizes that effective perioperative pain management prevents patient suffering, chronic postsurgical pain, and long-term opioid use, with pain intensity on average and worst pain intensity serving as primary indicators of postoperative pain. Notably, pain intensity during physiotherapy was excluded from the final COS due to insufficient consensus across surgical procedures, while procedure-specific PROMs were selected for pain during activity and physical function assessment. The study underscores that comprehensive adoption of this standardized COS will harmonize outcome assessment across studies and enable meaningful comparisons in perioperative pain care, though researchers acknowledge that PROMs assessing self-efficacy and adverse events require continued evaluation and potential replacement with higher-quality instruments as they become available.

title: Reducing Peritoneal Cell Dissemination in Laparoscopic Uterine Surgery: A Comparative Pilot Study on Morcellation Techniques and Peritoneal Irrigation
summary:
# Summary

This comparative pilot study evaluates strategies to reduce peritoneal cell dissemination during laparoscopic uterine surgery for benign gynecological conditions including endometriosis, adenomyosis, and uterine fibroids. Power morcellation significantly increases intra-abdominal cell spread (68% of cases), leading to serious complications such as parasitic leiomyomas, iatrogenic endometriosis, and potential leiomyosarcoma dissemination—concerns that prompted the FDA warning against this technique. In contrast, transvaginal cold knife morcellation and en bloc tissue removal resulted in substantially lower peritoneal cell dissemination rates. The study demonstrates that copious peritoneal irrigation with 3000 mL of saline solution effectively reduces residual smooth muscle cells and positive cytologies, providing a simple and safe adjunctive intervention compatible with minimally invasive techniques. Cell dissemination occurred not only during morcellation but also during tissue enucleation itself, indicating that multiple surgical steps contribute to cellular spillage. These findings support incorporating cold knife morcellation techniques and copious irrigation protocols to preserve the benefits of minimally invasive surgery while mitigating the risks of iatrogenic endometriosis and malignant cell spread.

title: Evaluating the Anchorflow Suture Technique Versus Conventional Continuous Suturing in Vaginal Cuff Closure During TLH: A Multicenter Retrospective Analysis
summary:
# Summary

This multicenter retrospective analysis compared the Anchorflow Suture (AFS) technique with conventional continuous suturing for vaginal cuff closure during total laparoscopic hysterectomy. The AFS technique significantly reduced vaginal cuff closure time (10.26 minutes versus 13.36 minutes) and demonstrated improved surgical visualization through midline suture placement and traction, enabling more efficient simultaneous suturing of both cuff layers. Both techniques showed comparable safety profiles with no cases of vaginal cuff dehiscence, although the AFS group experienced slightly lower rates of postoperative bleeding, urinary incontinence, and emergency relaparotomy. The AFS technique provides an additional benefit of bilateral uterosacral ligament suspension, enhancing pelvic support and preventing cuff prolapse while avoiding the use of barbed sutures. These findings suggest that the AFS technique improves surgical efficiency without compromising safety, offering an effective advancement in vaginal cuff closure methodology during minimally invasive hysterectomy.

title: Dynamic key vascular anatomy dataset for D2 lymph node dissection during laparoscopic gastric cancer surgery
summary: This article presents the LapGC-KVAD-30, a dynamic vascular anatomy dataset designed to improve the safety and precision of laparoscopic gastric cancer surgery, particularly during D2 lymph node dissection. The primary challenge addressed is that complex perigastric vascular anatomy creates a 45% probability of vascular injury during laparoscopic procedures, with intraoperative bleeding representing one of the most common complications that leads to poor prognosis. The dataset contains annotated surgical video frames capturing the dynamic exposure, dissection, and ligation of key perigastric vessels, with high annotation consistency demonstrated by Dice coefficients ranging from 0.84 to 0.97 across multiple annotators and expert surgeons. Computer vision and artificial intelligence technologies applied to this dataset can automatically recognize anatomical structures during surgery, thereby reducing vascular injury risk and improving surgical navigation. The LapGC-KVAD-30 aims to serve as a foundational resource for developing image-guided surgical AI navigation systems, enabling surgeons to enhance surgical planning, simulation training, and intraoperative decision-making. Ultimately, this computational approach to standardizing vessel identification addresses the 20% of intraoperative adverse events caused by human visual perception errors, with the potential to optimize clinical outcomes in gastric cancer treatment.

title: MINIMALLY-INVASIVE PAIN MANAGEMENT TECHNIQUES IN CANCER PAIN
summary:
# Summary

This article reviews minimally invasive pain management techniques for cancer patients, with particular emphasis on interventional nerve blocks and neuraxial delivery systems. The superior hypogastric plexus block and celiac plexus block are key techniques that treat visceral pain conditions including endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, and pancreatic cancer pain, with the superior hypogastric plexus block achieving 48.8% pain reduction at six months. Neurolytic agents such as phenol and ethanol denature proteins to induce Wallerian degeneration of nerves, with phenol offering advantages including faster axonal regeneration and lower neuritis risk compared to ethanol. Systemic opioid therapy, which fails in 10-30% of patients due to inadequate control or toxicities, can be supplemented or replaced by adjuvant analgesics like ketamine and clonidine that reduce required opioid dosages, while intrathecal and epidural analgesia using local anesthetics provide prolonged pain control for long-term survivors. Patient factors including diabetes, depression, and fatigue reduce procedural effectiveness, and complications such as diarrhea, paraplegia, and catheter-related issues require careful monitoring and technique selection.

title: Risk factors for the prognostic effects of patients with pelvic abscesses: a clinical retrospective study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective study identifies key prognostic factors for pelvic abscess outcomes, a severe complication of pelvic inflammatory disease. Multivariate analysis revealed that treatment modality, time from symptom onset to admission, pre-admission temperature, CA-125 levels, and maximum abscess diameter significantly predict clinical outcomes. Inflammatory biomarkers including C-reactive protein and procalcitonin associated with treatment failure, while elevated CA-125 predicted extended antibiotic requirements. Laparoscopic approaches demonstrated superior efficacy compared to open surgery, achieving shorter operative times and reduced blood loss, with fewer complications such as ureteral and intestinal injuries. Non-surgical management was reserved for smaller, unilateral abscesses with regular morphology and intact capsules but resulted in prolonged pain resolution, extended antibiotic duration, and longer hospitalization. The study emphasizes that early intervention combined with biomarker-guided decision-making and minimally invasive surgical techniques optimizes prognostic outcomes in pelvic abscess management.

title: From practice to perfection—complications and operative time learning curves in benign robotic-assisted laparoscopic hysterectomy
summary: This study examined the learning curves associated with robotic-assisted laparoscopic hysterectomy (RALH) for benign gynecological conditions including endometriosis, analyzing how surgeon experience affects operative outcomes across 1,276 procedures. Operative time demonstrated a rapid learning curve, achieving a plateau at approximately 50 cases with a stable operative time of 100 minutes, with surgeon experience continuing to reduce operative duration beyond this point. Complication rates showed a steeper learning curve, with significant decline occurring after 150 procedures, including intraoperative injuries (ureter, bladder, bowel) and postoperative infections (urinary tract, vaginal cuff, wound). Intraoperative blood loss remained consistently low and stable regardless of surgeon volume, suggesting this parameter is not experience-dependent. The study found that engagement in more complex cases, such as advanced endometriosis or larger uteri, did not destabilize learning curves, and overall severe complication rates remained below 3 percent. These findings demonstrate that proficiency in RALH for benign indications requires approximately 150 cases to optimize safety outcomes, though technical efficiency plateaus much earlier.

title: Laparoscopic Drainage of Severe Intra-abdominal Abscesses Following Cesarean Section: A Case Report
summary:
# Summary

Cesarean section carries significant risk for postoperative complications including intra-abdominal abscess formation, which may occur through bacterial seeding such as Enterococcus faecalis detected from placental material. Although intravenous antibiotic therapy alone achieves approximately 70% success in treating pelvic abscesses, this case demonstrated that laparoscopic drainage with adhesiolysis and peritoneal lavage provides an effective alternative when image-guided percutaneous drainage fails due to anatomically complex abscess distribution. Laparoscopic management offers distinct advantages over open laparotomy, including reduced postoperative pain, shorter hospital stays, lower intraoperative blood loss, and critically, reduced risk of postoperative adhesions that compromise fertility. The combination of antibiotic therapy targeting identified pathogens (such as ampicillin for Enterococcus faecalis) with minimally invasive laparoscopic intervention represents an optimal treatment strategy for severe multifocal intra-abdominal abscesses, particularly in high-risk patients where abdominal wall damage must be minimized. This case exemplifies how laparoscopic surgery has become increasingly accepted for acute peritoneal complications when technical expertise and appropriate clinical judgment guide surgical decision-making.

title: Risk factors for surgical site infection (SSI) in patients undergoing hysterectomy: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review and meta-analysis identifies multiple independent risk factors for surgical site infections (SSI) following hysterectomy, a common procedure used to treat endometriosis, leiomyoma, and abnormal uterine bleeding. Blood transfusion emerged as the strongest risk factor (OR=2.55), followed by tumor presence (OR=2.23), obesity (OR=1.79), diabetes (OR=1.70), and tobacco use (OR=1.43). The mechanistic pathways underlying these associations are well-characterized: tobacco use causes peripheral vasoconstriction via nicotine and carbon monoxide, reducing tissue oxygen supply and precipitating microthrombi formation that impairs healing; obesity and diabetes impair immune function through metabolic pathways; and malignant tumors compromise immunity both directly and through chemotherapy and radiotherapy. Notably, the hysterectomy approach substantially influences SSI risk, with laparoscopic and vaginal procedures conferring protective effects compared to abdominal hysterectomy. These findings underscore the importance of preoperative risk stratification and suggest that minimally invasive surgical techniques should be preferred when feasible to reduce postoperative infectious complications.

title: Comparison of complications and recovery after laparoscopic and abdominal hysterectomy for benign disease: the LAparoscopic Versus Abdominal hysterectomy (LAVA) randomised controlled trial
summary: The LAVA randomised controlled trial compared laparoscopic and abdominal hysterectomy for benign gynaecological conditions, including endometriosis, abnormal uterine bleeding, and pelvic pain. Major and minor surgical complications occurred significantly more frequently with abdominal hysterectomy, particularly bleeding-related complications including intraoperative haemorrhage and postoperative pelvic haematoma, whereas laparoscopic hysterectomy was associated with specific risks such as thermal ureteral injury and urinary tract injury. Hospital stay was substantially shorter following laparoscopic hysterectomy (median 1.0 night versus 2.0 nights), and readmission rates were lower in the laparoscopic group. Contrary to widespread perception, laparoscopic hysterectomy showed no clinically meaningful advantage in pain scores, quality of recovery at 24 hours, time to resumption of usual activities, or generic health-related quality of life compared to abdominal surgery, though patient satisfaction was higher with laparoscopic approach. The findings demonstrate that while laparoscopic hysterectomy reduces hospital stay and major bleeding complications, the overall recovery trajectory and functional outcomes are comparable between approaches, suggesting that surgical route selection should account for individual risk factors and surgeon expertise rather than assumptions about superior recovery.

title: Fluorescence-guided ureteral identification in robotic surgery for advanced endometriosis: a comparison of junior versus senior surgeons
summary: Advanced endometriosis presents significant surgical challenges, particularly regarding ureteral identification and injury prevention during robot-assisted laparoscopic excision. Deep infiltrating endometriosis frequently involves the ureter and causes severe pelvic adhesions and altered anatomy, complications that are further compounded by the diminished tactile feedback inherent to robotic surgery. This study demonstrates that Indocyanine Green (ICG) fluorescence imaging dramatically enhances real-time ureteral visualization and localization during robotic ureterolysis, enabling safer dissection while reducing operative time and surgeon workload. Notably, ICG-assisted ureteral mapping prevented all iatrogenic ureteral injuries in both junior and senior surgeon groups, with no temporary or permanent injuries occurring despite high ureterolysis rates (88.6% and 79.2%, respectively). The fluorescence-guided approach significantly reduced anxiety among junior surgeons by providing immediate anatomical reference points and improving spatial awareness, thereby shortening the learning curve for complex endometriosis resection. These findings suggest that ICG fluorescence integration into robot-assisted endometriosis surgery substantially elevates surgical safety and precision across experience levels, potentially becoming a standard of care for managing advanced-stage disease.

title: Comparison of Polyglactin 910 With Barbed Sutures for Vaginal Cuff Closure Time in Patients Undergoing Total Laparoscopic Hysterectomy
summary:
# Summary

This study compared barbed sutures with polyglactin 910 sutures for vaginal cuff closure during total laparoscopic hysterectomy, a minimally invasive procedure used to treat endometriosis and other benign uterine conditions. Barbed sutures significantly reduced vault closure time (6.94 versus 11.75 minutes) and overall operative time (102.27 versus 131.10 minutes) compared to traditional polyglactin sutures, primarily through their self-retaining barbed design that eliminates the need for knotting and provides secure tissue approximation. The barbed suture group also demonstrated reduced intraoperative blood loss and lower risk of vaginal cuff dehiscence, while maintaining consistent tensile strength throughout the wound. Notably, both suture types were associated with comparable postoperative pain levels and no vaginal cuff-related complications, indicating that barbed sutures offer improved surgical efficiency without compromising safety or patient outcomes. The anti-reflective black needle of the barbed suture system further enhanced visibility during the laparoscopic procedure, supporting barbed sutures as an advantageous option for intracorporeal suturing in minimally invasive hysterectomy.

title: Ovarian Reserve After Robotic Versus Laparoscopic Single-Site Ovarian Cystectomy for Mature Cystic Teratoma: A Prospective Comparative Study
summary:
# Summary

This prospective comparative study evaluated whether robotic single-site ovarian cystectomy (RO) preserves ovarian reserve better than laparoscopic single-site ovarian cystectomy (LO) in patients undergoing surgery for mature cystic teratoma, using anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) levels as the primary biomarker of ovarian reserve. Both surgical approaches resulted in similar postoperative AMH decline rates (approximately 13.3%), with no statistically significant differences between the two groups, indicating that the surgical method itself did not predict outcomes. Key findings revealed that laparoscopic cystectomy demonstrated shorter operative times despite higher patient BMI in that group, while suturing for hemostasis was associated with lower AMH decline compared to bipolar coagulation, regardless of surgical approach. The cyst type significantly influenced ovarian reserve impact, with mature cystic teratomas showing minimal AMH reduction compared to endometriomas or cystadenomas. Although robotic surgery offers technical advantages including enhanced visualization and instrument dexterity, the study concluded that RO does not provide clear advantages over LO for ovarian function preservation in mature cystic teratoma cases. These findings suggest that hemostasis technique and cyst pathology are more critical determinants of ovarian reserve preservation than the choice between robotic and laparoscopic surgical approaches.

title: Comparative Analysis of Vaginal and Abdominal Uterine Manipulation in Laparoscopic Hysterectomy: The Boztosun Technique
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluated the Boztosun technique, a modified intra-abdominal method of uterine manipulation, as an alternative to conventional vaginal manipulators for laparoscopic hysterectomy in treating benign gynecologic conditions including endometriosis, fibroids, and abnormal uterine bleeding. The Boztosun technique achieved significantly shorter operative times (81.67 versus 109 minutes), reduced colpotomy and manipulator placement times, and shortened hospital stays compared to standard vaginal manipulation approaches. By avoiding transvaginal and intrauterine instrumentation, the technique eliminates associated complications such as vaginal lacerations, loss of pneumoperitoneum, and cervical integrity disruption while enabling controlled multidirectional uterine traction for adequate surgical exposure. The method demonstrates superior cost-effectiveness using inexpensive materials like cotton tape and reusable instruments, making it particularly valuable for resource-limited settings, and appears safe and effective without increasing intraoperative risk.

title: Enhancing Pain Management: The Role of Superior Hypogastric Plexus Block in Chronic Pelvic Pain Treatment
summary:
# Summary

Superior hypogastric plexus block (SHPB) is an interventional pain management procedure that targets the superior hypogastric plexus, a key structure in visceral pain transmission from pelvic organs, to treat chronic pelvic pain associated with endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, and cancer-related pain. The procedure significantly reduces pain scores on validated measures such as the Visual Analogue Scale and Oswestry Disability Index while decreasing opioid analgesic requirements and improving functional outcomes including mobility and physical function. Modern imaging guidance techniques—including fluoroscopy, ultrasound, and CT imaging—enhance procedure safety and efficacy by improving visualization of anatomical structures, with ultrasound-guided approaches offering the additional benefit of real-time needle visualization while reducing radiation exposure. Although SHPB provides substantial therapeutic benefits, potential complications include local injection site effects (infection, bleeding, bruising), temporary neurological symptoms (numbness, weakness), and rarely, more serious complications such as bowel dysfunction, bladder dysfunction, and hypotension. Newer technical approaches such as transdiscal techniques continue to expand access options to the superior hypogastric plexus, further broadening the clinical applicability of this intervention for chronic pelvic pain management.

title: Preoperative anastomotic evaluation prior to ileostomy closure: A 5‐year UK survey, systematic review, and meta‐analysis
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review and UK survey examined preoperative imaging strategies for evaluating anastomotic integrity before ileostomy closure, with particular relevance to complex colorectal and endometrial surgery cases. Water-soluble contrast enema (WCE) and digital rectal examination (DRE) remain the most commonly used evaluation methods among UK surgeons, though endoscopy demonstrates superior sensitivity (73.1% versus 53.1%) for detecting anastomotic leaks. Despite WCE's widespread use, it carries a high false positive rate (21.2-28.9%), while small bowel follow-through showed excellent sensitivity and specificity (86% and 100%) when performed on postoperative day eight. Notably, occult radiological leaks identified on WCE are often inconsequential, with 81.8% proceeding to uneventful stoma closure, suggesting that clinically significant leaks typically become apparent without imaging. The evidence indicates that while endoscopic evaluation should be considered for preoperative assessment, WCE's clinical benefit for guiding management decisions remains questionable, and a multimodal approach incorporating DRE, endoscopy, and selective advanced imaging based on clinical suspicion may optimize patient outcomes while reducing unnecessary testing burden.

title: A machine learning-based predictive model for the occurrence of lower extremity deep vein thrombosis after laparoscopic surgery in abdominal surgery
summary:
# Summary

Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) is a common complication occurring in approximately 9.9% of patients following laparoscopic abdominal surgery, with potential to progress to life-threatening pulmonary embolism or chronic sequelae such as post-thrombotic syndrome and venous ulceration. Multiple clinical and laboratory factors significantly associate with DVT development, including advanced age, prior tumor history, prolonged surgical duration, and specific hematologic parameters such as hemoglobin levels, D-dimer, and activated partial thromboplastin time. A machine learning-based predictive model (LASDVT) was developed to identify DVT risk, integrating tumor history, patient age, surgical duration, and D-dimer levels as the strongest predictive features, while demonstrating superior performance compared to traditional scoring systems like the Caprini and Padua scores. The LASDVT model achieved an area under the receiver operating curve of 0.9476 and importantly identified different feature sets for predicting versus excluding DVT occurrence. These findings underscore the clinical value of machine learning approaches in personalizing thromboembolism risk stratification for laparoscopic surgery patients, potentially enabling more targeted preventive strategies to mitigate this significant postoperative complication.

title: Trends of Minimally Invasive Hysterectomy: Five Years of Experience
summary:
# Minimally Invasive Hysterectomy: Clinical Outcomes and Safety Profile

This five-year retrospective study evaluated trends in minimally invasive hysterectomy procedures, with abnormal uterine bleeding (30.1%), pelvic organ prolapse (20.8%), and uterine leiomyoma as the primary indications for surgery. Among the minimally invasive approaches, laparoscopic subtotal hysterectomy was most common (48.7%), with a trend toward increased total laparoscopic hysterectomy over the study period. Estimated blood loss emerged as the only significant independent predictor of both intraoperative and postoperative complications, with intraoperative complications occurring in 7.2% of cases (including rare bowel and ureteric injuries) and postoperative complications including febrile morbidity (1.1%) and bowel obstruction (0.4%). Minimally invasive hysterectomy demonstrated substantial clinical benefits, including shorter hospital stays and reduced postoperative pain compared to open procedures, with conversion to laparotomy required in only 2.5% of cases. Surgeon experience level and estimated blood loss were identified as key predictors of successful laparoscopic hysterectomy, suggesting that outcomes may be optimized through increased procedural volume and refined surgical technique to minimize intraoperative blood loss.

title: The feasibility and surgical outcomes of robotic vaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic single port hysterectomy for benign gynecologic diseases: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary: Robotic-assisted vaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery (R-vNOTES) represents a minimally invasive hysterectomy approach that demonstrates superior operative efficiency and safety outcomes compared to traditional robotic-assisted laparoscopic hysterectomy. Meta-analysis findings show that R-vNOTES significantly reduces operative time and blood loss while achieving lower hemoglobin decrease (approximately 1.4 grams) and shorter hospital stays (2.04 days on average). The procedure effectively treats multiple benign gynecologic conditions including fibroids, adenomyosis, abnormal bleeding, prolapse, and endometrial hyperplasia, with a low conversion rate of 1.3% and complication rate of 13.3%. Enhanced three-dimensional visualization from robotic assistance, combined with the single-port vaginal approach's inherent advantages of reduced instrument crowding, hematoma formation, and infection risk, contributes to improved postoperative outcomes including reduced pain and enhanced quality of life. These findings establish R-vNOTES as a feasible and advantageous minimally invasive surgical option for benign gynecologic disease management.

title: Rhabdomyosarcoma Requiring Ovarian Transposition Release for Recurrent Severe Ovulation Pain Following Laparoscopic Ovarian Transposition: A Case Report
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a pediatric patient with rhabdomyosarcoma who underwent laparoscopic ovarian transposition to preserve ovarian function prior to pelvic proton therapy, subsequently treated with combination chemotherapy including vincristine, cyclophosphamide, and actinomycin-D. While ovarian transposition successfully preserved ovarian function in most patients (91-93%), this case highlights a significant complication: severe, recurrent ovulation-related pain caused by peritoneal irritation from an enlarged corpus luteum in the transposed ovary, necessitating ovarian repositioning. The chronic pain and associated complications following transposition—including ovarian torsion, benign cysts, and peritoneal adhesions—underscore the importance of recognizing ovulation-related morbidity in adolescent patients with prior transposition and pelvic radiation. Novel techniques such as medial transposition approaches and ovarian tunnel preservation of blood supply may improve outcomes and reduce postoperative complications. Long-term monitoring of ovarian function using FSH, anti-Müllerian hormone, and imaging is essential to evaluate fertility preservation and manage late complications in this at-risk population.

title: Minimally Invasive Gynecologic Surgery and Enhanced Recovery and Outcomes: A Literature Review
summary:
# Summary

This literature review demonstrates that Enhanced Recovery After Surgery (ERAS) protocols significantly improve outcomes in minimally invasive gynecologic procedures, particularly laparoscopic hysterectomy for benign and malignant conditions including endometriosis, uterine fibroids, and adenomyosis. ERAS implementation reduces hospital length of stay by 1.6 to 3 days, decreases opioid requirements, lowers pain scores, promotes faster bowel function recovery and early ambulation, and reduces readmission rates and postoperative complications. The effectiveness of ERAS depends on multimodal non-opioid analgesia, preoperative counseling with carbohydrate loading, fluid control, minimally invasive surgical techniques, and early mobilization protocols. Additionally, technological advances including artificial intelligence, 3D imaging, and robotic-assisted hysterectomy further enhance surgical precision and patient outcomes while improving financial efficiency and resource utilization. These findings support establishing ERAS as standard practice in minimally invasive gynecologic surgery, with particular benefits demonstrated in elderly and pediatric populations.

title: Advances and Challenges in Minimally Invasive Myomectomy: A Narrative Review
summary: Uterine fibroids are highly prevalent benign tumors affecting 70-80% of reproductive-age women that cause significant clinical morbidity through multiple mechanisms. These fibroids induce abnormal uterine bleeding, bulk symptoms (pelvic pressure, urinary frequency, constipation), pelvic pain, and adverse reproductive outcomes by disrupting myometrial motility, fallopian tube function, endometrial inflammatory state, and local growth factor regulation. Myomectomy (fibroid removal) effectively improves pregnancy rates, live birth rates, and quality of life while preserving fertility, with minimally invasive approaches—particularly laparoscopic and robotic techniques—offering substantial advantages over open surgery including reduced operative complications, blood transfusion requirements, hospital stay duration, and faster return to work. Robotic-assisted myomectomy provides superior hemostatic control and shorter learning curves for complex cases, while laparoscopic myomectomy remains preferred for larger fibroid burdens and demonstrates lower overall costs. The choice between approaches depends on fibroid size and burden, surgeon expertise, and patient factors, though all minimally invasive techniques represent safer, cost-effective alternatives to hysterectomy for fibroid management in women desiring fertility preservation.

title: Vaginal Natural Orifice Transluminal Endoscopic Surgery (vNOTES) in Risk-Reducing Gynecologic Cancer Surgery: A New Frontier in Hereditary Cancer Prevention
summary:
# Summary

Women carrying mutations in BRCA1, BRCA2, or mismatch repair genes associated with Lynch syndrome face substantially elevated lifetime risks of ovarian and endometrial cancers, necessitating risk-reducing surgical interventions. Vaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery (vNOTES) has emerged as an innovative minimally invasive approach for prophylactic procedures such as risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy and hysterectomy in these high-risk populations, offering superior cosmetic outcomes, reduced postoperative pain, shorter hospital stays, and lower blood loss compared to conventional approaches while maintaining oncologic safety through specimen containment and adherence to the SEE-FIM protocol for detecting early precancerous lesions. Multiple case series and comparative studies demonstrate the feasibility and safety of vNOTES across BRCA mutation carriers and Lynch syndrome patients, with low complication rates (4.3–5.2%) and high patient satisfaction, though technical limitations including restricted upper abdominal access and a learning curve of 20–30 cases currently restrict its broader adoption. The procedure significantly reduces cancer risks—with risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy reducing ovarian cancer risk by over 80%—while enabling same-day discharge and faster recovery, positioning vNOTES as a promising patient-centered surgical frontier in hereditary cancer prevention strategies.

title: Comparison of Quadratus Lumborum Block and Rectus Sheath Block for Postoperative Analgesia in Single-Port Laparoscopic Adnexal Surgery: A Randomized Controlled Trial
summary:
# Summary

This randomized controlled trial compared rectus sheath block (RSB) and quadratus lumborum block (QLB) for postoperative pain management in single-port laparoscopic adnexal surgery. Both techniques provide somatic analgesia by blocking different sets of thoracolumbar nerves, with QLB additionally offering potential visceral pain modulation through spread to the thoracic paravertebral space. However, RSB demonstrated superior opioid-sparing effects, significantly reducing cumulative opioid consumption at 24 hours postoperatively and extending the time to first patient-controlled analgesia bolus compared to QLB. Pain severity scores remained comparable between groups at all postoperative timepoints, suggesting that RSB's more concentrated and immediate coverage of the anterior abdominal wall—particularly the periumbilical region innervated by T9–T11 nerves—provides sufficient analgesia for adnexal procedures that involve limited visceral manipulation. These findings suggest that RSB may be the preferred regional anesthesia technique for single-port laparoscopic adnexal surgery, as the robust somatic pain control from skin incisions outweighs the theoretical visceral analgesic advantages of QLB in this surgical setting.

title: Feasibility of Laparoscopic Removal of the Largest Documented Uterine Fibroid Without Morcellation
summary: Uterine fibroids, which affect approximately 25% of women of reproductive age, commonly cause lower abdominal pain, bladder pressure, anemia, and infertility. While intracavitary fibroids are treated with hysteroscopic myomectomy as first-line therapy, intramural and subserosal fibroids require surgical excision; uterine artery embolization for intramural fibroids is associated with lower pregnancy rates and higher miscarriage rates compared to myomectomy. This case demonstrates successful laparoscopic myomectomy of an exceptionally large fibroid using manual tissue fragmentation without power morcellation, a technique that avoids serious complications including bowel, bladder, and vascular injuries, as well as the risk of disseminating occult leiomyosarcoma or parasitic leiomyomas. Laparoscopic myomectomy preserves fertility with postoperative pregnancy rates exceeding 60% and offers significant advantages over open surgery, including reduced postoperative pain, faster recovery, improved quality of life, and enhanced sexual function. The strategic trocar placement and omission of a uterine manipulator optimized visualization and reduced perforation risk during en bloc fibroid dissection. This case establishes that careful laparoscopic techniques can safely manage even the largest fibroids while eliminating morcellation-related oncological and iatrogenic risks.

title: Comparative Analysis of 30-Day Complications in Transgender Men Undergoing Different Routes of Hysterectomy: A Study Utilizing American College of Surgeons National Surgical Quality Improvement Program (ACS-NSQIP) Data
summary: This comparative analysis examines 30-day surgical complications across different hysterectomy approaches in transgender men, utilizing national surgical quality data. Laparoscopic hysterectomy demonstrated the lowest overall complication rates and was most commonly performed as an outpatient procedure, while abdominal hysterectomy was associated with significantly higher risks of bleeding requiring transfusion, longer hospital stays, and higher rates of wound-related complications, though it remains indicated for cases with extensive pelvic pathology such as endometriosis and large uterine masses. Laparoscopic-assisted vaginal hysterectomy showed a notably elevated incidence of unplanned intubation compared to other approaches, even after adjusting for baseline comorbidities. Hysterectomy substantially improves quality of life and reduces psychological distress in transgender men by alleviating gender dysphoria, and the study found that being transgender was not independently associated with post-operative complications.
