Patient factors including body mass index and American Society of Anesthesiologists classification were important determinants of surgical route selection and outcomes. Overall, these findings support minimally invasive approaches as safer alternatives when anatomically feasible, while acknowledging that abdominal routes remain necessary for complex pathology.

title: Appropriateness of Hysterectomy as Treatment for Benign Gynecological Conditions
summary: This study examined the appropriateness of hysterectomy as a treatment for benign gynecological conditions, including endometriosis, between 2014 and 2017. The research found that missing documentation of diagnostic procedures was the most common factor associated with inappropriate hysterectomy ratings, occurring in 46.1% of cases evaluated by RAND methodology. The authors identified that diagnostic imaging requirements in current appropriateness guidelines have become outdated, as laparoscopic procedures—traditionally used for diagnostic confirmation—have significantly decreased in clinical practice. These findings suggest that appropriateness criteria for hysterectomy in benign conditions may not adequately reflect modern diagnostic practices and that improved documentation of diagnostic workup is critical for accurate assessment of surgical necessity. The study highlights a gap between hysterectomy indication guidelines and contemporary diagnostic approaches in gynecology.

title: The learning curve of V-NOTES hysterectomy: a single-surgeon experience
summary: This study documents a four-phase learning curve for vaginally-assisted hysterectomy (V-NOTES), identifying initial competency after 12 cases with progressive phases through consolidation (cases 13-53), complexity (cases 54-107), and mastery (cases 108-139). Compared to traditional laparoscopic hysterectomy, V-NOTES demonstrates non-inferiority regarding conversion rates, postoperative infections, blood transfusions, complications, and postoperative pain. The primary perioperative risk is bladder injury during anterior colpotomy, particularly in patients with prior cesarean sections and no history of vaginal delivery, with potential downstream lower urinary tract infections. Important contraindications include rectovaginal endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, prior rectal surgery, and pelvic radiation therapy. Operative time was longest during the initiation and complexity phases and potentially increased with trainee involvement, though overall complication rates remained low at 5.2% across 750 procedures. This evidence supports V-NOTES as a viable minimally invasive approach for hysterectomy in appropriately selected endometriosis patients once the surgeon completes the learning curve.

title: Robotic multidisciplinary endometriosis surgery with multi‐visceral resection: evaluation of short‐term feasibility and safety outcomes
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory condition where endometrial-like tissue develops at extrauterine sites, with 5-12% of patients developing deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIE) involving the gastrointestinal tract and genitourinary organs including the rectum, rectosigmoid, bladder, and ureter. This study demonstrates that multidisciplinary robotic surgery achieves short-term feasibility and safety for complex endometriosis management, with three-dimensional visualization improving lesion detection 2.36-fold compared to conventional approaches. The robotic platform enabled complete resection of multiorgan disease through diverse procedures including rectal resection (75.9% of colorectal cases), partial cystectomy, ureteral reimplantation, and hysterectomy with minimal morbidity—achieving zero conversions to open surgery, median blood loss of 100 mL, three-day hospital stays, and no anastomotic leaks or major infectious complications. Surgeon ergonomic benefits included reduced physical burden and fatigue during prolonged complex procedures, while emerging technologies such as near-infrared fluorescence imaging with indocyanine green and force feedback features promise further enhancement of endometriosis detection and outcomes. Only 6.9% of patients experienced minor postoperative complications within 30 days, confirming that robotic-assisted multidisciplinary surgery safely treats advanced endometriosis with expedited recovery compared to conventional laparoscopic approaches.

title: Pre-operative GnRH agonist use and surgical outcomes in rectovaginal/colorectal endometriosis: an international multicentre prospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This international multicentre prospective cohort study examined pre-operative GnRH agonist (GnRHa) use in patients undergoing surgery for rectovaginal/colorectal endometriosis, a severe form of deep endometriosis characterized by pelvic pain, dyspareunia, dysmenorrhea, and dyschezia. Although pre-operative GnRHa was intended to reduce inflammation, vascularization, and adhesions to facilitate complex bowel surgery, patients receiving GnRHa experienced significantly higher rates of perioperative and late complications, including pelvic hematoma, abscess formation, colostomy, and ileostomy. The increased complication risk was attributed to GnRHa-induced vaginal atrophy, which impaired wound healing and increased vaginal cuff dehiscence, particularly following extensive electrocautery that risked posterior vaginal cuff necrosis and subsequent rectovaginal fistula formation. Notably, GnRHa use did not significantly affect rates of major organ injury, urinary or bowel leakage, fistula formation, or conversion to open surgery. Despite the heightened surgical risks, pre-operative GnRHa improved health-related quality of life at twelve and twenty-four months post-operatively, suggesting a complex risk-benefit profile for this treatment approach in advanced rectovaginal endometriosis management.

title: Safety with the senhance™ robotic system in 3,239 patients across various surgical disciplines
summary: This comprehensive analysis of 3,239 patients demonstrates that the Senhance™ robotic system is a safe and effective tool across gastrointestinal, gynaecological, and urological procedures, offering enhanced surgical precision, improved dexterity, and reduced postoperative pain through advanced features including 3-D visualization, eye-tracking camera control, and tactile feedback. The system maintained a low conversion rate of 4.5%, with robotic malfunctions occurring in only 3% of cases and most technical limitations, such as limited motion (15.6%) and collisions (6.2%), being resolvable intraoperatively without necessitating conversion. Although adverse events were documented in 3.9% of cases, the majority were mild in severity and unrelated to the robotic system itself, with serious adverse events occurring in only 1.4% of overall procedures and being more common in gastrointestinal surgery (1.7%) than gynaecological (0.8%) or urological procedures (0.4%). Patient factors including elevated BMI and previous abdominal surgery with adhesions contributed to surgical challenges and conversions, while most serious complications such as organ perforations and hemorrhage were attributable to patient-specific conditions rather than robotic system failure. These findings underscore the Senhance™ system's clinical utility and safety profile for minimally invasive surgery across multiple disciplines, with its intuitive design and technical reliability supporting both patient and surgeon safety outcomes.

title: Optimizing ERAS protocols in robotic nephron-sparing surgery: a randomized trial
summary: This randomized trial demonstrates that Enhanced Recovery After Surgery (ERAS) nursing protocols significantly improve perioperative outcomes in robotic nephron-sparing surgery for renal cancer. The ERAS group achieved substantial reductions across multiple metrics, including operative time, intraoperative blood loss (32.2 mL reduction), hospital stay (4.3 days shorter), and overall complication rates (14.2% versus 39.2% in controls), with particularly notable decreases in urinary infections and wound hematomas. ERAS implementation also accelerated functional recovery milestones, including earlier ambulation (14.3 versus 18.7 hours) and faster catheter removal (2.1 versus 3.5 days), while improving patient comfort scores post-intervention. These benefits were achieved through evidence-based perioperative care strategies that reduce surgical stress and promote early mobilization, complementing the advantages of robotic-assisted partial nephrectomy—including enhanced visualization and precision—and selective arterial clamping techniques for optimal renal function preservation. The findings support ERAS protocols as a valuable adjunct to minimally invasive nephron-sparing surgery, offering patients both improved immediate recovery and protection against chronic kidney disease progression.

title: Comparison of transvaginal or transumbilical tissue extraction at laparoscopic gynecologic surgery: A 12‐year experience
journal: International Journal of Gynaecology and Obstetrics
summary: This 12-year comparative study evaluated tissue extraction routes during laparoscopic gynecologic surgery, specifically comparing transvaginal and transumbilical approaches for specimen removal in patients undergoing procedures including hysterectomy for endometriosis-related conditions. The transvaginal route demonstrated superior outcomes across multiple parameters, including significantly lower postoperative pain scores at 1, 6, and 24 hours post-surgery, higher cosmetic satisfaction scores at 3 months, and reduced requirements for additional analgesic agents compared to the transumbilical approach. Conversely, transumbilical extraction was associated with increased postoperative pain and lower cosmetic outcomes, with factors such as myomectomy and umbilical incision extension independently predicting worse pain control. The study also highlighted important safety considerations, including the use of containment bags during specimen removal to prevent spread of malignant cells and the application of temporary bilateral uterine artery clips to minimize blood loss during myomectomy. While transvaginal extraction offered clear advantages in postoperative recovery and patient satisfaction, the authors noted that careful patient selection and technique refinement remain essential for optimizing outcomes in laparoscopic gynecologic procedures.

title: Gynecology residents' preparedness to perform standard gynecological procedures autonomously: A national survey among French residents and faculty
journal: International Journal of Gynaecology and Obstetrics
summary:
# Summary

This French national survey of gynecology residents and faculty assessed surgical preparedness for standard gynecological procedures, with particular attention to hysterectomy approaches including laparoscopic techniques relevant to complex cases like endometriosis. Residents demonstrate strong early autonomy in operative hysteroscopy and laparoscopic adnexal surgery, with over 90% perceived as prepared by graduation, while laparoscopic hysterectomy shows the highest preparedness among hysterectomy approaches at 67% perceived competence by graduation. However, significant preparedness gaps exist for vaginal and open hysterectomy approaches, with only 60% perceived readiness for laparotomy-based procedures, reflecting a documented decline in traditional surgical routes across multiple countries. The study identified case volume and coaching quality as the two essential factors for achieving surgical autonomy, with both residents and faculty emphasizing these over formal summative assessment. The authors advocate for competency-based training models incorporating simulation and validated assessment tools like the Zwisch scale and SIMPL tool to standardize surgical skill preparation and establish consensus on core competencies required for all graduating gynecologists.

title: Transvaginal Repair of Supratrigonal, Juxtacervical, Vault, and Apical Vesicovaginal Fistulae: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review and meta-analysis evaluates the efficacy and safety of transvaginal repair for various types of vesicovaginal fistulae (VVF), abnormal communications between the bladder and vagina caused primarily by gynecological surgeries, particularly hysterectomy, and by obstructed labor in developing countries. Transvaginal repair successfully treats VVF and significantly improves quality of life by restoring continence and enabling early ambulation and resumed sexual activity without discomfort. The procedure is associated with favorable outcomes when fistulae are accessible through examination under anesthesia with mobile vaginal walls and pelvic floor relaxation, and avoids major intraoperative complications such as bowel injury and additional abdominal wounds. Postoperative complications include urinary tract infections, stress incontinence, and urge incontinence, with success rates influenced by fistula location, etiology, and previous repair history. The vaginal approach offers significant advantages over abdominal approaches, including shorter hospital stays, faster recovery, cost-effectiveness, minimal learning curve for techniques like the Latzko procedure, and no need for blood transfusions, making it the preferred first-line treatment for most supratrigonal, juxtacervical, vault, and apical fistulae.

title: A Review of Local Consent Practices for Colorectal Surgery With a Focus on Postoperative Bowel Dysfunction
summary:
# Summary

Colorectal surgery, including anterior resection procedures for conditions such as severe bowel endometriosis, frequently results in anterior resection syndrome (ARS), a constellation of postoperative bowel dysfunctions characterized by frequent stool passage, incomplete evacuation, continence disorders, and urgency. Multiple risk factors contribute to ARS development, including low tumor or anastomosis height, perioperative chemotherapy or radiotherapy, anastomotic leaks, and stoma dysfunction, with anastomotic leaks specifically associated with a twofold increase in long-term fecal incontinence requiring continence aids. Beyond bowel dysfunction itself, colorectal surgery carries significant morbidity including acute and chronic pain (affecting up to 11.8% of patients at 12 months), infections, bleeding, venous thromboembolism, ileus, hernia formation, and sexual dysfunction, with complications such as anastomotic leaks and ileus prolonging hospital stay. The resulting bowel and sexual dysfunction substantially impacts patients' quality of life by disrupting social activities, sexual function, and sleep patterns. Comprehensive informed consent discussions should address the high incidence and long-term consequences of these postoperative complications to ensure patients understand the functional outcomes beyond immediate surgical success.

title: Dual-port laparoscopic myomectomy: a balanced yet potentially more optimal surgical approach
summary:
# Summary

Uterine fibroids commonly cause heavy menstrual bleeding, pelvic pain, urinary frequency, and infertility, which laparoscopic myomectomy effectively treats while reducing postoperative pain. Single-incision laparoscopic surgery presents significant technical challenges including restricted instrument triangulation, limited maneuverability, and increased operative time and blood loss. Dual-port laparoscopic myomectomy overcomes these limitations by enabling proper instrument triangulation and improved surgical ergonomics, resulting in significantly lower intraoperative bleeding, reduced postoperative analgesic use, and shorter hospital stays compared to single-incision approaches. Fibroid size, posterior wall location, and increased operative time independently predicted greater bleeding and prolonged recovery, though these complications were mitigated by the dual-port configuration and surgeon experience. The dual-port technique thus represents an optimal surgical balance, maintaining excellent cosmetic outcomes while substantially improving hemostatic control and patient recovery profiles in myomectomy procedures.

title: Long‐term gastrointestinal function outcomes of women undergoing nerve‐vessel sparing segmental or full‐thickness discoid resection for deep colorectal endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Colorectal deep endometriosis affects reproductive-aged women and is strongly associated with severe pain, impaired fertility, and compromised gastrointestinal function, including lower anterior resection syndrome (LARS)-like symptoms. Both nerve-vessel-sparing segmental resection (NVSSR) and full-thickness discoid resection (FTDR) are effective surgical approaches for treating this condition, with long-term outcomes showing sustained improvements in gastrointestinal quality of life as measured by the gastrointestinal quality of life index (GIQLI). NVSSR demonstrates stable gastrointestinal function at long-term follow-up with significant reductions in LARS scores, while FTDR achieves permanently increased GIQLI scores despite some patients showing increased LARS scores postoperatively, suggesting that quality of life and objective bowel dysfunction measures may not always correlate. These findings indicate that surgical excision of colorectal endometriosis provides sustained positive clinical benefits for patients, though the relationship between specific gastrointestinal symptoms and quality of life remains complex. GIQLI is recommended as the preferred outcome measurement for assessing gastrointestinal function in endometriosis patients undergoing colorectal surgery.

title: Telehealth vs Clinic Postoperative Visit After Hysterectomy: A Randomized Controlled Trial
summary:
# Summary

This randomized controlled trial compared telehealth versus in-person clinic visits for postoperative monitoring after minimally invasive hysterectomy, including cases with concurrent endometriosis excision. Patient satisfaction was comparable between groups (94.3% clinic vs. 92.0% telehealth), and postoperative complication rates, unplanned clinic visits, and emergency department utilization did not differ significantly between the two delivery methods. Telehealth visits provided substantial convenience benefits and improved access to care while maintaining safety and effectiveness, as evidenced by the low overall complication rates in both groups, with all complications being minor (Clavien-Dindo grade 1-2). While the clinic group had more frequent contact with providers overall, telehealth patients reported high satisfaction with convenience and likelihood of future use, though some patients expressed preference for in-person visits when they had specific clinical concerns. These findings suggest that telehealth represents a safe and effective alternative for routine postoperative monitoring after minimally invasive hysterectomy, potentially reducing patient burden while maintaining quality of care.

title: A Stepwise Anatomy-Based Protocol for Total Laparoscopic Hysterectomy: Educational Tool with Broad Clinical Utility
summary:
# Summary

This article presents a standardized anatomy-based protocol for total laparoscopic hysterectomy (TLH), demonstrating that procedural standardization significantly improves patient safety by reducing operative time, hospital stay, and perioperative complications. A key technical innovation is early ligation of the uterine artery at its origin from the internal iliac artery, which minimizes intraoperative bleeding and shortens operative time and hospital stay. The protocol emphasizes anatomical safety measures including broad ligament fenestration to prevent ureteral injury and systematic dissection of retroperitoneal spaces to identify critical vascular structures before tissue transection. While TLH effectively treats benign gynecologic conditions including stage I-II endometriosis, abnormal uterine bleeding, and uterine fibroids with reduced postoperative pain, the procedure carries low but documented complication rates including bladder injury (1.8%), vaginal cuff dehiscence (1.8%), and postoperative bleeding (3.7%). The standardized approach emphasizing careful anatomical dissection, appropriate use of energy devices, and meticulous vaginal cuff closure with figure-of-eight suturing technique provides an effective educational framework for safe TLH performance across diverse clinical settings.

title: An Evaluation of Polish Women’s Knowledge of Perineal Incision: A Cross-Sectional Study
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study evaluated Polish women's knowledge of perineal incision procedures and their protective methods. The research identified that mediolateral episiotomy reduces the risk of severe perineal trauma compared to midline incision, with episiotomy indicated specifically for life-threatening fetal conditions, shoulder dystocia, operative delivery, and fetal macrosomia. Non-surgical interventions including warm compresses and hands-off delivery techniques significantly reduce perineal injuries, pain, and the need for incision. Knowledge disparities were substantial, with women over 26 years old, those with higher education, those with childbirth experience, and those accessing information through medical personnel and internet sources demonstrating significantly greater understanding of perineal incision indications, consent requirements, protection methods, and recovery implications. Conversely, younger women (≤25 years) and those in smaller cities showed knowledge gaps. The study emphasizes that informed consent is critical for preventing obstetric violence, as performing perineal incision without patient consent or explanation can cause traumatic childbirth memories and represents a violation of reproductive autonomy.

title: Minilaparoscopic Versus Conventional Laparoscopic Hysterectomy: Insights from a Single-Center Retrospective Cohort Study with Legal Considerations
summary: This retrospective cohort study compared minilaparoscopic hysterectomy (MLH) with conventional laparoscopic hysterectomy (LH) for treating endometriosis and other gynecological conditions. Both techniques effectively treated endometriosis, but MLH demonstrated superior perioperative outcomes, including significantly shorter operative time (98.8 versus 105.5 minutes), reduced intraoperative blood loss (100.3 versus 195.1 mL), and shorter hospital stays (3.2 versus 4.0 days). The minilaparoscopic approach, utilizing 3 mm trocars and specially developed 3 mm bipolar instruments, produced additional patient benefits including reduced postoperative pain, smaller surgical scars, improved cosmetic satisfaction, and lower risks of incisional hernias and infection compared to conventional laparoscopy. However, dense pelvic adhesions and large or fixed uteri occasionally necessitated conversion to conventional laparoscopic technique in 12 cases. Overall, this study establishes minilaparoscopic hysterectomy as an effective and functionally superior alternative for endometriosis treatment, offering meaningful advantages in surgical efficiency and patient recovery.

title: Hymen-Sparing Myomectomy: Innovative Laparoscopic Approach for Prolapsed Pedunculated Cervicovaginal Leiomyoma in a Virgin Case
summary:
# Summary

Uterine leiomyomas, particularly pedunculated submucosal variants, cause multiple clinical complications including abnormal uterine bleeding, pelvic pain, and when prolapsed, risk serious sequelae such as necrosis, sepsis, and urinary retention. This case report presents a novel Laparoscopic-And-Suprapubic Hysteroscopic Approach (LASHA) for removing a prolapsed pedunculated cervicovaginal leiomyoma while preserving hymenal integrity in a virgin patient, combining the safety benefits of vaginal myomectomy with laparoscopic visualization. The procedure employed multiple hemostatic strategies including vasopressin injection, bipolar electrocautery, and barbed sutures to minimize blood loss while avoiding traditional complications associated with hysteroscopic myomectomy, such as uterine perforation and excessive fluid absorption. Minimally invasive myomectomy approaches, including this innovative technique, have become increasingly prevalent and reduce postoperative morbidity, pain, and blood loss compared to conventional open procedures. The LASHA approach demonstrates that fertility-preserving, hymen-sparing treatment is feasible for complex prolapsed leiomyomas through careful surgical planning and integration of laparoscopic and hysteroscopic techniques.

title: Efficacy, Quality of Life, and Cost‐Effectiveness of Superselective Adrenal Arterial Embolization in Idiopathic Hyperaldosteronism: A Comparative Study
summary:
# Summary

Primary aldosteronism, characterized by autonomous aldosterone secretion and suppression of plasma renin, is a significant cause of secondary hypertension linked to multiple cardiovascular, cerebrovascular, renal, and metabolic complications. This study compared superselective adrenal arterial embolization (SAAE) to mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists (MRAs) for treating idiopathic hyperaldosteronism, the most common subtype of primary aldosteronism. SAAE demonstrated superior efficacy, achieving greater reductions in systolic and diastolic blood pressure (27.4 and 23.1 mmHg respectively) compared to MRAs, while also normalizing plasma aldosterone levels, increasing plasma renin levels, and improving serum potassium and the aldosterone-to-renin ratio. The SAAE group also showed significant reductions in inflammatory markers (CRP and IL-6) and improved quality of life metrics across physical, mental, and overall health domains, with none developing adrenal insufficiency despite long-term treatment. A Random Forest model identified baseline aldosterone levels, CRP, and hypertension duration as key predictors of clinical success, while SAAE proved cost-effective at $6,200 per patient with lower costs over five years, offering a promising minimally invasive alternative to MRA therapy for bilateral idiopathic hyperaldosteronism.

title: Laparoscopic Myomectomy and Pregnancy Outcomes: A Narrative Review
summary:
# Summary

Uterine fibroids are common benign tumors in reproductive-age women that cause infertility, heavy menstrual bleeding, and pelvic discomfort, with submucosal and intramural types particularly affecting fertility. Laparoscopic myomectomy (LSM) effectively treats fibroids and improves pregnancy outcomes, with postoperative pregnancy rates ranging from 44% to 62% and high live birth rates across multiple studies. The procedure offers significant perioperative advantages including reduced blood loss, shorter hospital stays, reduced postoperative pain, and minimal adhesion formation, which preserves uterine and tubal function critical for conception. Proper multilayer uterine closure techniques and patient selection based on fibroid characteristics are essential for favorable reproductive outcomes and low uterine rupture risk, with vaginal delivery safe in appropriately selected cases with superficial fibroids and single incisions. LSM achieves reproductive outcomes comparable to open myomectomy while providing a superior perioperative profile, making it a safe, effective, and fertility-preserving surgical option for symptomatic fibroids in women seeking to conceive. Delivery mode decisions should be individualized based on fibroid size, location, and interpregnancy interval to optimize maternal and fetal outcomes.

title: Impact of uterine weight on surgical outcomes in robotic hysterectomy: An ambispective analysis
summary: Robotic hysterectomy effectively treats common benign gynecological conditions including abnormal uterine bleeding, leiomyoma, and adenomyosis, with the procedure offering advantages of three-dimensional visualization and improved ergonomics over traditional approaches. Uterine weight emerged as a significant predictor of surgical outcomes, with heavier uteri (>500 g) associated with substantially longer console times, increased total operative time, greater hemoglobin drop, higher transfusion requirements, and prolonged hospital stays compared to lighter uteri (<250 g). While body mass index did not correlate with operative time, larger uterine size and myomatous pathology increased manipulation difficulty and extraction time through in-bag morcellation or coring procedures. Console time was identified as the primary driver of increased operative time in cases with heavier uteri, as docking time remained largely independent of patient factors. Despite these associations, intraoperative and postoperative complication rates remained comparable across weight groups, and quality of life improvements were consistent across all patient categories. These findings suggest that while uterine weight significantly influences procedural efficiency metrics and perioperative blood management, robotic hysterectomy maintains safety and efficacy across varying uterine sizes.

title: How to Deal with Bladder Stones Associated with Exposed Intravesical Mesh
summary: Transvaginal mesh surgery for pelvic organ prolapse can cause mesh exposure inside the bladder, leading to bladder stone formation and urinary symptoms including frequency, urgency, dysuria, hematuria, and recurrent urinary tract infections. While mesh-augmented procedures effectively reduce prolapse recurrence compared to native tissue repair, management of intravesical mesh exposure and associated bladder stones requires careful surgical planning. Laparoscopic resection of exposed mesh provides superior outcomes compared to transurethral resection, offering excellent visualization of the surgical plane and enabling layer-to-layer suturing of bladder urothelium to promote proper healing and prevent stone recurrence. Transurethral approaches, though less invasive, are associated with high recurrence rates of bladder stones, whereas laparoscopic removal achieves durable results with no recurrence or need for reoperation in reported cases. The key to success involves complete resection of the exposed intravesical mesh portion while preserving remaining mesh to maintain pelvic support, combined with meticulous bladder reconstruction to ensure complete mucosal coverage and optimal wound healing.

title: Comparison of Single-port Robotic Surgery Using the Da Vinci SP Surgical System and Single-port Laparoscopic Surgery for Benign Indications
summary: This study compared single-port robot-assisted total hysterectomy (SP-RAH) using the da Vinci SP system with conventional single-port laparoscopic hysterectomy (SP-TLH) for benign gynecological conditions including uterine fibroids. SP-RAH demonstrated superior surgical outcomes, achieving significantly lower intraoperative blood loss (12 mL versus 80 mL) and shorter operative times compared to SP-TLH, with total surgery duration of 199 minutes versus 239 minutes. The robotic approach also resulted in reduced postoperative inflammation, as evidenced by lower C-reactive protein levels on postoperative day one (2.7 mg/dL versus 4.9 mg/dL). The da Vinci SP system overcomes limitations inherent to single-port laparoscopic surgery, such as restricted workspace and limited instrument maneuverability, while maintaining cosmetic benefits and demonstrating a relatively short learning curve with stable performance after 5-10 cases. These findings support the adoption of single-port robotic-assisted hysterectomy as an effective minimally invasive surgical option that enhances precision, reduces surgeon fatigue, and improves patient outcomes for benign gynecological indications.

title: Totally intracorporeal colorectal anastomosis (TICA) after segmental colorectal resection for deep endometriosis: technical notes and case series
summary: Deep endometriosis is an infiltrative form of the disease that commonly affects the rectum and sigmoid colon, presenting with symptoms including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, chronic pelvic pain, constipation, and symptomatic colorectal stenosis, with some patients experiencing bowel obstruction and rectal bleeding. Colorectal resection remains the most effective surgical treatment for selected cases of deep endometriosis involving the bowel. This study introduces totally intracorporeal colorectal anastomosis (TICA) as an innovative laparoscopic reconstruction technique that avoids traditional mini-laparotomy approaches, which are associated with wound infections and incisional hernias. TICA provides multiple surgical advantages including better cosmetic outcomes, reduced postoperative pain, prevention of rectovaginal fistulas, preservation of intestinal innervation through limited colon mobilization, and improved vascular perfusion via inferior mesenteric artery preservation. While TICA successfully treats deep endometriosis-related symptoms, the technique carries risks of anastomotic bleeding and intra-abdominal contamination that require careful surgical attention. Overall, TICA represents a feasible and safe alternative reconstruction method that addresses the limitations of conventional open approaches while maintaining the definitive therapeutic benefits of colorectal resection for deep endometriosis.

title: Important aspects of the multimodal perioperative management in gynecology
summary:
# Summary

Laparoscopic hysterectomy for endometriosis, performed as a minimally invasive procedure, significantly reduces hospital stay duration, accelerates patient convalescence, and lowers treatment costs while maintaining comparable safety profiles and improving patient satisfaction. Successful perioperative management requires a multimodal approach incorporating multiple standardized interventions including venous thromboembolism prophylaxis through early mobilization, compression stockings, and pharmacologic agents such as heparins and anticoagulants, along with careful perioperative antithrombotic management to balance bleeding and mortality risks. Surgical site infection prevention relies on timely prophylactic antibiotic administration within one hour preoperatively and avoidance of prolonged postoperative antibiotic continuation, with combined mechanical and oral antibiotic bowel preparation proving superior to mechanical preparation alone. Postoperative nausea and vomiting reduction employs multimodal strategies combining antiemetic medications such as dexamethasone, ondansetron, and propofol-based total intravenous anesthesia alongside nonpharmacologic approaches including acupuncture point P6 stimulation. Implementation of enhanced recovery after surgery (ERAS) and fast-track concepts substantially diminishes perioperative complications, inpatient stays, and treatment costs. Optimal outcomes depend on comprehensive patient education, well-coordinated multidisciplinary collaboration, careful medication management, and assessment of patient physical resilience to stratify perioperative risk.

title: The impact of uterine manipulator assistance on intraoperative exposure and outcomes in laparoscopic rectal cancer surgery
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluated the utility of uterine manipulators in laparoscopic rectal cancer surgery for female patients with rectal adenocarcinoma. Uterine manipulators significantly improved intraoperative pelvic exposure and surgical visualization (VAS score 8.8 vs. 6.05; p < 0.001) by permitting dynamic retraction and positioning of the uterus, which can obstruct the surgical field during deep pelvic dissections. Despite these exposure advantages, the manipulator did not compromise oncologic outcomes or increase perioperative complications, with comparable rates of anastomotic leakage, complete mesorectal excision, lymph node harvest, and metastatic counts between groups. Hospital stay was slightly shorter in the manipulator group (4.55 vs. 5.80 days), though operative time and blood loss showed no significant differences. The findings suggest that uterine manipulators represent a simple, inexpensive intervention that enhances surgical ergonomics and exposure in laparoscopic rectal resection without impairing total mesorectal excision quality or compromising oncologic completeness, supporting their adoption from gynecologic procedures into colorectal laparoscopic surgery.

title: Comparative outcomes of transurethral resection of the prostate and prostate kinetic resection in treating benign prostatic hyperplasia with prostatic stones
summary: This study compared transurethral bipolar plasma enucleation (TBPE) and traditional transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP) for treating benign prostatic hyperplasia complicated by prostatic stones. TBPE demonstrated superior clinical effectiveness, producing greater improvements in maximum urinary flow rate, post-void residual volume, and International Prostate Symptom Score compared to TURP. Beyond symptom improvement, TBPE was associated with significant clinical advantages including reduced intraoperative bleeding, fewer overall complications, shorter catheterization time, and shorter hospital stays. Additionally, TBPE resulted in better postoperative sexual function preservation compared to the traditional approach. These findings establish TBPE as a safer and more efficacious surgical option for managing benign prostatic hyperplasia with the complicating factor of prostatic stones.

title: Exploring Urinary Tract Injuries in Gynecological Surgery: Current Insights and Future Directions
summary: Gynecological surgery, particularly hysterectomy for endometriosis, carries substantial risk of iatrogenic urinary tract injury due to the anatomical proximity of the bladder and ureters to the uterus and their vulnerability to operative trauma. Deep infiltrating endometriosis significantly elevates injury risk by promoting adhesion formation and direct infiltration of endometrial implants into the bladder and ureters, thereby distorting normal pelvic anatomy. Ureteral injury represents the most common urinary tract complication in endometriosis surgery and can result from laceration, ligation, devascularization, or electrosurgical trauma, while bladder injuries typically stem from sharp dissection or thermal damage. Risk factors for bladder injury include previous cesarean delivery and fibrotic adhesions that obscure identification of the bladder-uterine septum, complicating dissection and increasing injury incidence. Prevention strategies encompass preoperative catheterization, sharp dissection techniques, careful use of high-energy instruments, cephalad uterine traction during laparoscopic procedures, and intraoperative cystoscopy for immediate detection and repair. When undetected intraoperatively, these injuries manifest postoperatively as peritonitis, sepsis, hematuria, or continuous urinary leakage, requiring imaging confirmation and surgical repair using techniques such as the Latzko procedure, O'Conor technique, or Martius flap reinforcement.

title: Urinary Tract Injuries in Abdominal, Laparoscopic and Robotic Surgery: a Comprehensive Review
summary: Hysterectomy, colorectal surgery, and prostatectomy carry significant risks of urinary tract injury (UTI), with incidence varying substantially by surgical approach. Open surgery demonstrates the highest UTI rates (0.5–3.0%), attributed to invasive techniques and prolonged operative times, while laparoscopic surgery substantially reduces this risk to 0.1–1.0% despite challenges with thermal injury and visualization. Robotic surgery shows intermediate incidence rates (0.2–1.5%), with enhanced precision lowering UTI risk in complex cases. The primary mechanisms of injury include direct trauma, thermal injury from energy-based instruments, and postoperative ischemia. Advanced intraoperative imaging and robotic systems effectively mitigate these risks by improving visualization and surgical precision. These findings underscore the importance of surgical technique selection and real-time monitoring technologies in preventing urinary tract complications during major abdominal and pelvic procedures.

title: Vaginal NOTES adnexal surgery: Results of a retrospective cohort study in The Netherlands
journal: European Journal of Obstetrics & Gynecology and Reproductive Biology: X
summary: Vaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery (vNOTES) represents a safe and effective minimally invasive approach for treating benign adnexal pathology, including ovarian cysts, ectopic pregnancies, and definitive contraception procedures. Compared to conventional laparoscopy, vNOTES adnexal surgery demonstrates significant advantages including shorter operative times, reduced postoperative pain with lower visual analog scale scores, and reduced analgesic requirements, while maintaining low rates of conversion and intraoperative complications. The procedure offers substantial patient benefits through high same-day discharge rates (88.8% in this cohort) and absence of abdominal scarring, making it a cosmetically superior alternative. However, severe pelvic adhesions, bilateral cysts, and endometriotic cysts were identified as predictors of surgical conversion, with adhesions also associated with longer procedure times. Intraoperative complications were rare (2.5%), consisting of isolated thermic injuries, and postoperative complications occurred in only 4.9% of cases. This retrospective cohort study confirms vNOTES adnexal surgery as a non-inferior, minimally invasive alternative to conventional laparoscopy for appropriate candidates.

title: Abdominoperineal pull-through with delayed coloanal anastomosis for pelvic anastomotic failure—a systematic review
summary:
# Summary

Pelvic anastomotic complications following restorative rectal surgery represent a significant clinical challenge, particularly after low anterior resection where anastomotic leak rates reach 20% and chronic presacral sinus formation occurs in approximately 9.5% of cases. Abdominoperineal pull-through with delayed coloanal anastomosis (DCAA) has emerged as an effective salvage procedure for managing these complications, including chronic fistulas, pelvic sepsis, and anastomotic failures in patients treated primarily for rectal cancer or pelvic endometriosis. The technique involves staged surgery with rectal resection and transanal delivery of the proximal colon, often requiring splenic flexure mobilization or the Deloyers procedure to achieve adequate bowel length despite challenges from intraperitoneal adhesions. While DCAA is associated with postoperative morbidity including anastomotic stricture, rectal prolapse, and colonic ischemia (particularly with vascular injury or obesity), bowel diversion significantly reduces symptom severity and the rate of permanent stoma formation. Long-term functional outcomes demonstrate acceptable anorectal function with minor low anterior resection syndrome at 26 months and an encouraging 81.4% pooled stoma-free survival rate over a mean 24-month follow-up period.

title: Optimization of postoperative pain management in gynecological laparoscopic surgery with combined Nalbuphine and Remifentanil: A retrospective analysis with propensity score matching
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective analysis demonstrates that combined Nalbuphine and Remifentanil therapy provides superior postoperative pain management in gynecological laparoscopic surgery compared to either opioid alone. Nalbuphine, a κ-receptor agonist and μ-receptor antagonist, and Remifentanil, a short-acting μ-receptor agonist, work synergistically to reduce pain while minimizing adverse effects including respiratory depression, nausea, and vomiting. The study identified that postoperative pain in laparoscopic procedures stems from multiple sources including incisional trauma, pneumoperitoneum-induced peritoneal stretching, and diaphragmatic irritation, with more invasive procedures like hysterectomy and pelvic lymphadenectomy carrying substantially greater pain risk. Patient factors including higher ASA scores and increased respiratory rates were significantly associated with postoperative pain severity, emphasizing the importance of individualized anesthetic approaches. The combined analgesic strategy proved particularly effective at the critical 6-hour postoperative window and resulted in lower pain scores and reduced opioid-related side effects compared to monotherapy approaches.

title: Comparison of conventional laparoscopic hysterectomy and vNOTES hysterectomy in previous pelvic surgery: a retrospective study
summary: This retrospective study demonstrates that vaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery (vNOTES) hysterectomy offers significant clinical advantages over conventional laparoscopic hysterectomy in patients with prior pelvic surgery. The vNOTES approach achieved substantially shorter operative times, reduced early postoperative pain (significantly lower visual analog scale scores at 6 hours), and faster hospital discharge compared to traditional laparoscopic hysterectomy. The superior ergonomic design of the transvaginal platform, combined with direct and stable visualization of the vaginal cuff and efficient suturing mechanics, directly contributes to these operational improvements. Additionally, vNOTES eliminates the need for abdominal port-site incisions required in conventional laparoscopy, resulting in scarless surgery and superior cosmetic outcomes that enhance patient satisfaction. Notably, a history of pelvic surgery, which carries potential risk for adhesion-related complications and has traditionally raised surgical concerns, does not contraindicate vNOTES and may be safely performed with reduced morbidity. Overall, this study establishes vNOTES as a safe and effective minimally invasive hysterectomy method even in the challenging population of patients with previous pelvic surgery.

title: Laparoscopic lateral suspension combined with uterosacral ligament folding and shortening versus laparoscopic sacrocolpopexy for the treatment of pelvic organ prolapse: a retrospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective cohort study compared laparoscopic lateral suspension combined with uterosacral ligament folding and shortening (LLS-ULFS) to laparoscopic sacrocolpopexy (LSC) for treating pelvic organ prolapse across anterior, apical, and posterior compartments. Both techniques effectively corrected prolapse, with LLS-ULFS demonstrating superior perioperative outcomes including shorter operative time (85 versus 105 minutes), reduced blood loss, and lower postoperative pain scores. The LLS-ULFS approach additionally improved quality-of-life measures including pelvic floor symptoms (PFDI-20 and POPDI-6 scores), sexual function (PISQ-12), and defecatory symptoms (CRADI-8), while uterosacral ligament shortening concurrently reduced lower urinary tract symptoms such as overactive bladder, nocturia, and urinary frequency. LSC was associated with more serious complications, including sacral back pain from presacral fixation, major vessel injury, and ureteral injury, whereas LLS-ULFS avoided these presacral dissection-related morbidities. These findings establish LLS-ULFS as a viable and potentially superior alternative to LSC, offering comparable anatomical correction with meaningfully improved recovery and functional outcomes while preserving the physiological vaginal axis orientation.

title: Predictors for sentinel lymph node mapping failure using indocyanine green injection in apparent early stages of endometrial cancer: A single‐center prospective study
journal: International Journal of Gynaecology and Obstetrics
summary:
# Summary

This prospective study identified clinical and anatomical predictors of sentinel lymph node (SLN) mapping failure in early-stage endometrial cancer surgery using indocyanine green (ICG) injection. While ICG injection significantly improved SLN detection with bilateral success rates of 78–85%, higher body mass index, advanced FIGO stage, and presence of uterine myomas were the primary factors independently associated with mapping failure, particularly for bilateral node detection. The mechanisms underlying these failures likely involve BMI-related increases in vascular permeability and visceral adiposity impairing lymphatic visualization, as well as myoma-induced alterations in physiological uterine lymphatic drainage. Adequate preoperative staging to exclude bulky lymphadenopathy or distant metastases, combined with proper ICG application technique using doses exceeding 3 mL and careful attention to operative sequencing, are critical modifiable factors for optimizing SLN identification and avoiding the morbidity associated with systematic lymphadenectomy while maintaining accurate pathological assessment for micrometastasis detection.

title: Association of body mass index with surgical complications after minimally invasive hysterectomy
summary: This study examined the relationship between body mass index (BMI) and surgical outcomes in patients undergoing minimally invasive hysterectomy for indications including endometriosis, adenomyosis, and pelvic pain. Higher BMI was consistently associated with increased minor complications and longer operative times, with class II and III obesity showing significantly elevated risks compared to normal BMI groups. Paradoxically, patients in higher BMI categories demonstrated lower rates of major complications, a phenomenon potentially explained by the "obesity paradox" wherein increased adiposity may enhance glucose availability and immune activation, facilitating better recovery during acute physiological stress. Class III obesity was specifically associated with increased superficial surgical site infections, sepsis, and adhesiolysis requirements, while also correlating with higher rates of comorbidities including diabetes and hypertension. Surgical volume and fellowship training emerged as protective factors reducing complication risk across all BMI categories. These findings suggest that while higher BMI increases minor perioperative morbidity in minimally invasive hysterectomy, careful surgical technique and institutional experience can mitigate these risks and help optimize outcomes.

title: Vaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery for malignant ovarian tumors: a single-institution study
summary: Vaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery (vNOTES) represents a versatile minimally invasive technique for treating a broad range of gynecological malignancies and benign conditions, including ovarian and endometrial cancers, leiomyomas, endometriosis, and ovarian cysts. This single-institution study demonstrates that vNOTES achieves favorable outcomes with low rates of intraoperative and postoperative complications, minimal postoperative pain, and shortened hospital stays compared to standard laparoscopy while offering superior cosmetic results. Primary staging surgeries for ovarian cancer via vNOTES routinely incorporated hysterectomy, omentectomy, and pelvic lymph node dissection, with patients achieving zero pain scores within 24 hours and discharge by postoperative day one or two, with no 30-day readmissions. Notably, minimally invasive approaches including vNOTES demonstrated similar recurrence rates and survival outcomes to traditional open laparotomy for ovarian cancer. The technique shows particular advantages in obese patients and successfully accommodates advanced procedures such as sentinel lymph node mapping at comparable success rates to standard laparoscopy. However, severe endometriosis and previous anterior resection served as relative contraindications due to risks of dense pelvic adhesions, and concerns regarding tumor rupture during cystectomy of initially benign-appearing lesions underscore the importance of careful patient selection and histopathological assessment.

title: Fertility-sparing surgery upon reproductive and oncologic results in ovarian cancer patients stage I (FIGO): a systematic review
summary:
# Summary

Fertility-sparing surgery (FSS) represents a viable treatment alternative for stage I ovarian cancer in carefully selected reproductive-age women, achieving comparable overall survival (88-100%) and disease-free survival (>90%) to traditional radical approaches while preserving fertility potential. The procedure requires rigorous patient selection based on tumor characteristics, with low-grade epithelial tumors (mucinous and endometrioid subtypes) and non-epithelial tumors demonstrating particularly favorable outcomes, whereas mucinous carcinomas and FIGO Stage IC2/IC3 disease present higher recurrence risks (3-33.3%). Women undergoing FSS experience high pregnancy rates (25-91.3% with >80% live birth rates), predominantly through natural conception, though assisted reproductive technologies are utilized in 3.7-28% of cases. Pregnancies following FSS show comparable maternal and neonatal outcomes to control populations, with no significant differences in preterm birth or neonatal morbidity. The success of FSS hinges on comprehensive multidisciplinary counseling, thorough preoperative assessment, and long-term surveillance, as patients must be counseled regarding potential recurrence risks and the possibility of future chemotherapy, which may further compromise fertility.

title: Safety and Efficacy of Transvaginal Natural Orifice Transluminal Endoscopic (vNOTES) Right Colectomy: A Systematic Review
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review evaluates transvaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery (vNOTES) for right colectomy, demonstrating that the technique achieves an acceptable overall complication rate of 21.9% while significantly reducing operation duration, hospital stay length, and postoperative pain compared to traditional transabdominal approaches. vNOTES leverages the transvaginal route for specimen retrieval due to its superior cosmesis and elasticity, with the procedure involving mobilization, vessel ligation, and colonic transection through a single-port device, resulting in improved patient satisfaction and avoidance of wound-related morbidities such as infection and incisional hernias. However, the technique is associated with notable intraoperative complications, particularly bladder injury (10.4%), hemorrhage (9.7%), and a conversion rate of 5.3%, attributed to anatomical restrictions and loss of surgical triangulation inherent to single-port access. The procedure demonstrates adequate oncological efficacy with a mean lymph node yield of 20.6 and a recurrence rate of 6.2%, though adoption remains limited by steep learning curves and technical challenges that may be partially mitigated through hybrid approaches utilizing assistant ports. Overall, vNOTES represents a promising minimally invasive alternative for selected female patients, offering substantial advantages in cosmesis and functional recovery while requiring careful patient selection and surgeon expertise to minimize intraoperative complications.

title: Vaginal Natural Orifice Transluminal Endoscopic Surgery (vNOTES) for Gynecological Procedures in Obese Patients: A Systematic Review
summary:
# Summary

Vaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery (vNOTES) represents a promising minimally invasive alternative for gynecological procedures in obese patients, who face significant surgical challenges and health complications. Obesity is associated with multiple gynecological pathologies including infertility, abnormal uterine bleeding, endometrial hyperplasia, and increased risk of endometrial cancer, alongside elevated perioperative risks such as vascular injury, hemorrhage, venous thromboembolism, and wound infections. vNOTES hysterectomy effectively treats early-stage endometrial cancer, endometrial hyperplasia, and postmenopausal bleeding while demonstrating substantial advantages over conventional laparoscopy: significantly shorter operative times, reduced hospitalization, lower postoperative pain at 6 and 24 hours, and improved quality-of-life scores. The technique achieves these benefits through scarless vaginal access that avoids abdominal-wall trauma and nerve injury, easier instrument handling without traversing thick adipose tissue, and the option for retroperitoneal approaches that eliminate steep Trendelenburg positioning. Notably, vNOTES maintains low intraoperative and postoperative complication rates comparable to conventional laparoscopy while offering reduced surgical-site infections and improved cosmetic outcomes, making it particularly valuable in low-resource settings where quicker recovery and shorter hospital stays are clinically advantageous.

title: Applications of Deep Learning Models in Laparoscopy for Gynecology
summary:
# Summary

Deep learning models, particularly convolutional neural networks and their variants, have emerged as powerful tools for analyzing laparoscopic images in gynecological surgery, including procedures for endometriosis management. These models demonstrate high performance in multiple critical tasks including segmentation of anatomical structures (uterus, ovaries, ureter, uterine arteries), identification of surgical instruments, classification of endometriotic lesions, and recognition of surgical actions and procedural phases. Architectures such as ResNet50, Mask R-CNN, U-Net, and GoogleNet have been successfully applied to datasets like GLENDA and SurgAI to enhance intraoperative safety and orientation by providing real-time anatomical guidance and detecting inappropriate instrument use near vital structures. Beyond immediate surgical support, deep learning applications extend to objective surgical performance evaluation, preoperative planning, and automated operative report generation, potentially transforming surgical training and workflow efficiency. However, significant challenges remain, including the difficulty of segmenting less prominent anatomical structures like the ureter due to morphological variability and the need for more comprehensively annotated datasets to improve model robustness.

title: Versius surgical system: tips and tricks for OR setting and port placement for pelvic surgery, our experience in a multi-robotic referral centre
summary:
# Summary

This multi-center study evaluates the Versius robotic surgical system for pelvic gynecological procedures, including endometriosis management via hysterectomy and adnexal surgery. The Versius system, which features four independent bedside units unlike the Da Vinci system, demonstrated safety and feasibility for benign gynecological pathology with low conversion rates, though instrument collisions occurred in 84% of operations (16/19), with high-impact collisions requiring instrument reconnection in 58% (11/19). Critical technical findings established specific trocar placement distances to minimize collisions: minimum 19 cm for bipolar instruments and 15 cm for scissors instruments from target organs, with shorter distances of 11 cm and 9 cm respectively acceptable only if high-impact collisions are tolerated. Notably, patient anatomy factors including BMI and bisiliac spine distance did not significantly affect collision rates, whereas suboptimal port positioning and training reduced robotic arm dexterity and caused system arrests. These findings provide practical guidance for optimizing the Versius system's port placement and operational protocols to improve surgical precision during complex pelvic procedures like endometriosis-related hysterectomies and adnexectomies.

title: Breast Cancer Risk After Hysterectomy: A Health Insurance Database-Based Analysis
summary: This database study found no significant association between hysterectomy and breast cancer risk, regardless of whether adnexal surgery (ovary removal) was performed, with hazard ratios near unity across surgical approaches including laparoscopic procedures. While breast cancer development is closely linked to exposure to endogenous and exogenous estrogens that promote tissue progression from normal growth to hyperplasia and neoplasia, the study suggests that hysterectomy does not substantially alter this relationship. The analysis revealed a potential mechanism whereby hysterectomy may reduce ovarian blood flow through ligation of the uterine artery, potentially leading to impaired hormone production and earlier menopause, which could theoretically lower breast cancer risk. However, the ovarian artery appears to compensate for reduced blood flow after hysterectomy, maintaining adequate ovarian function in most patients. These findings align with some prior studies showing reduced breast cancer risk with ovarian-sparing procedures, though other evidence suggests hysterectomy alone may increase risk. The study highlights that menopausal hormone therapy decisions post-hysterectomy are critical, as combined estrogen-progestogen therapy is associated with increased breast cancer risk, whereas estrogen monotherapy shows limited or reduced risk.

title: An Analysis of Opioid Consumption and Patient Recovery after Hysterectomy by Surgical Approach
summary: This study demonstrates that minimally invasive hysterectomy approaches substantially reduce postoperative opioid consumption compared to open surgical procedures in patients undergoing hysterectomy for endometriosis. Both laparoscopic and vaginal hysterectomy techniques were associated with significantly lower opioid requirements in the immediate postoperative period compared to open hysterectomy, reflecting the reduced tissue trauma inherent to these approaches. Beyond surgical methodology, older patient age and surgical procedures performed in 2019 versus 2018 were independently associated with lower opioid consumption, potentially indicating improved pain management protocols and patient selection over time. The use of minimally invasive surgical techniques not only reduced opioid dependency but also facilitated more rapid patient recovery. These findings support the adoption of laparoscopic or vaginal hysterectomy as preferred surgical approaches for managing endometriosis-related symptoms, offering the dual benefit of decreased postoperative pain medication requirements and faster functional recovery compared to traditional open surgery.

title: A Systematic Review and Meta‐Analysis of the Effectiveness and Cost of Single‐Site Robotic Surgery and Single‐Site Laparoscopic Surgery in Gynecological Diseases: The Era of Single‐Site Robotic Surgery May Have Arrived
summary:
# Summary

This meta-analysis demonstrates that single-site Da Vinci robotic surgery (RSS) offers distinct advantages over single-site laparoscopic surgery (LESS) in gynecological procedures, though with important contextual variations. For malignant tumor surgery, RSS significantly reduces operative time and estimated blood loss compared to LESS, whereas for total hysterectomy, RSS requires longer operative times despite achieving less hemoglobin drop. Single-site robotic surgery substantially reduces postoperative complications including wound infections, trocar bleeding, postoperative pain, and hernia formation, while overcoming the steeper learning curve associated with LESS, which requires 40 cases compared to 10-15 cases for robotic proficiency. The superior three-dimensional visualization and surgical comfort provided by the robotic platform are tempered by its significantly higher total hospital costs and lack of tactile feedback. The authors conclude that technological improvements in commercial single-point ports and the fourth-generation Da Vinci SP system have narrowed operative time differences after 2020, supporting the clinical viability of single-site robotic surgery in gynecological applications despite cost considerations.

title: Comparison of postoperative pain rates with different hysterectomy techniques: A retrospective study
summary:
# Comparison of Postoperative Pain Rates with Different Hysterectomy Techniques

This retrospective study compared pain outcomes across three hysterectomy approaches for benign gynecological conditions including endometriosis, uterine fibroids, adenomyosis, and chronic pelvic pain. Vaginally-assisted natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery (vNOTES) demonstrated superior pain control compared to abdominal and laparoscopic hysterectomy, with patients experiencing significantly lower pain intensity, reduced analgesic requirements, and shorter pain duration at three and six months postoperatively. The minimally invasive transvaginal approach of vNOTES avoids the large abdominal incision and associated nerve damage characteristic of abdominal hysterectomy, while eliminating trocar-related complications and reducing adhesion formation from previous surgeries. Early postoperative pain intensity at 24 hours and one week emerged as a critical predictor of postsurgical chronic pain development, with patient risk factors including preoperative pain, young age, anxiety, and depression also contributing significantly. Beyond pain management, vNOTES offered additional clinical advantages including shorter operative duration, improved cosmetic outcomes, and reduced hospital stays compared to traditional approaches, establishing it as an effective technique for hysterectomy with superior postoperative pain profiles.

title: Implementing Robotic Gynecologic Surgery: Early Outcomes From a Tertiary Care Hospital in Portugal
summary:
# Summary

This study reports early outcomes from implementing robot-assisted gynecologic surgery at a Portuguese tertiary care hospital, demonstrating that robotic technology provides significant advantages over traditional open surgery for treating benign and malignant gynecologic conditions including endometriosis, hysterectomy, myomectomy, and adnexal tumors. Robot-assisted procedures achieved superior outcomes through enhanced three-dimensional visualization and improved surgical precision, resulting in minimal blood loss (66.1% of patients lost <100 mL with zero transfusions), low intraoperative complications (1.8%), minimal readmission rates (2.8%), and faster recovery compared to laparotomy. Among 109 patients, hysterectomy was the most common procedure (69.7%), with an exceptionally low conversion rate to open surgery of 0.9% despite complex cases. The robotic approach proved particularly beneficial for obese patients, reducing complication rates and hospital stays substantially compared to open surgery, while structured surgical team training was critical for achieving the program's success and patient safety outcomes.

title: A Comparative Analysis of the Pipelle Device and Conventional Dilatation and Curettage as Investigative Tools for Abnormal Uterine Bleeding: Assessing Sample Adequacy and Patient Perspectives: A Prospective Observational Cohort Study
summary:
# Summary

Abnormal uterine bleeding requires investigation to exclude serious intrauterine pathologies including endometrial hyperplasia, carcinoma, adenomyosis, and fibroids, with risk substantially elevated in women over 40. This prospective study compared the Pipelle aspirator and conventional dilatation and curettage (D&C) as diagnostic tools, finding both methods yielded adequate endometrial tissue samples in approximately 94-95% of cases and successfully detected pathologies including endometrial adenocarcinoma, hyperplasia with atypia, and tuberculosis. While D&C under anesthesia provided superior patient comfort and satisfaction by reducing postoperative pain, the Pipelle aspirator offers a less invasive alternative, though it carries limitations including potential difficulty accessing the uterine cavity and higher pain scores during the procedure. The accuracy of histopathological diagnosis directly correlates with endometrial tissue volume obtained, making sample adequacy critical to clinical decision-making. D&C's associated risks of uterine perforation, hemorrhage, and infection, coupled with its anesthesia requirements, make the Pipelle aspirator an attractive option for premenopausal women, though its success rates decline significantly in women over 55 due to increased cervical stenosis.

title: A bibliometric study of the top 100 most-cited papers in ureteral stricture reconstruction
summary:
# Summary

This bibliometric analysis examines the 100 most-cited papers in ureteral stricture reconstruction, revealing a significant shift toward robot-assisted surgical approaches and mucosal graft techniques. Ureteral strictures, caused primarily by iatrogenic injury, trauma, malignancy, and retroperitoneal fibrosis, can progress to functional obstruction and chronic renal failure if untreated. Modern reconstruction strategies increasingly employ robot-assisted technology in combination with mucosal grafts—particularly buccal mucosa, lingual mucosa, and appendiceal grafts—which offer advantages over traditional ileal substitution. Key institutions including Temple University, Peking University, and the University of California San Francisco, led by prominent researchers such as Daniel D. Eun and Xuesong Li, have driven innovation in this field. Robot-assisted reconstruction demonstrates superior outcomes including reduced recurrence rates, improved aesthetic results, and better hydronephrosis resolution compared to endoscopic stenting, reflecting the field's evolution toward minimally invasive, tissue-sparing surgical techniques.

title: Clinical effects of transumbilical laparoendoscopic single-site extracorporeal surgery in treatment of giant ovarian cysts and huge cystic pelvic masses
summary: Transumbilical laparoendoscopic single-site extracorporeal surgery (TU-LESS-E) demonstrates superior clinical efficacy for treating giant ovarian cysts and huge cystic pelvic masses compared to conventional multiport laparoscopy. TU-LESS-E significantly reduces intraoperative cystic fluid overflow, blood loss, and postoperative incisional pain while minimizing surgical trauma and preventing cyst perforation through improved visualization. Critically, TU-LESS-E preserves ovarian function and ovarian reserve better than traditional laparoscopic approaches, reducing oxidative stress responses and decreasing the risk of premature ovarian insufficiency. Since ovarian surgery, endometriosis, and chemotherapy all contribute to declining ovarian reserve through hormonal imbalances and tissue loss, preserving ovarian function is essential for maintaining fertility potential. Biomarkers including AMH, FSH, LH, and E2 serve as indicators of ovarian reserve, with low AMH levels correlating with reduced clinical pregnancy rates. This single-site approach therefore represents an advancement in minimally invasive gynecological surgery by combining reduced morbidity with improved preservation of reproductive function.

title: Does lymphadenectomy predict survival in early-stage epithelial ovarian cancer? An updated systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

This meta-analysis evaluates whether lymphadenectomy improves survival in early-stage epithelial ovarian cancer, synthesizing data from multiple study types. The analysis demonstrates that lymphadenectomy is associated with a 20% improvement in overall survival and 26% improvement in progression-free survival, with particularly pronounced benefits in clear cell histological subtypes. However, this survival benefit appears primarily in retrospective studies, while prospective studies and randomized controlled trials show no significant survival improvement, highlighting important methodological limitations in the literature. Lymphadenectomy carries substantial perioperative risks including vascular damage, nerve injury, lymphedema, and lymphcele formation, with comprehensive retroperitoneal staging causing increased postoperative complications compared to lymph node sampling. The procedure's value extends beyond survival to accurate disease staging, though lymph node involvement varies significantly by histological subtype, being highest in serous tumors and lowest in endometrioid and mucinous variants. Due to inconsistent evidence quality and a lack of robust prospective data, current ESMO-ESGO guidelines remain cautious about recommending routine lymphadenectomy despite its theoretical staging benefits.

title: Preoperative Assessment for Transmasculine Patients Undergoing Gender-Affirming Hysterectomy
summary: This study evaluates the utility of preoperative pelvic ultrasonography in transmasculine patients undergoing gender-affirming hysterectomy. Among 57 patients on long-term testosterone therapy (mean duration 53 months), most ultrasonograms (72%) showed normal findings, with physiologic ovarian cysts being the most common incidental finding. Critically, preoperative ultrasound findings did not alter the planned conventional laparoscopic surgical approach in any patient, and review of ultrasound reports did not change the operative management. Only one patient required additional workup—an endometrial biopsy for thickened endometrium, which was clinically indicated independent of the imaging study. The authors conclude that routine preoperative ultrasonography may not be necessary before gender-affirming hysterectomy, though targeted endometrial sampling remains warranted for patients with persistent breakthrough bleeding despite testosterone therapy. These findings suggest that surgical planning for this population can proceed without standard preoperative imaging in straightforward cases.

title: Effects of Minimally Invasive Gynecologic Surgery Subspecialty Training on Outcomes of Hysterectomy for Benign Indications
summary: This study demonstrates that fellowship training in minimally invasive gynecologic surgery (MIGS) significantly improves hysterectomy outcomes for benign indications including endometriosis, adenomyosis, and pelvic pain. MIGS-trained surgeons achieved substantially lower perioperative complication rates (11.9%) compared to general gynecology (18.2%) and obstetric-gynecology groups, with conversion to laparotomy rates of only 0.3% versus 7.1-7.6% in non-MIGS groups. The MIGS approach also reduced estimated blood loss, total admission time, and hospitalization duration, with higher rates of same-day discharge achieved through fellowship-trained surgeons' expertise and enhanced recovery protocols. MIGS surgeons preferentially performed laparoscopic rather than abdominal hysterectomy, with nearly all MIGS cases utilizing minimally invasive techniques compared to approximately one-third in other groups. These findings underscore the clinical value of subspecialty training in managing complex benign gynecologic pathology, particularly conditions like endometriosis requiring advanced surgical skills. Overall, MIGS fellowship training independently reduces both intraoperative and postoperative complications while improving recovery profiles for benign hysterectomy cases.

title: Pioneering robotic surgery for complex gynecologic conditions: implementation beyond cancer care
summary: Robot-assisted surgery represents an advanced minimally invasive approach for treating complex gynecologic conditions including endometriosis, myomectomy, and gynecologic malignancies. Compared to traditional laparoscopy, robotic systems offer superior visualization through high-definition 3D imaging, elimination of tremors, and increased instrument maneuverability, which collectively enhance surgical precision and reduce the learning curve for surgeons. In this series of 184 robot-assisted gynecologic procedures, the approach demonstrated excellent safety and efficacy, with only a 1.1% conversion rate, a mean hospitalization time of 1.5 days, and 2.2% minor post-operative complications, with endometriosis procedures showing particularly short recovery periods. The robotic platform is especially beneficial for treating complex endometriosis cases involving adhesions and intestinal involvement, as it improves precision of dissection and identification of autonomic nerves while reducing blood loss and tissue trauma. Despite high initial costs and increased operative times for complex endometriosis cases, robot-assisted surgery extends minimally invasive options to patients who might otherwise require open surgery, including obese patients and those with deep endometriosis, while also reducing surgeon physical strain through ergonomic console positioning.

title: Isobaric NOTES hysterectomy: an alternative to transvaginal NOTES hysterectomy
summary:
# Summary: Isobaric NOTES Hysterectomy

This study introduces isobaric natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery (iNH) hysterectomy as a gasless alternative to conventional transvaginal NOTES hysterectomy, eliminating complications associated with CO2 pneumoperitoneum such as gas leakage, subcutaneous emphysema, and cardiopulmonary dysfunction. The isobaric technique combines the procedural simplicity of traditional vaginal hysterectomy with enhanced visualization and instrument access through a single-hole vaginal platform, permitting continuous suction to clear blood and smoke while maintaining stable working conditions without pneumoperitoneum. Compared to single-port laparoscopic-assisted vaginal hysterectomy (SP-LAVH), iNH demonstrated significantly superior outcomes including lower median blood loss (120 mL vs. 200 mL), reduced hemoglobin changes on postoperative day one, and shorter hospital stays, while maintaining safety even in challenging cases with large uteri and significant pelvic adhesions. The technique proved viable and safe with no intraoperative complications in the iNH group, whereas the SP-LAVH group experienced two complications including bowel and bladder injuries. By avoiding the need for closed cavity insufflation and permitting simultaneous use of multiple instruments, iNH simplifies surgical workflow while reducing CO2-related risks, resulting in less postoperative discomfort and earlier patient discharge. This novel approach represents a distinct advancement within transvaginal hysterectomy techniques, merging the benefits of minimally invasive visualization with the physiologic advantages of gasless laparoscopic surgery.

title: Multicenter evaluation of the DEXTER® Robotic Surgery System for Total Hysterectomy with Adnexal Surgery
summary: This multicenter study evaluated the DEXTER® Robotic Surgery System for minimally invasive total hysterectomy with adnexal surgery across 52 patients with diverse indications including abnormal uterine bleeding (40.4%), fibroids (23.1%), symptomatic adenomyosis (21.2%), and endometriosis (13.5%). The DEXTER system demonstrated excellent safety and operational efficiency, achieving zero intraoperative complications or conversions, with mean operative time of 116.2 minutes and minimal blood loss (87.8 mL), alongside short hospitalization (mean 2.0 days). The system's innovative features—including modular design, open architecture for standard laparoscopic equipment compatibility, quick 3.8-minute docking time, and sterile console setup enabling seamless transitions between laparoscopic and robotic modalities—significantly reduce infrastructure barriers compared to conventional robotic platforms. Postoperative adverse events were predominantly minor (46.2% Grade I–II), with one Grade IIIb event involving vaginal cuff bleeding from suture dehiscence. The feasibility of three-trocar techniques in 21.2% of cases with smaller uteri further contributed to reduced postoperative pain and improved cosmetic outcomes. These findings support DEXTER's role as a cost-effective, accessible alternative to conventional robotic systems for expanding minimally invasive hysterectomy access across diverse hospital settings.
