title: Predictive value of the systemic immune-inflammation index in 28-day survival after surgery for intracerebral hemorrhage
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluates the systemic immune-inflammation index (SIRI)—calculated from neutrophil, monocyte, and lymphocyte counts—as a prognostic biomarker for 28-day mortality in intracerebral hemorrhage (ICH) patients undergoing surgical intervention. The research identifies SIRI as an independent risk factor for mortality, with higher SIRI values significantly elevating mortality odds (OR = 1.76), reflecting the critical role inflammatory cascade activation plays in ICH pathogenesis and neurological outcomes. Multivariate analysis identified five independent mortality predictors: SIRI elevation, multidrug-resistant bacterial infection, altered mental status, gastrointestinal hemorrhage, and prolonged activated partial thromboplastin time, while surgical decompression (open craniotomy with hematoma evacuation) demonstrated a protective effect. Multiple clinical parameters—including Glasgow Coma Scale score, systolic blood pressure, blood volume, prothrombin time, fibrinogen, and D-dimer levels—were associated with postoperative complications, alongside anatomical factors such as hematoma volume, intraventricular hemorrhage, and hydrocephalus. These findings establish SIRI as a reliable, dynamic inflammatory biomarker for individualized prognostic prediction in surgically treated ICH patients, offering clinicians an accessible tool to identify high-risk patients and optimize management strategies.

title: V-Notes Sentinel Lymph Node Staging for Endometrial Cancer: A Systematic Review
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review evaluates V-NOTES (vaginally-assisted natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery) for sentinel lymph node mapping in early-stage endometrial cancer surgical staging. V-NOTES achieved a 98.19% sentinel lymph node detection rate with 93.7% bilateral detection, significantly outperforming conventional laparoscopic approaches while reducing blood loss (mean 72.03 mL), operative time (average 155 minutes), and hospitalization duration (mean 2.4 days). The technique proved particularly advantageous for obese patients who have increased abdominal wall thickness and comorbidities that complicate traditional open or laparoscopic surgery, while avoiding conversion to laparotomy in all reported cases. Indocyanine green emerged as the superior tracer for lymphatic mapping, with sentinel nodes most frequently identified in obturator and external iliac regions. Complications were minimal at 3.9%, comparable to conventional minimally invasive techniques, though the technique is contraindicated in patients with deep endometriosis. These findings establish V-NOTES as a highly effective and safe approach for lymph node evaluation that guides adjuvant treatment decisions and improves patient prognosis in endometrial cancer staging.

title: Reliability of Frozen Section Biopsy in Detecting Endometrial Pathologies: A Systematic Review of Sensitivity and Specificity
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review evaluates the reliability of frozen section (FS) biopsy for detecting endometrial pathologies across 15 studies, revealing a diagnostic method with consistently high specificity but variable sensitivity. FS biopsy demonstrates excellent performance for identifying endometrial carcinomas, particularly high-grade tumors and myometrial invasion, with specificity often reaching 100% and positive predictive values as high as 100%, making it valuable for confirming malignancy during surgery. However, the technique shows significant limitations for detecting premalignant lesions, especially complex atypical hyperplasia (CAH) and early-stage cancers, with sensitivity ranging widely from 41.1% to 96.9% and negative predictive values sometimes as low as 12.5%. Key factors compromising FS accuracy include sampling errors, tissue artifacts created during freezing, the heterogeneity of endometrial lesions, and pathologist expertise, with performance improving substantially when gross lesions are present. The authors conclude that FS biopsy should function as a complementary diagnostic tool rather than a definitive method, and recommend standardizing protocols, enhancing pathologist training, and integrating multimodal approaches to improve diagnostic reliability in complex endometrial pathology cases.

title: Comparison of Transvaginal Natural Orifice Surgery (vNOTES) and Laparoscopic Tubal Ligation: Effects on Postoperative Pain, Sexual Functions, and Surgical Outcomes
journal: JSLS : Journal of the Society of Laparoscopic & Robotic Surgeons
summary: This study compared transvaginal natural orifice surgery (vNOTES) with conventional laparoscopy (CL) for bilateral tubal ligation, demonstrating that vNOTES substantially reduces postoperative morbidity. Patients undergoing vNOTES experienced significantly lower pain scores at all measured timepoints and markedly reduced shoulder pain incidence (15% versus 85% in the CL group), which results from lower intra-abdominal pressure requirements during the procedure. The vNOTES approach also reduced intraoperative opioid requirements compared to conventional laparoscopy, contributing to faster overall recovery without negatively impacting sexual function. Both techniques effectively accomplish bilateral tubal ligation, but vNOTES emerges as a safe and effective alternative that provides superior pain management outcomes. These findings suggest vNOTES offers clinically meaningful advantages for sterilization procedures while maintaining equivalent surgical efficacy to the standard laparoscopic approach.

title: Robot-assisted ureteral reimplantation using the KangDuo surgical Robot-01 system: a prospective, single-center, single-arm pilot study
summary: This pilot study demonstrates that the KangDuo surgical Robot-01 (KD-SR-01) system is technically feasible and safe for robot-assisted ureteral reimplantation to treat distal ureteral stricture, vesicoureteral reflux, and ureterovaginal fistula. Notably, ureteral endometriosis was identified as a significant etiology of distal ureteral stricture in 25.8% of patients, highlighting the relevance to gynecological conditions. At median follow-up of 22 months, 96.8% of patients demonstrated resolution or improvement of flank pain, hydronephrosis, and ureteral obstruction following robotic reimplantation. The KD-SR-01 system offers distinct advantages over the da Vinci system, including superior 3D depth perception, rapid docking capability (median 4.3 minutes), an open console design that reduces surgeon neck strain and eye fatigue, and potentially lower costs. Laparoscopic reimplantation techniques employing psoas hitch procedures and submucosal tunnel reimplantation with anti-reflux nipple formation achieved outcomes comparable to open surgery while reducing blood loss and postoperative pain. Although 25.8% of patients developed postoperative urinary tract infections amenable to antibiotic therapy, the robotic platform's enhanced precision and ergonomics support its potential as a viable alternative for complex ureteral reconstruction in endometriosis-related ureteral pathology.

title: Research Trends and Hotspots in Minimally Invasive Gynecologic Surgery: A Bibliometric and Visualization Analysis
summary:
# Summary

This bibliometric analysis reveals that minimally invasive gynecologic surgery (MIGS) has emerged as a dominant research focus globally, with endometriosis, laparoscopy, and hysterectomy representing key hotspots in the field. MIGS techniques, encompassing multiport laparoscopy, robotic-assisted approaches, and laparoendoscopic single-site surgery, substantially reduce blood loss, intra-operative bleeding, postoperative discomfort, and hospitalization duration compared to open surgical procedures. Endometriosis, endometrial cancer, and uterine fibroids rank as the top three MIGS-associated diseases, with laparoscopy representing the most commonly employed therapeutic approach. A notable exception exists for minimally invasive radical hysterectomy in cancer treatment, which demonstrates inferior disease-free and overall survival outcomes, warranting careful clinical consideration. Geographic and institutional variation in research priorities is evident, with USA institutions emphasizing endometriosis management, European centers focusing on hysteroscopy and clinical guidelines, and Chinese institutions concentrating on general gynecologic applications. Recent research trends show a shift toward broader healthcare outcomes, including cost-effectiveness (with projections of $2.8 million in savings from a 10% increase in MIGS for endometrial cancer), quality-of-life assessments, and fertility preservation concerns.

title: Ways to Repair Multicompartment Prolapse: Decision-Making and Surgical Approach
journal: Clinics in Colon and Rectal Surgery
summary:
# Summary

This article examines the surgical management of multicompartment pelvic organ prolapse (POP), emphasizing that concurrent rectal prolapse and POP are frequently underrecognized conditions requiring coordinated treatment. The authors demonstrate that combined surgical approaches—including robotic or laparoscopic techniques such as suture rectopexy, anterior colporrhaphy, hysteropexy, and ventral mesh rectopexy—significantly improve outcomes compared to isolated procedures, with patients reporting superior quality of life, defecatory function, and sexual function. Importantly, the study highlights that failure to address all affected compartments increases recurrence risk, particularly in patients with connective tissue disorders, young nulliparous patients, and those with obesity; consequently, abdominal approaches using native tissue repair are preferred in these higher-risk populations. The authors stress that multidisciplinary collaboration among urology, urogynecology, and colorectal surgery is essential for comprehensive care, and that adjunctive interventions such as pelvic floor physical therapy and vaginal estrogen supplementation enhance surgical outcomes. Case presentations illustrate how surgical selection must be individualized based on patient comorbidities, prolapse morphology, and functional deficits documented by anorectal manometry and defecography.

title: Innovative laparoscopic technique for #ENZIAN C3 intestinal endometriotic nodule and concurrent uterine fibroids: NOSES
summary:
# Summary

This case study demonstrates the successful treatment of a complex presentation combining deep infiltrating endometriosis (ENZIAN C3 nodule affecting the lower rectum), bilateral ovarian endometriomas, and uterine fibroids using an innovative combination of advanced laparoscopic techniques. Deep infiltrating endometriosis caused the patient's severe symptoms including hypermenorrhea, dysmenorrhea, bowel dysfunction, and urinary symptoms, while the ENZIAN C3 intestinal nodule induced anatomical distortion and obstructive bowel symptoms. The surgical approach integrated the reverse laparoscopic technique, natural orifice specimen extraction surgery (NOSES), and advanced intraoperative bleeding control strategies (vasopressin injection and temporary ligation of vascular structures) to achieve optimal outcomes. This combined approach significantly reduced operative time to 180 minutes, minimized blood loss to 40 cc, preserved ovarian tissue integrity, and reduced postoperative complications including pain, recovery time, and infection risk. The patient demonstrated symptom resolution at one month follow-up, highlighting how the integration of multiple advanced techniques enables both effective treatment of severe endometriosis and improved perioperative outcomes compared to conventional approaches.

title: Practice patterns regarding female reproductive organ-sparing and nerve-sparing radical cystectomy among urologic oncologists in the U.S.
summary:
# Summary

While radical cystectomy remains the gold standard treatment for muscle-invasive and high-risk non-muscle-invasive bladder cancer, standard en bloc approaches that remove adjacent reproductive organs cause significant morbidity including neurovascular bundle damage, disruption of clitoral blood supply, and surgical menopause. Reproductive organ-sparing and nerve-sparing radical cystectomy techniques preserve fertility, sexual function, urinary control, and pelvic floor support while preventing the adverse health consequences of surgical menopause, including increased mortality, bone loss, cardiovascular dysfunction, and cognitive impairment. Preservation of the ovaries maintains hormonal and bone health, while vaginal preservation improves sexual activity and pelvic floor support by protecting the neurovascular bundles coursing along the vaginal lateral walls. Among urologic oncologists surveyed, there is emerging recognition of these organ-sparing techniques as oncologically safe alternatives, though practice pattern variation exists regarding implementation and patient selection, with some surgeons considering factors such as concomitant endometriosis when deciding on oophorectomy.

title: Evaluating surgical strategies for small bowel obstruction in virgin abdomen: A comparative analysis of laparoscopic and open approaches
summary:
# Summary

Small bowel obstruction in virgin abdomen (patients without prior abdominal surgery) is most commonly caused by adhesions (60%), followed by Meckel diverticulum, internal hernia, and malignancy, with endometriosis and strictures also identified as causative factors. Laparoscopic surgery demonstrates significant clinical advantages over open surgery for managing these obstructions, including reduced incision length, lower postoperative pain scores, decreased complication rates, shorter hospital stays, and faster recovery without negative exploration outcomes. While nonoperative management with nasogastric decompression can resolve some cases, it carries risks of diagnostic delay for malignancy, increased recurrence, and bowel strangulation, making surgical intervention preferable in many patients. The laparoscopic approach is particularly suitable for adhesiolysis and allows definitive diagnosis while providing simultaneous therapeutic relief, though conversion to open surgery may be necessary for massive adhesions, long-segment bowel ischemia, or iatrogenic perforation. Computed tomography imaging has limited diagnostic accuracy for closed-loop obstruction and bowel ischemia, making clinical judgment essential in determining surgical candidacy. Overall, laparoscopic surgery represents the superior treatment strategy for small bowel obstruction in virgin abdomen, achieving both diagnostic and therapeutic goals with improved patient outcomes.

title: Quality of life after laparoscopic surgery for very low rectal cancer: A sub‐analysis of the ultimate trial
summary: This prospective trial examined quality of life outcomes following laparoscopic surgery for very low rectal cancer using validated instruments including the SF-36 questionnaire and Wexner bowel dysfunction score. Laparoscopic low anterior resection effectively treats rectal cancer but frequently causes postoperative bowel dysfunction, particularly related to tumor distance from the anal margin, which significantly compromises patients' physical quality of life and ability to work or engage in social activities. Stage III cancer patients experienced the most pronounced decline in physical health scores, largely attributable to adjuvant chemotherapy toxicities such as oxaliplatin-induced peripheral neuropathy and capecitabine-induced hand-foot syndrome, with shortened three-month oxaliplatin courses showing promise in reducing these effects. While physical component scores remained diminished even three years after surgery, mental component scores consistently improved over time across all patient subgroups, suggesting psychological adaptation to cancer treatment. Severe bowel dysfunction at one year post-surgery (Wexner score ≥9) emerged as a strong predictor of lower role-based quality of life scores, indicating that surgical technique and chemotherapy management should prioritize functional bowel preservation to optimize long-term patient well-being.

title: Diagnostic yield of cystography after sigmoid resection for colovesical fistula due to complicated diverticulitis
summary: Complicated diverticulitis can cause colovesical fistula in approximately 4% of patients, presenting with characteristic symptoms including pneumaturia, recurrent urinary tract infections, and fecaluria, with the condition affecting predominantly male patients. Sigmoid resection is the definitive treatment, and intraoperative methylene blue testing can identify vesical defects, allowing surgeons to determine whether simple or complex bladder repair is needed. Postoperative cystography has limited diagnostic value in patients undergoing simple bladder repair or no repair, with 91.9% of studies showing normal results and no significant difference in indwelling urinary catheter duration or long-term urinary tract symptoms regardless of cystography performance. However, cystography should be considered after complex bladder repairs, as extravesical contrast was detected in some cases and resolved with extended catheter drainage. The study demonstrates that intraoperative vesical leakage testing with negative results reliably predicts negative postoperative cystography, potentially eliminating the need for routine imaging in straightforward cases. Long-term follow-up revealed no fistula recurrence, supporting the efficacy of sigmoid resection combined with selective bladder repair strategies.

title: Association of Living Alone and Same-Day Discharge After Hysterectomy for Benign Gynecologic Indications
summary: This study demonstrates that social support status significantly influences discharge outcomes following hysterectomy for benign gynecologic conditions, including endometriosis cases treated with laparoscopic approaches. Patients living alone showed substantially reduced same-day discharge rates (22% versus 28%) and had 32% higher odds of requiring overnight hospitalization compared to those with household support, independent of clinical factors. Surgical approach emerged as a critical predictor, with laparoscopic hysterectomy strongly associated with same-day discharge success and reduced recovery times, though this benefit was partially offset by lack of caregiver support. Patient age and operative duration exceeding 180 minutes independently increased overnight stay likelihood, with each additional year of age increasing admission odds by 6%. The findings underscore that adequate caregiver support facilitates postoperative recovery and safe disposition, while its absence contributes to delayed discharge and potentially higher readmission risk. These results suggest that preoperative counseling and discharge planning should incorporate social support assessment, and that programs enhancing home support services could substantially improve outcomes for vulnerable patients undergoing minimally invasive gynecologic surgery.

title: Spinal Analgesia Versus Intravenous Low-Dose Oxycodone for Pain Management After Robotic Hysterectomy: Preliminary Results from an ERAS Institution
summary: This preliminary study compared two postoperative pain management strategies following robotic hysterectomy within an Enhanced Recovery After Surgery (ERAS) framework. Patients received either spinal analgesia (morphine and ropivacaine) or intravenous low-dose oxycodone as part of a multimodal analgesia protocol. While spinal analgesia provided excellent pain coverage, intravenous low-dose oxycodone demonstrated superior outcomes: patients receiving oxycodone experienced significantly lower pain scores by day 2, better tolerance of solid food on postoperative day 0, and substantially reduced postoperative nausea and vomiting at all time points. The findings support a shift from traditional spinal analgesia toward low-dose oxycodone in the context of minimally invasive robotic surgery, as this approach better aligns with ERAS principles by minimizing opioid-related complications that can impair early mobilization and recovery. By reducing postoperative nausea, vomiting, and adequate pain control, low-dose oxycodone enables earlier resumption of oral nutrition and mobilization, facilitating faster overall recovery in robotic hysterectomy patients.

title: ‘En bloc’ peritoneal mesometrial resection (PMMR) and pelvic targeted compartmental lymphadenectomy (TCL) for management of patients with endometrial cancer – feasibility and safety study of a ‘new kid on the block’ in robotic surgery
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluates the feasibility and safety of robotic-assisted peritoneal mesometrial resection combined with targeted compartmental lymphadenectomy (PMMR+TCL) for endometrial cancer management, comparing it to robotic subtotal hysterectomy with sentinel lymph node dissection. PMMR+TCL, based on cancer field surgery principles, enables superior locoregional control without requiring adjuvant radiotherapy and detected additional pathological findings including endometriosis and carcinoma deposits in the resected tissue compartments. The procedure successfully identified lymph node metastases in 5.1% of patients, with molecular subtypes MMRd, NSMP, and p53 mutations showing higher metastatic potential. While PMMR+TCL required longer operative time and was better tolerated in younger patients with lower BMI and fewer comorbidities, postoperative complications included obturator nerve injury, vaginal dehiscence with lymphorrhea, and pelvic hematomas, though most were grade I-II severity. The findings support PMMR+TCL as a feasible oncologic approach for selected endometrial cancer patients, particularly those without significant risk factors that would impair wound healing or surgical tolerance.

title: Investigating the Impact of Benign Indication Hysterectomy on Pelvic Floor Symptoms and Sexual Function: A Prospective Study Integrating Pelvic Floor Ultrasonography and Surface Electromyography Test
summary:
# Summary

Single-port laparoscopic hysterectomy for benign gynecological conditions including endometriosis, uterine fibroids, and abnormal uterine bleeding significantly improves pelvic floor function and reduces pelvic floor disorder symptoms as measured by PFDI-20 scores six months postoperatively. However, the procedure paradoxically reduces multiple dimensions of sexual function, including desire, arousal, lubrication, orgasm, and satisfaction, despite improvements in pelvic floor muscle assessment parameters. Postoperative changes include increased bladder neck descent and altered pelvic floor muscle contraction dynamics, with pelvic floor muscle strength and contraction duration associated with better sexual outcomes. The study demonstrates that while pelvic floor structural and functional improvements occur after hysterectomy, sexual dysfunction may be influenced by factors including age, menopausal transition, and hormonal changes rather than anatomical pelvic floor changes alone. Integrated assessment using pelvic floor ultrasonography and surface electromyography revealed that improvements in pelvic floor symptoms across all domains—urinary, fecal, and prolapse-related—do not translate to preserved or improved sexual function postoperatively.

title: Application Effect of Different Robotic Surgery Systems in Radical Prostatectomy: A Comparative Study Based on a Network Meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

Radical prostatectomy using robot-assisted surgery is the standard treatment for localized prostate cancer and reduces cancer-specific mortality by 48% with an average survival benefit of 2.2 years. This network meta-analysis compared four robotic surgery systems—Da Vinci, Hugo, Hinotori, and KangDuo—for radical prostatectomy outcomes. The Da Vinci system demonstrated superior intraoperative efficiency, achieving the shortest operative and console times with a SUCRA score of 90.7 for operative time, significantly outperforming the other three systems. While the Hugo system exhibited the lowest complication rate and the KangDuo system showed slightly shorter hospital stays, all robotic platforms achieved comparable safety profiles regarding surgical margins, blood loss, postoperative incontinence, and overall complication rates. Notably, operative time efficiency appears to be the primary distinguishing factor between systems, with the KangDuo platform potentially offering cost-effectiveness advantages despite longer operative durations, whereas lifestyle risk factors including obesity, high-fat diet, and smoking contribute to prostate cancer development.

title: A Study to Evaluate the Effect of Back Muscle Strengthening Exercises on Pain Among Post-Hysterectomy Women Admitted in Tertiary Care Hospitals
summary:
# Summary

Hysterectomy is an effective surgical treatment for endometriosis, uterine fibroids, and abnormal uterine bleeding, but the procedure commonly results in significant postoperative pain and reduced mobility. This study demonstrated that back muscle strengthening exercises substantially reduced post-hysterectomy pain, with pain scores decreasing from 7.80 ± 1.92 to 4.03 ± 2.18 (P < 0.0001) in the intervention group over six days. These exercises improve spinal stability and muscular support, thereby alleviating discomfort associated with immobility and poor posture following surgery. Patient-specific factors including age, education level, and type of hysterectomy significantly influenced pain outcomes, suggesting the need for tailored rehabilitation approaches. The findings support integrating back muscle strengthening exercises into routine post-hysterectomy care as an effective non-pharmacological strategy to enhance recovery and improve patient comfort during the postoperative period.

title: Colpocleisis—Still a Valuable Option: A Point of Technique
summary:
# Summary

Pelvic organ prolapse is a common age-related condition affecting up to 40% of women, caused by pelvic floor weakness stemming from childbirth, obesity, aging, and connective tissue or muscle atrophy. Patients experience bothersome symptoms including sensation of bulge, lower abdominal pain, and dyspareunia, with advanced cases potentially causing bladder dysfunction through urinary obstruction. While first-line treatments include pessaries and pelvic floor muscle training, colpocleisis—both total and Le-Fort techniques—represents an effective surgical option for elderly or polymorbid patients with advanced prolapse, significantly improving objective prolapse measures and bladder voiding dysfunction including stress urinary incontinence, overactive bladder, and elevated postvoid residual volumes. Prior pelvic surgery, hysterectomy, diabetes, and factors such as reduced vaginal blood flow and abnormal collagen cross-linking contribute to increased recurrence risk by impairing wound healing and tissue remodelling. The procedure carries low perioperative morbidity, with urinary tract infections being the most common complication, making colpocleisis a valuable native-tissue repair option for appropriate candidates seeking durable prolapse correction.

title: Opioid-Associated Postoperative Nausea and Vomiting in Women Undergoing Laparoscopic Hysterectomy: A Network Meta-Analysis
summary:
# Summary

Laparoscopic hysterectomy for endometriosis and related benign conditions provides significantly reduced postoperative pain and morbidity, yet effective pain control remains essential for patient comfort and functional recovery. While opioids remain cornerstone analgesics for moderate-to-severe postoperative pain, they commonly cause opioid-induced nausea and vomiting (OINV), a concern particularly relevant to women who experience higher incidence rates than men. A network meta-analysis comparing analgesic regimens revealed that oxycodone/ketorolac combination therapy carried the highest OINV risk, whereas opioid-sparing multimodal approaches using dexmedetomidine/ketorolac, fentanyl/ketorolac, or remifentanil/ketorolac demonstrated significantly lower risk, with dexmedetomidine/ketorolac showing the most pronounced benefit through non-opioid mechanisms of action. Route of administration also influenced outcomes, with epidural delivery producing lower OINV incidence than intravenous administration. These findings underscore the importance of tailoring postoperative analgesia to multimodal, opioid-sparing strategies that minimize opioid exposure and adverse events, thereby improving patient recovery trajectories and satisfaction following laparoscopic hysterectomy.

title: From Theatre to Intensive Care: A Narrative Review of Life-Threatening Complications in Gynaecological Laparoscopy and Hysteroscopy
summary:
# Summary

Laparoscopy and hysteroscopy offer significant clinical advantages including faster recovery, reduced postoperative pain, and shorter hospital stays compared to open surgery; however, both techniques carry distinctive life-threatening complication risks that require careful management. Hysteroscopy presents physiological challenges primarily related to distension media absorption, causing fluid overload, electrolyte imbalance, and hyponatraemia that can cascade into pulmonary oedema and cerebral oedema, ultimately leading to cardiovascular collapse. Laparoscopy carries mechanical and cardiopulmonary risks including visceral and vascular injuries, with pneumoperitoneum-induced effects such as carbon dioxide insufflation triggering vagal stimulation and bradycardia, potentially culminating in asystolic cardiac arrest. Critical complications like venous gas embolism during endometrial ablation cause acute right-heart strain, hypoxaemia, and cardiovascular collapse, while bowel perforation from blind trocar entry or thermal injury risks delayed diagnosis leading to peritonitis and sepsis. Risk factors for major vascular injury include prior abdominal surgery and obesity, with trocar insertion during instrument manipulation being particularly hazardous. Recognition and prevention strategies, including proper patient selection, adherence to safe surgical technique, and vigilance for early signs of complications, are essential to mitigate morbidity and mortality in these minimally invasive gynaecological procedures.

title: Incidence of severe subcutaneous emphysema after robotic versus conventional laparoscopic surgery: A retrospective single-center study
summary: This retrospective study examined the incidence of severe subcutaneous emphysema (SE), a complication where gas enters subcutaneous tissues, following robotic versus conventional laparoscopic surgery across 13,837 procedures. Robotic surgery was associated with a significantly higher incidence of severe SE (0.23%) compared to laparoscopic surgery (0.05%), though both rates remained relatively low. Gastric surgery carried the highest risk for severe SE (0.27%), and female patients, particularly those with advanced age, lean body mass index, and prolonged operative times, demonstrated substantially elevated risk compared to male counterparts. While SE is typically benign, severe cases can progress to serious complications including hypercarbia, acidosis, airway obstruction, and potentially fatal cardiopulmonary collapse, with cervical involvement occasionally causing extubation failure. Additional risk factors identified included high pneumoperitoneal pressure (≥15 cmH₂O), operative duration exceeding 200 minutes, chronic lung disease, and steroid use. The reverse Trendelenburg position used during gastric procedures may facilitate upward spread of SE to the neck region, highlighting the importance of recognizing this complication in high-risk patient populations undergoing minimally invasive surgery.

title: A systematic review and bibliometric analysis of robot vs. laparoscopic surgery in urogynecology: current trends and future directions
summary: Robot-assisted surgery has emerged as a prominent minimally invasive approach for urogynecological procedures including hysterectomy, endometriosis surgery, myomectomy, and pelvic organ prolapse treatment, with sacrocolpopexy being the most frequently studied procedure. While robot-assisted sacrocolpopexy demonstrates high anatomic cure rates for apical prolapse and low complication incidence, it is associated with longer operating times and increased postoperative pain compared to laparoscopic approaches. Advanced technological features such as 3D visualization and surgical instruments with seven degrees of freedom enhance precision and field visualization during minimally invasive procedures. The clinical landscape has shifted away from transvaginal mesh procedures, with significant declines observed in posterior compartment and enterocele surgeries, while anterior compartment mesh operations have remained relatively stable. Mesh-related complications such as erosion remain comparable between robot-assisted and laparoscopic approaches. The increasing prevalence of pelvic floor disorders driven by aging populations and rising obesity rates continues to expand the demand for these advanced surgical interventions in urogynecology.

title: Effect of hysterectomy on risk of thyroid cancer and benign thyroid disorders in South Korean women
summary: This study demonstrates that hysterectomy is associated with significantly increased risks of thyroid cancer (hazard ratio 1.35) and benign thyroid disorders including goiter and nontoxic nodules in South Korean women. The underlying mechanism appears to involve estrogen's role as a potent growth factor for thyroid cells, as both benign and malignant thyroid tissues express estrogen receptors and estrogen stimulation promotes thyrocyte proliferation. Notably, the elevated thyroid cancer risk was observed primarily in women undergoing hysterectomy without concurrent adnexal surgery (bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy), suggesting that preservation of ovarian estrogen production may contribute to thyroid pathology. The study also identifies iodine deficiency as a potential cofactor that amplifies estrogen-dependent effects by reducing estrogen metabolism and increasing estrogen-induced transcription in thyroid tissue. Additionally, machine learning analysis identified connections among endometriosis, benign breast disease, and non-toxic goiter, suggesting shared estrogen-dependent pathways across reproductive and thyroid tissues. These findings highlight an important but previously underrecognized association between gynecologic surgery and thyroid disease risk in reproductive-age women.

title: Identification of Risk Factors in Postoperative Urinary Dysfunction for Rectal Gastrointestinal Stromal Tumors
summary: This study identifies risk factors for postoperative urinary dysfunction following surgical treatment of rectal gastrointestinal stromal tumors (GISTs), rare mesenchymal tumors arising from interstitial cells of Cajal that commonly harbor c-Kit (CD117) or platelet-derived growth factor receptor alpha mutations. Tumors larger than 50 mm and male sex emerged as independent risk factors for postoperative urinary dysfunction, with larger tumors requiring partial or complete resection of pelvic autonomic nerves essential for urinary control. Preoperative targeted therapy with imatinib achieved a 33% median tumor shrinkage rate, improving the feasibility of complete autonomic nerve preservation and reducing postoperative urinary complications through organ-sparing surgical approaches including laparoscopic surgery and trans-anal total mesorectal excision. Complete surgical resection (R0) is the curative standard, with recurrence rates increasing dramatically with incomplete margins (R0: 3.75% versus R1: 23.1% versus R2: 100%). Postoperative urinary dysfunction was managed pharmacologically with parasympathomimetic agents and α-blockers when voiding efficiency remained below 50%. These findings demonstrate that tumor size reduction via neoadjuvant therapy significantly improves both oncologic outcomes and functional preservation in rectal GIST management.

title: Transvaginal Natural orifice transluminal endoscopic hysterectomy vs. abdominal laparoscopic approach in real-life practices: a retrospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective cohort study compares vaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic hysterectomy (vNOTES) to conventional laparoscopic hysterectomy for benign gynecologic conditions, including uterine fibroids and prolapse. The vNOTES approach, which combines vaginal hysterectomy benefits with laparoscopic visualization through a single natural orifice, demonstrated superior perioperative outcomes including significantly shorter operative times (69.5 vs. 117.4 minutes), reduced anesthesia duration (102 vs. 146.3 minutes), and lower blood loss (108.6 vs. 237.9 mL). Patients in the vNOTES group experienced substantially less postoperative pain, with lower visual analog scale scores (2.33 vs. 3.11) and fewer requests for intravenous analgesics, attributed to the absence of abdominal incisions and minimally invasive nature of the procedure. Importantly, vNOTES maintained a comparable safety profile with no significant differences in complication rates, conversions to alternative surgical routes, or transfusion requirements between the two groups. These findings support vNOTES hysterectomy as a safe, feasible, and advantageous alternative to conventional laparoscopy for treating benign gynecologic pathology, offering faster recovery and improved patient outcomes.

title: Electrosurgical hysteroscopic metroplasty for septate uterus: a single-center retrospective analysis of safety, adhesion rates, and perioperative outcomes
summary:
# Summary

Septate uterus is a common Müllerian anomaly strongly associated with adverse reproductive outcomes including markedly elevated miscarriage rates (dropping from ~94% to ~10% after treatment) and reduced live birth rates (~38% versus 85% in unaffected women), with emerging evidence linking this condition to endometriosis—notably present in approximately 77-93% of women with septal abnormalities. Hysteroscopic electrosurgical metroplasty, the current treatment of choice, significantly improves reproductive outcomes by restoring normal intrauterine anatomy and endometrial function, with substantial increases in clinical pregnancy and live birth rates following septum resection. Modern bipolar electrosurgical systems offer enhanced safety compared to monopolar techniques by permitting isotonic saline distension media, which virtually eliminates the risk of hyponatremia and fluid-overload syndromes that historically complicated monopolar procedures requiring non-conductive distension media. In this single-center retrospective series, electrosurgical septum dissection demonstrated excellent safety and efficacy, with no major complications (uterine perforations, major hemorrhages, or fluid-overload syndromes) and low intrauterine adhesion rates (4.3%) when combined with barrier agents such as hyaluronic acid gel. The simultaneous cutting and coagulating action of loop electrodes provides excellent hemostasis and visualization while minimizing adhesion formation compared to cold scissors techniques. These findings support electrosurgical hysteroscopic metroplasty as a safe, effective treatment for septate uterus that restores reproductive potential in infertile women with recurrent pregnancy loss and coexisting endometriosis.

title: Paracervical block during vaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery reduces postoperative pain and analgesic consumption: a retrospective cohort study
summary: This retrospective cohort study demonstrates that paracervical block (PBC) with 0.5% bupivacaine significantly enhances pain management and recovery outcomes in patients undergoing vaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery (vNOTES). The paracervical block reduced postoperative pain as measured by 24-hour visual analog scale scores (3 versus 4; P=0.02) and substantially decreased intraoperative opioid requirements compared to controls (0 versus 10 mg morphine equivalent; P=0.01). The vasoconstrictive properties of bupivacaine contributed to reduced intraoperative blood loss and improved surgical visibility, while lower opioid consumption facilitated earlier mobilization and recovery without delaying discharge. Notably, patients receiving paracervical block demonstrated superior sexual function recovery at one month postoperatively and experienced reduced dyspareunia, likely because pain relief allowed earlier normalization of sexual activity. By mitigating the sedation, nausea, and ileus associated with systemic opioids, paracervical block incorporation into vNOTES procedures enhances early postoperative recovery and patient satisfaction in this minimally invasive surgical approach for endometriosis.

title: Proceedings of the 28th Ain Shams Obstetrics and Gynecology International Conference (ASOGIC), Cairo, Egypt, August 2025
summary: This study from the Ain Shams Obstetrics and Gynecology Conference examined multiple surgical and pharmacological interventions relevant to operative gynecology, with several findings applicable to laparoscopic hysterectomy for endometriosis. Regarding anesthesia and pain management, bupivacaine demonstrated superior analgesic efficacy compared to lignocaine for postoperative pain control, while diathermy incision reduced both postoperative pain scores and blood loss compared to scalpel techniques. For infection prevention, both low-dose (1g) and high-dose (2g) cefazolin prophylaxis proved equally effective at preventing surgical site infections, with gentamicin irrigation showing additional benefit in reducing wound infection rates. In hemorrhage prevention, carbetocin emerged as the most effective agent with the lowest adverse effect profile (95.5% without adverse effects), substantially outperforming oxytocin and misoprostol for postpartum hemorrhage prevention. Finally, monofilament suturing was associated with significantly better surgical outcomes, including lower isthmocele rates (17.1% versus 37.1%) and greater residual myometrial thickness preservation compared to multifilament sutures, suggesting improved tissue integrity and reduced postoperative morbidity after hysterectomy.

title: Surgical success following robotic upper urinary tract reconstruction, results from a global network of healthcare organizations
summary: This multi-institutional study evaluated outcomes of robotic-assisted upper urinary tract reconstruction across three procedure types—pyeloplasty, ureteral reimplantation, and ureteroplasty—achieving success rates of 89-92% in treating ureteral obstruction. Multiple comorbidities emerged as significant risk factors for surgical complications and failure, including diabetes mellitus, smoking history, elevated body mass index, and prior radiation therapy, with diabetes showing the strongest association with both drain placement and surgical failure across procedure types. Age over 50 years and history of radiation therapy were particularly predictive of complications in ureteral reimplantation patients, while smoking and BMI were primary risk factors for pyeloplasty failures. The robotic-assisted approach offers improved tissue handling precision and access to the pelvis and retroperitoneum compared to open surgery, establishing it as the new standard of care with demonstrated efficacy in both primary procedures and secondary/redo cases of ureteropelvic junction obstruction. Chronic kidney disease and vascular disease also contributed to poor wound healing and recovery outcomes, highlighting the importance of comprehensive preoperative risk assessment in patient selection for these minimally invasive reconstructive procedures.

title: Efficacy and safety of TU-LESS retroperitoneal lymphadenectomy with manipulator-free laparoscopic hysterectomy in endometrial cancer staging
summary: This study evaluated transumbilical laparoendoscopic single-site (TU-LESS) surgery combined with manipulator-free laparoscopic hysterectomy for endometrial cancer staging and demonstrated superior efficacy and safety compared to conventional multi-port laparoscopic approaches. The TU-LESS technique significantly improved para-aortic lymph node harvesting (12.7 versus 8.3 nodes), a critical prognostic factor in endometrial cancer, while the manipulator-free approach eliminated tumor spillage and cervical contamination that can compromise oncologic outcomes. Patients undergoing TU-LESS experienced substantially improved recovery profiles, including reduced postoperative pain, shorter hospital stays, faster drainage removal, and lower tumor marker levels (CA-125, CA-199, CEA, and HE4), with superior quality of life scores across physical, cognitive, emotional, and role domains. The retroperitoneal approach provided enhanced visualization and direct exposure of pelvic and para-aortic lymph node regions while minimizing bowel interference and concealing surgical scars. Safety outcomes were excellent, with no major intraoperative complications including vascular, urinary, or gastrointestinal injuries, and no recurrences observed at 24-month follow-up in either group. These findings support TU-LESS retroperitoneal lymphadenectomy with manipulator-free hysterectomy as an effective and safe oncologic approach for endometrial cancer staging that combines minimally invasive benefits with optimal lymphatic staging.

title: Comparison of Laparoscopic and Laparotomic Total Hysterectomy in Terms of Patient Satisfaction and Cosmetic Outcomes
summary:
# Summary

This study compared laparoscopic and open (laparotomic) total hysterectomy for benign gynecologic conditions including endometriosis, uterine prolapse, abnormal uterine bleeding, and myoma uteri, focusing on patient satisfaction and cosmetic outcomes. Laparoscopic hysterectomy demonstrated substantial clinical advantages over open surgery, significantly reducing scar area, blood loss, postoperative pain, analgesic requirements, hospitalization duration, and perioperative morbidity, while improving overall recovery and cosmetic outcomes. However, the laparoscopic approach required longer operative time and presented a marginally elevated risk of ureteral injury. Patient satisfaction assessments revealed a notable paradox: laparoscopic patients reported greater satisfaction with their surgical scars according to the POSAS Patient Scale and had higher Body Image Questionnaire scores, indicating better cosmetic perception; yet paradoxically, these same patients were less satisfied with their overall body image. The study identified that multiport laparoscopy, which creates five visible scar sites on the anterior abdominal wall, reduced patient satisfaction compared to approaches minimizing port number, suggesting that surgical technique refinements such as single-incision laparoscopy and subcuticular skin closure further enhance cosmetic outcomes and patient acceptance.

title: Novel Application of the Lagis LapBase Cap in Transvaginal NOTES Hysterectomy: Surgical Outcomes and Cost-Effectiveness in 107 Cases
summary:
# Summary

Transvaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery (vNOTES) hysterectomy with the Lagis LapBase Cap represents an effective minimally invasive approach for treating benign gynecologic disease including leiomyoma, adenomyosis, and cervical dysplasia, demonstrating a 94.8% success rate across 107 cases. The procedure consistently outperforms traditional laparoscopic hysterectomy, achieving reduced postoperative pain, minimal scarring, faster recovery, shorter surgical time, decreased blood loss, and shorter hospitalization with high rates of same-day discharge and low complication rates. The Lagis LapBase Cap proved to be a cost-efficient tool that provided stable surgical access and ease of use with a reliable seal during the procedure. Surgical complexity was primarily influenced by uterine weight exceeding 300 grams and multiple prior cesarean sections, which correlated with longer operative time and greater blood loss, while preoperative anemia demonstrated the strongest association with extended hospital stay. Preoperative dynamic transvaginal ultrasound assessment of the "sliding sign" effectively identified patients with pouch of Douglas involvement or deep endometriosis, improving patient selection and surgical outcomes. Overall, vNOTES hysterectomy represents a safe, feasible, and economical advancement in minimally invasive gynecologic surgery that can be successfully applied even in challenging cases with larger uteri.

title: Patient satisfaction after outpatient hysteroscopy: a retrospective descriptive study
summary:
# Summary

Outpatient hysteroscopy is an effective minimally invasive procedure for treating intrauterine and endometrial pathology, achieving clinical remission in approximately 70% of patients with a high overall satisfaction rate of 9/10. The procedure offers significant advantages including avoidance of hospitalization, reduced costs, and lower complication rates such as cervical tears and uterine perforations compared to conventional approaches. However, pain experienced during the procedure—particularly during cavity entry—significantly impacts patient satisfaction and is the primary reason for dissatisfaction among the 11.5% of patients with low satisfaction scores. Two key modifiable factors enhance patient satisfaction: pre-procedure counseling and comprehensive information provision about potential discomfort, which together reduce preoperative anxiety and improve outcomes. Only 65.3% of patients reported receiving sufficient information beforehand, highlighting an opportunity to improve satisfaction through better patient education and managing expectations, particularly given that misinformation from social media can amplify fear and anxiety prior to the procedure.

title: Comparison of Cold-Cup Biopsy Versus Resection Biopsy in the Early Detection of Detrusor Muscle Invasion in the Case of Bladder Tumor
summary: This study compares cold-cup biopsy (CCB) and resection biopsy techniques for detecting detrusor muscle invasion in bladder tumors, which is critical for accurate staging and clinical management of muscle-invasive bladder cancer. The detrusor muscle layer serves as the key anatomical boundary distinguishing non-muscle-invasive from muscle-invasive disease, and its absence from biopsy specimens is associated with under-staging, residual disease, and earlier recurrence. Cold-cup biopsy demonstrated superior performance, retrieving detrusor muscle in 86.5% of cases compared to 65.4% with resection biopsy, primarily because the mechanical cutting action preserves tissue architecture and eliminates thermal artifacts that compromise staging accuracy in resection techniques. Loop resection biopsies carry risks of bladder perforation and thermal artifact-induced invasion mimicry, whereas cold-cup biopsy offers lower perforation risk while enabling high-precision sampling with minimal cautery artifact. Surgeon expertise, tumor location, and specimen handling—including en bloc resection and separation of superficial from deep tissue—independently influence detrusor muscle retrieval and overall staging integrity. The findings support cold-cup biopsy as the preferred approach for early detection of detrusor muscle invasion, potentially reducing re-intervention rates through improved diagnostic accuracy.

title: A comparative analysis of hysterectomy outcomes: robotic single-port vs. traditional transvaginal NOTES approaches
summary:
# Summary

This comparative analysis evaluated robotic single-port vaginal NOTES (RSP-vNOTES) hysterectomy versus traditional vaginal NOTES (T-vNOTES) for treating benign gynecologic conditions, particularly endometriosis. The RSP-vNOTES approach, utilizing the Da Vinci single-port robotic system with wristed instruments capable of 360° exploration, demonstrated superior technical advantages including improved three-dimensional visualization, eliminated tremors, enhanced surgical accuracy, and reduced nerve and vascular damage risk. While the RSP-vNOTES group experienced longer operative times and higher pain levels at three weeks post-surgery, they achieved significantly lower estimated blood loss and intraoperative complications, with only one conversion to laparotomy compared to six in the T-vNOTES group. RSP-vNOTES proved particularly advantageous for complex endometriosis cases involving excision, adhesiolysis, and bowel procedures, offering superior precision for dissection around critical structures such as vasculature, bowel, and ureters. Overall, robotic single-port vaginal hysterectomy appears safer and more feasible for managing complicated endometriosis with concurrent pathology, though the technique requires acceptance of prolonged operative time to achieve superior hemostasis and reduced complications.

title: Complications of total laparoscopic hysterectomy: with vs. without a uterine manipulator
summary: Total laparoscopic hysterectomy (TLH) is an effective minimally invasive surgical approach for treating benign and malignant gynecological conditions, including endometriosis, offering advantages such as reduced postoperative pain, minimal blood loss, early ambulation, and shortened hospital stays compared to open surgery. The use of a uterine manipulator during TLH generally reduces intraoperative complications and improves surgical exposure and tissue traction, though it carries concerns regarding peritoneal tumor cell dissemination in malignant cases. Conversely, TLH without a uterine manipulator can be safely performed, including in patients with large uterine masses exceeding 250 grams, and does not increase rates of bladder, ureteral, or intestinal injuries; this approach offers benefits such as reducing uterine perforation risk and preventing tumor spillage while improving pelvic anatomy visualization. However, omitting the uterine manipulator requires extensive knowledge of retroperitoneal anatomy, meticulous dissection technique, and structured training programs due to the steeper learning curve associated with this approach. Recent systematic reviews have demonstrated that the presence or absence of a uterine manipulator does not affect disease-free survival or overall survival outcomes in malignant cases, supporting the safety of either technique when performed by experienced surgeons.

title: The Hugo™ RAS system in gynecologic robotic surgery: a systematic review of current applications
summary:
# Summary

The Hugo™ Robotic-Assisted Surgery (RAS) system is increasingly adopted for gynecologic procedures, with hysterectomy representing the main indication (64.9% of cases) and endometriosis among the primary conditions treated (20.7% of cases), alongside fibromatosis, adenomyosis, adnexal masses, and complex cases including deep infiltrating endometriosis. Across 340 procedures analyzed, the system demonstrated a favorable safety profile with low conversion rates (0.22% to laparotomy, 1.12% to laparoscopy), minimal intraoperative complications (0.90%), and rare postoperative complications, with no bowel injuries observed. Operative metrics showed a weighted mean operative time of 141.84 minutes, docking time of 5.90 minutes, and estimated blood loss of 110.75 mL, with hospital stay averaging 2 days. Surgeon experience significantly reduced both docking and console times, and prior robotic platform experience shortened the learning curve, though system malfunctions including instrument failures and console errors occurred in some cases without compromising patient safety. The Hugo RAS system's open surgical console with glasses-based safety monitoring and enhanced instrument dexterity effectively mitigate technical and anatomical challenges in complex gynecologic procedures.

title: Exploratory Study of Cesarean Scar Healing After Interrupted Versus Continuous Sutures: Prospective Magnetic Resonance Imaging Assessment in Cynomolgus Monkeys
summary: This prospective magnetic resonance imaging study in cynomolgus monkeys demonstrates that double-layer interrupted sutures (DIS) produce superior cesarean scar healing compared to continuous suturing techniques. DIS preserved myometrial thickness and maintained higher uterine blood perfusion (measured by Ktrans values) at six months post-operatively, while continuous suturing was associated with increased niche formation—cavities that promote intrauterine fluid retention and compromise fertility. Post-cesarean adhesions emerged as a critical impediment to healing, reducing both uterine blood flow and residual myometrial thickness while mechanically hindering tissue remodeling and contributing to cesarean scar defects. The study highlights that adequate angiogenesis and preserved uterine perfusion are essential for effective wound healing, with myometrial thinning at the incision site increasing risks of uterine rupture in subsequent pregnancies and post-menstrual complications. These findings suggest that surgical suturing technique and adhesion prevention are modifiable factors that significantly influence long-term uterine scar integrity and function.

title: The Effect of Postoperative Modified Trendelenburg Position to Decrease Shoulder Pain after Laparoscopic Hysterectomy: A Randomized Controlled Trial
summary:
# Summary

Postoperative shoulder pain is a frequent complication of laparoscopic hysterectomy, primarily caused by CO2 insufflation used to create pneumoperitoneum. The mechanism involves CO2 stimulation of the phrenic nerve and conversion to carbonic acid on peritoneal surfaces, along with mechanical effects from residual gas pockets. This trial evaluated whether a modified Trendelenburg position maintained for 6 hours postoperatively would reduce shoulder pain, but found no statistically significant benefit compared to standard positioning. However, the study confirms that more effective interventions include active gas evacuation techniques, pulmonary recruitment maneuvers, intraperitoneal local anesthetics, manual abdominal compression, and deep breathing exercises. The findings underscore that while positional approaches alone may be insufficient, comprehensive multimodal strategies addressing gas residue—particularly through mechanical evacuation during surgery—remain critical for minimizing postoperative pain and improving recovery outcomes in laparoscopic hysterectomy for endometriosis.

title: Transvaginal Natural Orifice Transluminal Endoscopic Surgery (vNOTES) Hysterectomy in Overweight and Obese Patients: Pearls and Pitfalls
summary: Obesity presents significant challenges for hysterectomy in patients with gynecological pathology, including endometriosis and adenomyosis. Overweight and obese patients face increased risks of abnormal uterine bleeding, endometrial hyperplasia, and endometrial cancer, partly driven by elevated estrogen effects in adipose tissue. Patients with BMI ≥25 kg/m² undergoing vNOTES (transvaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic) hysterectomy experienced longer operative times, higher estimated blood loss, and extended hospitalization compared to normal-weight controls, with obese patients (BMI ≥30) showing even greater increases in operative and postoperative stay duration. vNOTES hysterectomy offers advantages including reduced postoperative pain, better cosmesis, and lower insufflation pressures compared to traditional laparoscopic approaches, though obesity-related factors such as increased intra-abdominal adipose tissue and poor Trendelenburg tolerance create technical difficulties. Additional patient factors like nulliparity and difficult vaginal access further prolonged operative time in the vNOTES population. Despite these challenges, vNOTES remains an effective treatment option for various hysterectomy indications including leiomyomas, adenomyosis, endometriosis, and endometrial malignancies.

title: Evaluating the Negative Appendicectomy Rate: A Retrospective Observational Cohort Study at a UK Teaching Hospital Trust
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective study examined negative appendicectomy rates (NAR)—unnecessary surgical removal of normal appendices—at a UK teaching hospital, finding an overall NAR of 18.4% that was significantly elevated in females (25.6%) and children (23.6%) due to diagnostic overlap with conditions including endometriosis, ovarian cysts, and pelvic inflammatory disease in reproductive-age women. Computed tomography imaging substantially reduces NAR compared to ultrasound, and the Appendicitis Inflammatory Response (AIR) score demonstrates superior diagnostic performance over the Alvarado score by incorporating inflammatory markers such as C-reactive protein and white cell count. Laparoscopic appendicectomy is strongly favored over open approaches, offering advantages in reducing surgical site infections, postoperative pain, and hospital length of stay, though 50% of patients with negative appendicectomy proceeded to surgery without preoperative imaging. The study highlights that structured diagnostic algorithms and validated clinical scoring systems are essential to reduce unnecessary surgery, particularly in female and pediatric populations where diagnostic uncertainty remains high despite advances in imaging technology.

title: Comparative analysis of postoperative outcomes following hysterectomy versus sacrocolpopexy: Insights from global federated health research network☆
journal: European Journal of Obstetrics & Gynecology and Reproductive Biology: X
summary: This comparative analysis reveals significant postoperative outcome differences between abdominal hysterectomy and sacrocolpopexy for treating gynecological conditions including endometriosis, fibroids, and pelvic organ prolapse. Hysterectomy patients experienced substantially higher postoperative opioid use (44.4% vs. 15.6%) and antimicrobial use (25.9% vs. 14.1%) compared to sacrocolpopexy patients, with notably elevated rates of opioid abuse and dependence (6.04% vs. 2.02%). The open abdominal hysterectomy approach, comprising the majority of procedures globally, was associated with elongated recovery times, greater opioid reliance, and higher infection risk, whereas minimally invasive sacrocolpopexy approaches correlated with shorter recovery and reduced pain. A key mechanistic difference is that sacrocolpopexy avoids vaginal cuff formation, eliminating a direct source of infection from vaginal microbiota, whereas hysterectomy creates this complication site. Appropriate antimicrobial stewardship and surgical planning, including prophylactic antibiotic regimens, effectively mitigated antimicrobial resistance despite higher antibiotic exposure in hysterectomy patients. These findings suggest that minimally invasive surgical approaches and procedure selection based on indication may substantially reduce postoperative complications and opioid-related morbidity.

title: Application value of laparo-endoscopic single-site surgery during the second and third trimesters complicated with gynecological pelvic mass
summary:
# Summary

This study compares laparo-endoscopic single-site (LESS) surgery with conventional laparoscopic (CL) and transabdominal (TA) approaches for treating gynecological pelvic masses during pregnancy in the second and third trimesters. LESS surgery demonstrated superior postoperative outcomes, including significantly lower pain scores, improved scar appearance, and better cosmetic results through its transumbilical single-port approach compared to the multiport CL technique. Compared to traditional TA surgery, laparoscopic approaches (both LESS and CL) substantially reduced operative time, hospital stay duration, bed rest requirements, and inflammatory markers (CRP and WBC), while TA surgery was associated with higher complication rates including fever and incision infections. Importantly, all three surgical approaches proved safe for fetal development, with no significant differences in infant growth, neurodevelopmental outcomes, or neuromotor scores at one year of follow-up, supporting the use of minimally invasive surgery during pregnancy when indicated. The study confirms that LESS surgery effectively combines minimal invasiveness with improved cosmesis, making it a viable alternative to conventional approaches in experienced hands, though it may be contraindicated in cases with significant pelvic surgery history or endometriosis.

title: Single port access laparoscopic fundusectomy, more efficient uterus-sparing surgical method for multiple fibroids in patient with no pregnancy plan
summary:
# Summary

Single-port access laparoscopic fundusectomy represents a more efficient uterus-sparing alternative to traditional myomectomy for women with multiple uterine fibroids who do not desire future pregnancy. Compared to multiple myomectomy, fundusectomy significantly reduces operating time (150.71 versus 172.13 minutes), estimated blood loss (198.29 versus 314.04 mL), and postoperative hemoglobin and hematocrit changes, while removing substantially more fibroids per procedure (average 16.06 versus 9.75 fibroids). This approach is particularly indicated for patients with large uteri, coexisting adenomyosis, or concurrent pelvic endometriosis, conditions where uterine volume reduction provides additional clinical benefit. The single-port technique offers additional cosmetic advantages through a single umbilical incision while preserving uterine structure, and patients demonstrated symptom improvement in menstrual volume and dysmenorrhea with no fibroid recurrence requiring reoperation within two years of follow-up.

title: Protocol for a study comparing the efficacy of hysterectomy in patients with enlarged uterus via TU-LESS and vNOTES: a single-blind, prospective randomised clinical trial
summary: Uterine enlargement, commonly caused by adenomyosis and uterine fibroids, produces significant clinical morbidity including constipation, urinary frequency, dysmenorrhea, and irregular menstruation, with menorrhagia potentially leading to anemia. Hysterectomy represents the definitive treatment for this condition, though traditional open laparotomy carries substantial risks including high complication rates and prolonged recovery periods. This study protocol compares two minimally invasive surgical approaches—transumbilical laparoendoscopic single-site surgery (TU-LESS) and transvaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery (vNOTES)—both of which offer significant advantages over conventional surgery. Both techniques demonstrate shortened recovery times, reduced postoperative pain, minimal scarring, and fewer complications compared to laparotomy, with vNOTES additionally associated with shorter hospital stays and minimal blood loss. The trial aims to establish the relative efficacy of these two minimally invasive approaches for hysterectomy in patients with uterine enlargement, representing an important advancement in optimizing patient outcomes through reduced surgical morbidity.

title: Robot-assisted versus standard laparoscopic approach of total hysterectomy for deep infiltrating endometriosis and adenomyosis (ENDORAS TRIAL): study protocol for a randomised controlled trial
summary:
# Summary

This randomized controlled trial (ENDORAS) compares robot-assisted and standard laparoscopic hysterectomy for treating deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIE) and adenomyosis, two closely associated conditions that cause chronic pelvic pain and infertility in reproductive-age women. Although total laparoscopic hysterectomy is the established gold standard for DIE management, it carries significantly higher complication risks, including urinary tract damage and other postoperative morbidities. Robot-assisted laparoscopic surgery offers potential advantages over conventional laparoscopy through improved ergonomics, three-dimensional visualization, enhanced dexterity, and better preservation of pelvic autonomic nerves during precise dissection of deep lesions. Evidence from French multicentre cohorts demonstrates that robot-assisted approaches reduce blood loss, intraoperative complications, and postoperative complications while potentially improving urinary and sexual function. This study aims to rigorously evaluate whether robotic assistance meaningfully improves surgical outcomes and quality of life for endometriosis patients undergoing hysterectomy.

title: Minimizing Risk of Retroperitoneal Major Vascular Injury with Abdominal Wall Elevation Device during Abdominal Entry for Laparoscopic and Robotic Surgery
journal: JSLS : Journal of the Society of Laparoscopic & Robotic Surgeons
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that abdominal wall elevation devices significantly improve safety during laparoscopic and robotic surgical entry for gynecological procedures, including those for endometriosis management. Using the LevaLap 1.0 elevation device with a 3-mm direct trocar technique achieved first-attempt entry in all 25 patients without any major vascular or visceral injuries and with no failed entries. The standardized elevation approach effectively reduces the risk of retroperitoneal major vascular injury, a serious complication associated with traditional abdominal entry methods. By mechanically elevating the abdominal wall during closed technique direct entry, the device increases the distance between the trocar insertion site and underlying critical structures, thereby minimizing injury risk. These findings suggest that abdominal wall elevation devices represent a feasible and effective safety measure for reducing entry-related complications in minimally invasive gynecological surgery.

title: Opioid Use After Robotic Assisted versus Laparoscopic Endometriosis Surgery
journal: JSLS : Journal of the Society of Laparoscopic & Robotic Surgeons
summary: This study compared postoperative opioid use patterns between robotic-assisted and laparoscopic surgical approaches for endometriosis treatment. Laparoscopic surgery was associated with a significantly higher probability of postoperative opioid prescription fills compared to robotic-assisted procedures. Conversely, robotic-assisted surgery demonstrated a reduced likelihood of opioid prescription fills in the postoperative period, with this benefit particularly evident among patients with baseline pain disorders who received robotic-assisted intervention. The choice of surgical modality appears to substantially impact postoperative pain levels and the consequent need for opioid pain management. These findings suggest that robotic-assisted surgery may offer a clinically meaningful advantage in reducing postoperative opioid requirements for endometriosis patients, potentially contributing to lower opioid exposure and associated risks in this surgical population.

title: Efficiency and Diagnostic Utility of CO2 Cystoscopy After Laparoscopic Gynecologic Surgery
journal: JSLS : Journal of the Society of Laparoscopic & Robotic Surgeons
summary:
# Summary

CO2 cystoscopy represents a safe and efficient diagnostic alternative for detecting lower urinary tract injuries following laparoscopic gynecologic surgery, including procedures for endometriosis. Compared to conventional saline cystoscopy, CO2 significantly reduces both overall procedural duration and time required to visualize ureteral jets, streamlining the intraoperative assessment process. A key practical advantage of CO2 cystoscopy is the elimination of the need for ancillary agents such as diuretics and chromatic dyes, simplifying the diagnostic workflow without compromising sensitivity for injury detection. In this series, the technique successfully identified and facilitated immediate repair of bladder and ureteral injuries, demonstrating its clinical utility. The evidence supports CO2 cystoscopy as an efficient diagnostic tool that maintains diagnostic effectiveness while reducing procedural complexity and operative time.

title: Reproductive Outcomes After Laparoscopic Treatment of Endometriosis in Women with Infertility
journal: JSLS : Journal of the Society of Laparoscopic & Robotic Surgeons
summary: Laparoscopic surgery for endometriosis significantly improves fertility outcomes in women with infertility, with pregnancy rates of 82% in patients with prior failed embryo transfer (FET) and 78% in treatment-naive patients, 31% of whom conceived spontaneously. Age emerged as a significant predictor of pregnancy success, with younger patients demonstrating better outcomes in the non-prior FET group, while endometriosis stage did not substantially influence reproductive results. Patients presenting with endometriomas were preferentially referred for preemptive surgery before pursuing FET, suggesting that the presence of ovarian endometriotic cysts influences clinical management decisions. The data support laparoscopic surgery as an essential component of fertility management in endometriosis, particularly for optimizing outcomes in both treatment-naive and previously unsuccessful FET cycles. The high conception rates across both cohorts, with 97% of successful pregnancies in the prior FET group requiring assisted reproductive technology, indicate that surgical treatment of endometriosis enhances overall fertility potential regardless of prior treatment history.

title: Single-port robotic surgery in gynecology: first experience in German-speaking countries using the Da Vinci SP system in benign andneoplastic diseases
summary:
# Summary

This study reports the first experience with the da Vinci SP single-port robotic surgical system in German-speaking countries for treating both benign and malignant gynecologic conditions, including endometriosis management via hysterectomy, salpingo-oophorectomy, and pectopexy procedures. The da Vinci SP system demonstrated significant advantages over multiport robotic systems, including faster docking time, reduced blood loss, and prevention of incisional hernias, while maintaining the minimally invasive benefits of reduced tissue trauma, shorter hospital stays, and improved precision and visualization in the confined pelvic space. Among 62 gynecologic procedures, endometriosis surgery required 192.5 minutes on average, with operative time being the primary factor associated with hospital stay length, while hysterectomy took approximately 119 minutes. The single-port approach improved cosmesis and postoperative discomfort compared to multiport surgery, with low pain scores (2.9 ± 1.4 on postoperative day one) and no incisional hernias, blood transfusions, intraoperative complications, conversions to open surgery, or postoperative fever observed. Patient factors such as age and BMI were not significantly associated with operative duration, though previous abdominal operations were associated with increased postoperative pain. Overall, the da Vinci SP platform demonstrated robust versatility and safe outcomes for minimally invasive gynecologic surgery in this initial German experience.

title: Thyroid cancer treatment among adolescents and young adult women and reproductive outcomes: a population-based cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This population-based cohort study demonstrates that thyroid cancer treatment in adolescents and young adult women is significantly associated with increased infertility diagnosis and early menopause, with combined total thyroidectomy plus radioactive iodine therapy (TOT+RAI) carrying the highest risk (weighted relative risk 1.34 for infertility and 1.54 for early menopause). The mechanisms underlying these reproductive effects involve thyroid hormone imbalances disrupting thyroid-gonadal signaling and menstrual cyclicity; notably, radioactive iodine therapy additionally causes declines in anti-Müllerian hormone and transient amenorrhea. Interestingly, premature ovarian insufficiency rates were similar between treatment and control groups across all surgical approaches, and overall childbirth rates remained comparable, suggesting that despite higher infertility diagnoses, many women retained reproductive capacity. Residual thyroid tissue after less-than-total thyroidectomy may paradoxically confer greater early menopause risk through subclinical hypothyroidism, while levothyroxine replacement following total thyroidectomy appears to stabilize thyroid-gonadal signaling. These findings indicate that thyroid cancer treatment poses measurable reproductive risks in young women, particularly through hormonal mechanisms rather than permanent ovarian damage.

title: Association between hysterectomy for benign indications and the risk of breast cancer: a systematic review and meta- analysis
summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis examined the association between hysterectomy for benign indications and breast cancer risk, with findings that vary substantially by study design and surgical approach. Case-control studies demonstrated that hysterectomy reduces breast cancer risk (RR = 0.839), an effect particularly pronounced with bilateral oophorectomy, hysterectomy before age 45, and follow-up periods exceeding 10 years, whereas cohort studies showed no significant association. The protective mechanism appears to involve hysterectomy-induced early menopause (advancing menopause by 3.7 years on average) and alterations in hormone levels, including reduced prostaglandin F2α and elevated FSH/LH, with specific reduction in hormone receptor-positive breast cancer incidence but not hormone receptor-negative disease. However, this protective effect is limited to certain populations and may be reversed by postoperative hormone therapy, as hysterectomy combined with estrogen-progestin therapy significantly increased breast cancer risk. The analysis also identified complex relationships where reproductive and lifestyle factors—such as early menarche, low parity, alcohol consumption, and body mass index—are shared risk factors between endometriosis and breast cancer, suggesting that the relationship between hysterectomy for endometriosis and breast cancer risk remains nuanced and context-dependent.

title: Fertility sparing surgery for malignant ovarian sex-cord stromal tumors: long-term obstetric and oncologic outcomes
journal: Journal of the Turkish German Gynecological Association
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian sex-cord stromal tumors (SCSTs) are rare neoplasms accounting for less than 5% of ovarian malignancies but generally have a favorable prognosis compared to epithelial ovarian cancers. Most cases present at early stage I disease, with granulosa cell tumors being the most common subtype (65.7%), typically occurring in younger women or perimenopausal women depending on type. This study demonstrates that fertility-sparing surgery (FSS) achieves excellent oncologic outcomes with 5-year disease-free survival of 85.7% and overall survival of 97.1%, comparable to radical surgical approaches (87.0% disease-free survival), while preserving reproductive capacity. All patients maintained regular menstrual cycles post-treatment, and 36% achieved pregnancy with 50% resulting in live births, with outcomes unaffected by adjuvant chemotherapy or age over 35 years. Adjuvant chemotherapy showed limited therapeutic benefit for SCSTs and was associated with shorter overall survival, suggesting it may not be necessary for early-stage tumors managed with FSS.
