These findings establish FSS as a safe and appropriate treatment option for malignant ovarian SCSTs in selected patients, balancing excellent long-term oncologic control with favorable fertility and obstetric outcomes.

title: Feasibility of vNOTES hysterectomy in patients with enlarged uteri: a single-center experience
journal: Journal of the Turkish German Gynecological Association
summary: This study demonstrates that vaginally accessed natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery (vNOTES) hysterectomy is a feasible and safe minimally invasive approach for patients with enlarged uteri, including those with complicating factors such as obesity and prior abdominal surgery. vNOTES combines traditional vaginal hysterectomy with single-port laparoscopic magnified visualization and improved access to uterine vasculature, resulting in reduced postoperative pain, shorter operative times, and faster recovery compared to conventional open approaches. The procedure successfully treated various indications including fibroids, adenomyosis, abnormal uterine bleeding, and premalignant and malignant pathology, with particularly high feasibility for myomatous uteri and adnexal masses. The study cohort showed low complication and conversion rates (4.3% each), with manageable intraoperative and postoperative bleeding complications, and the approach proved safe even in patients with intra-abdominal adhesions or deep infiltrating endometriosis requiring conversion. These findings support vNOTES hysterectomy as an effective alternative to traditional open hysterectomy and standard minimally invasive approaches in appropriately selected patients with enlarged uteri.

title: Comparison of vaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery and vaginal hysterectomy for benign indications: a prospective randomized controlled study
summary:
# Summary

This prospective randomized controlled trial compared vaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery (vNOTES) with traditional vaginal hysterectomy (VH) for treating benign gynecological conditions, demonstrating that both techniques achieve comparable surgical outcomes for benign uterine pathology. The vNOTES approach offered distinct advantages including a scar-free abdomen, improved visualization and access to the adnexa, reduced tissue trauma, and lower postoperative pain requiring fewer nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs compared to VH. Notably, the vNOTES group demonstrated higher rates of bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy and required significantly less analgesic medication (median 2.0±1.0 versus 3.0±1.0 NSAIDs), likely attributable to the minimized tissue trauma inherent to the technique. Both vaginal approaches offered broader advantages over traditional laparoscopic surgery, including reduced blood transfusion requirements, quicker recovery, and a lower surgical carbon footprint, making them preferable for appropriately selected patients. The findings suggest that vNOTES represents an evolution of minimally invasive gynecologic surgery, offering improved cosmesis and reduced postoperative morbidity while maintaining equivalent efficacy to established vaginal hysterectomy in resource-rich settings with adequate training and equipment.

title: The effect of robot-assisted surgery on the gynecology patients’ experience and quality of life after surgery
summary: Robot-assisted gynecological surgery significantly improves postoperative outcomes and long-term quality of life in patients undergoing procedures for benign gynecological conditions including endometriosis, adenomyoma, leiomyoma, and ovarian cysts. The technique reduces postoperative pain, blood loss, and hospital stays while accelerating recovery, with vitality scores returning to baseline within three weeks compared to traditional laparoscopic approaches. Patients experienced initial declines in physical functioning, emotional well-being, and social functioning in the first week post-surgery, but these domains showed consistent improvement over the following weeks and months. Sexual function, measured by the Female Sexual Function Index, demonstrated transient impairment immediately post-surgery with decreases in arousal, lubrication, and orgasm domains, though these parameters gradually recovered over six months. Notably, patients undergoing less invasive procedures such as myomectomy and cystectomy recovered faster than those undergoing total hysterectomy. Overall, robot-assisted gynecological surgery offers superior long-term quality of life improvements, making it a transformative alternative for treating endometriosis and related gynecological pathology.

title: Real-world outcomes of robotic and non-robotic hysterectomy for endometrial cancer: insights from a national cohort
summary: This national cohort study demonstrates that robot-assisted hysterectomy (RAH) offers substantial perioperative and long-term advantages over open hysterectomy (OH) for treating endometrial cancer, particularly given that endometrial cancer incidence has escalated 132% over three decades due to obesity and aging populations. Robot-assisted surgery achieves superior outcomes through technical benefits including three-dimensional visualization and enhanced instrument precision, resulting in dramatically fewer transfusions (15.5% vs. 29.7%), shorter hospital stays (5.99 vs. 8.09 days), and markedly reduced overall complication rates (0.95% vs. 7.52% at one month postoperatively). Specific complications significantly reduced with RAH include vascular events, cardiac events, stroke, pneumonia, peritonitis, wound disruption, ileus, acute renal failure, surgical site infection, genitourinary complications, and sepsis. Open hysterectomy patients had substantially higher mortality hazard ratios (HR 1.39) and more advanced disease presentation, while RAH demonstrated comparable safety and outcomes to conventional laparoscopic hysterectomy with additional cost savings (NTD 65,992 vs. 130,281). These findings establish RAH as the preferred surgical approach for endometrial cancer, particularly for technically demanding cases complicated by endometriosis or extensive pelvic adhesions.

title: First Case of Robot-Assisted Surgery for Gynecologic Cancer at the Oncology Hospital, Centro Médico Nacional Siglo XXI
summary: This case report demonstrates that robot-assisted laparoscopic surgery is a safe and effective treatment option for early-stage endometrial cancer, offering significant clinical advantages over traditional open surgery. The robotic platform's enhanced three-dimensional visualization, tremor filtration, and articulated instruments enable superior surgical precision and facilitate complex dissections, particularly in patients with obesity, severe endometriosis, or extensive pelvic adhesions. Robot-assisted approaches are associated with reduced postoperative pain, lower blood loss, fewer postoperative complications, and shorter hospital stays compared to conventional laparotomy. In this case of a 49-year-old patient with moderately differentiated endometrioid adenocarcinoma presenting with postmenopausal vaginal bleeding and thickened endometrium on ultrasound, robotic surgery achieved successful oncological outcomes without compromising assessment of critical prognostic factors such as myometrial invasion, lymphovascular involvement, and nodal metastasis. The patient achieved disease-free survival at 30-month follow-up without requiring adjuvant therapy, underscoring the importance of timely diagnosis and accurate surgical staging. These findings support the adoption of robot-assisted surgery as a minimally invasive alternative for managing early-stage endometrial cancer in specialized centers.

title: Hugo™ robotically assisted surgery versus laparoscopic hysterectomy for benign gynecological diseases: a secondary data analysis of clinical records in Panama
summary: This secondary analysis compared Hugo™ robotically assisted surgery (RAS) with laparoscopic hysterectomy for benign gynecological conditions including endometriosis, adenomyosis, uterine myomatosis, and abnormal uterine bleeding. Hugo™ RAS demonstrated significant clinical advantages over laparoscopy, reducing operative time by 22% (76 versus 117 minutes) and substantially decreasing blood loss (30 versus 200 milliliters). Patients undergoing RAS experienced shorter hospital stays and lower readmission rates while maintaining equivalent surgical safety profiles. The robotic platform's superior precision, three-dimensional visualization, enhanced instrument dexterity, and ergonomic design reduced surgeon fatigue and hand tremors while improving surgical flexibility and feedback. Both minimally invasive approaches substantially reduced tissue damage and postoperative complications compared to open surgery, with the faster operative times in RAS further diminishing anesthesia-related risks and accelerating patient recovery.

title: Incidence, Detection, and Management of Urological Injuries in Obstetric and Gynecologic Surgeries: A Five-Year Observational Study
summary:
# Summary

Urological injuries represent significant complications in obstetric and gynecologic surgeries, with high morbidity and medicolegal implications, particularly during complex pelvic procedures including hysterectomy for endometriosis. Bladder injuries occur more frequently than ureteric injuries and are typically detected intraoperatively in most cases, allowing for immediate primary repair with suprapubic cystostomy and resulting in favorable outcomes. In contrast, ureteric injuries are often missed during surgery and present later as serious chronic complications including urinoma, strictures, fistulas, and potential renal loss. Total abdominal hysterectomy and laparoscopic-assisted procedures carry the highest risk of urological injuries, with risk further increased by surgeon inexperience, dense adhesions, and endometriosis. Prevention through meticulous surgical technique, intraoperative vigilance, cystoscopic evaluation in high-risk cases, and appropriate patient counseling are essential, while early intraoperative detection significantly reduces morbidity and hospital stay compared to postoperative identification requiring more complex interventions such as ureteric reimplantation or endoscopic stenting.

title: Vaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery (vNOTES) in myomectomy: a novel minimally invasive technique review
summary: Vaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery (vNOTES) represents an emerging minimally invasive approach for myomectomy that treats uterine leiomyomas while preserving fertility, offering significant clinical advantages over traditional trans-abdominal techniques. The procedure is associated with optimal cosmetic results, shorter hospital stays, earlier resumption of bowel function, reduced pain medication requirements, and avoidance of trans-abdominal wound complications. Key technical factors influencing outcomes include myoma size and location, with tumors exceeding 8 cm in diameter associated with increased intraoperative blood loss, and fundal or broad ligament locations presenting technical challenges due to instrument range limitations. The prone patient positioning enhances surgical visualization, streamlines procedural efficiency, and enables forehand suturing techniques, though it carries potential risks of elevated abdominal pressure and associated complications including cardiovascular dysfunction and visual disturbances, which can be mitigated with supportive padding. Important contraindications include rectovaginal endometriosis, prior pelvic radiation, and severe pelvic inflammatory disease, while high BMI and previous pelvic surgery are not exclusionary. Prophylactic antibiotic administration and strict vaginal disinfection are essential for infection prevention, as the vaginal entry route converts the surgical field into a contaminated wound.

title: The Impact of Treatment Setting on Periurethral Bulking
summary:
# Summary

Periurethral bulking (PUB) using polyacrylamide hydrogel effectively treats stress urinary incontinence, with approximately two-thirds of patients achieving symptom improvement, but treatment setting significantly impacts early clinical outcomes. Operating room–based procedures demonstrated superior success rates, with only 32.4% six-month treatment failure compared to 51.3% in office settings, conferring a 3.23-fold increased odds of early failure for office procedures. This difference appears attributable to procedural factors, as approximately 15% of office cases required abandonment due to patient discomfort with local anesthesia alone and anatomical challenges, whereas all operating room cases completed successfully under intravenous sedation. Multiple comorbidities including overactive bladder medication use, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, pelvic pain syndrome, and fibromyalgia independently associated with six-month treatment failure regardless of setting. By twelve months, the failure rate differential between settings narrowed substantially, suggesting office-performed procedures may ultimately achieve comparable long-term efficacy despite higher early failure rates, making PUB a minimally invasive option suitable for high-risk surgical candidates.

title: Roles of minimally invasive techniques in the management of epithelial ovarian cancer (Review)
summary: Epithelial ovarian cancer remains a leading cause of gynecological mortality, predominantly due to late-stage diagnoses, though multiple minimally invasive and targeted therapeutic approaches have improved outcomes. Laparoscopic and robotic-assisted surgical techniques enable optimal cytoreduction comparable to open surgery while reducing perioperative morbidity, blood loss, operative time, and hospital stays, with the added benefit of earlier initiation of adjuvant therapy. Contemporary treatment combines surgery with platinum-taxane chemotherapy and increasingly incorporates targeted therapies, including poly(ADP-ribose) polymerase (PARP) inhibitors such as olaparib and niraparib, which disrupt DNA repair and induce apoptosis, particularly in BRCA-mutated cancers, demonstrating substantial progression-free survival improvements. Combination approaches such as PARP inhibitors with bevacizumab (an anti-angiogenic agent targeting VEGF) or PD-1 checkpoint blockade have demonstrated additive antitumor activity and higher response rates in refractory disease. Neoadjuvant systemic therapy can reduce tumor burden and render disease more resectable for minimally invasive surgeons, facilitating fertility-preserving treatment options. Despite these advances, therapeutic resistance remains a notable concern, suggesting that combination therapy utilizing multiple targeted agents may provide more durable efficacy in managing this aggressive malignancy.

title: Intersectionality and women's empowerment in hysterectomy decisions: an inquiry using data from a large cross-sectional sample survey in India
summary: This cross-sectional study in India examines how sociodemographic and empowerment factors influence hysterectomy decisions, with endometriosis and fibroids identified as major clinical indications alongside excessive menstrual bleeding. The research reveals a complex intersectionality pattern where women's empowerment—operationalized through decision-making ability, household headship, and bank account ownership—paradoxically increases hysterectomy likelihood, suggesting these factors enable women to access surgical care. Protective factors include higher education, media exposure, reproductive health knowledge, and delayed first birth (after age 21), which reduce hysterectomy odds by 30-90%, indicating that health literacy and informed decision-making prevent unnecessary surgical intervention. Socioeconomic vulnerabilities significantly increase hysterectomy risk, with illiterate women showing 72 per 1,000 higher rates, rural residents 1.42 times higher odds, and poorer wealth status correlating with increased procedures. Notably, the richest wealth status showed the highest odds (2.25-fold increase), and religious/caste factors (Hindu religion and general caste) also associated with elevated hysterectomy rates. The findings underscore how women's empowerment intersects with healthcare access and knowledge to shape reproductive surgical outcomes, highlighting the need for balanced interventions that combine women's agency with health education and reproductive autonomy.

title: Bowel surgery for endometriosis-associated infertility: navigating amidst the certainty of the uncertainty
summary: Severe endometriosis with bowel involvement presents complex fertility challenges complicated by coexisting adenomyosis, which independently reduces implantation likelihood and live birth rates. Colorectal surgery to remove bowel endometriosis may reduce local inflammation and prevent complications such as occlusion or perforation, but carries significant operative risks including rectovaginal fistula, anastomotic leakage, and pelvic abscess, with complications correlating directly to surgical approach and surgeon experience. Conservative techniques such as shaving demonstrate lower complication rates compared to segmental resection, and centers performing more procedures annually report better outcomes. Bowel endometriosis is typically an indicator of advanced disease associated with extensive anatomical distortion, adhesions, and abdominopelvic inflammation that collectively impair fertility beyond the isolated effect of bowel lesions themselves. Adenomyosis and advancing maternal age emerge as independent negative prognostic factors for live birth, while placenta praevia risk is primarily attributable to adenomyosis rather than bowel lesions. The decision to proceed with colorectal surgery in infertile patients requires careful consideration of these multifactorial fertility determinants alongside surgical morbidity.

title: Robotic-assisted hysterectomy using DEXTER®: the first prospective multicentre study
summary: This prospective multicentre study evaluated the DEXTER robotic surgery system for hysterectomy, including cases of endometriosis and other benign gynecological conditions. The DEXTER platform combines minimally invasive benefits with robotic advantages such as improved ergonomics, enhanced dexterity with seven degrees of freedom per instrument, and 3D visualization including indocyanine green fluorescence. The system achieved excellent surgical outcomes with zero conversions to open surgery, zero intraoperative complications, and minimal blood loss (median 100 mL) despite high procedural complexity, including endometriosis resection and lymph node dissection in selected cases. The DEXTER's compact, modular design enables rapid integration into existing laparoscopic infrastructure with seamless transitions between robotic and conventional laparoscopy, maintaining sterility through a draped console and offering superior surgeon ergonomics and situational awareness compared to conventional robotic systems. Median operative time was 125.5 minutes with a median docking time of only 5 minutes, and patients were discharged after a median 2-day hospital stay. These findings demonstrate that DEXTER provides a safe and effective option for complex hysterectomy procedures while overcoming the limitations of larger robotic platforms through its flexible workflow integration and reduced facility requirements.

title: Surgical Outcomes of Low Anterior Resection Using the Senhance Surgical System: A Single‐Center Case Series
summary: This single-center case series evaluates the Senhance robotic surgical system for low anterior resection in rectal cancer treatment, a minimally invasive approach that combines laparoscopic principles with unique technological features including eye-tracking camera control and haptic feedback. The study demonstrates that Senhance-assisted low anterior resection achieves safe outcomes with minimal blood loss (median 0 mL), successful R0 resection in 14 of 16 cases, and no major postoperative complications, supporting the system's feasibility in gastrointestinal surgery. However, the procedure encountered notable technical limitations in deep pelvic dissection, with 31.3% of cases requiring conversion to standard laparoscopy due to difficulty with pelvic manipulation and instrument-camera interference that restricted surgical field exposure. While the system's lack of articulated instruments limited multidirectional adjustment in the confined pelvic space, short-term oncological outcomes remained acceptable despite these challenges. The findings suggest Senhance-assisted low anterior resection represents a viable alternative to traditional laparoscopy for rectal cancer surgery, though refinements in instrument articulation and consideration of hybrid approaches like transanal total mesorectal excision may improve outcomes in anatomically challenging cases.

title: Efficacy of laparoscopic versus open abdominal radical hysterectomy on stress response and urodynamic status in patients with early cervical cancer
summary:
# Summary

This study compared laparoscopic radical hysterectomy (LRH) with open abdominal radical hysterectomy (ARH) for early cervical cancer treatment, evaluating surgical outcomes, stress response, and urodynamic function. Laparoscopic approach significantly reduced surgical duration, intraoperative blood loss, hospitalization time, and complications while achieving superior lymph node dissection through improved visualization and precise operation. Importantly, LRH substantially attenuated the surgical stress response by reducing circulating stress hormones—serum angiotensin II, growth hormone, and cortisol—thereby minimizing immune suppression that could impair postoperative recovery. Additionally, LRH patients demonstrated improved urodynamic outcomes with increased bladder capacity, enhanced maximum urethral pressure, and reduced residual urine volume, alongside decreased frequency of lower urinary tract symptoms including urinary urgency, frequency, and incontinence. Despite requiring higher technical skills and associated costs, LRH provided greater clinical benefits during early postoperative recovery through minimized surgical trauma, reduced systemic stress response, and better preservation of urinary tract function.

title: Minimally Invasive Hysterectomy Approaches: Comparative Learning Curves and Perioperative Outcomes of Robotic Versus V-NOTES Techniques
summary:
# Summary

This comparative study evaluates robotic and vaginally natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery (V-NOTES) hysterectomy approaches for benign gynecological conditions, revealing distinct trade-offs between the two minimally invasive techniques. Robotic hysterectomy demonstrates superior hemostatic control with significantly lower postoperative hemoglobin decreases and reduced operative times, advantages attributed to its three-dimensional high-definition visualization, tremor filtration technology, and articulated instruments that enhance vascular control and surgical precision. Conversely, V-NOTES provides enhanced cosmetic outcomes, reduced postoperative pain, and shorter hospital stays by utilizing the vaginal route as a natural access point, though it carries a steeper learning curve with four distinct phases requiring experience at 14, 25, and 40 cases compared to robotic surgery's more predictable two-phase curve. The V-NOTES cohort experienced higher rates of larger uteri and associated complications including bladder injuries, increased blood loss, and longer operative times, while robotic surgery required collective team experience for optimal institutional efficiency. Both techniques prove safe and effective for appropriate patient selection, with robotic surgery favoring cases with smaller uteri and V-NOTES broadening indications for vaginal surgery in patients with larger uteri and prior abdominal surgery.

title: Indications and Limitations of vNOTES for the Surgical Staging of Early-Stage Ovarian Cancer: A Narrative Literature Review
summary: Vaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery (vNOTES) represents an emerging minimally invasive approach for gynecologic cancer staging that offers significant patient benefits but with important technical limitations. The technique enables multiple surgical procedures including hysterectomy, adnexectomy, omentectomy, and pelvic lymph node biopsies while achieving minimal blood loss, low complication rates, and reduced postoperative pain compared to traditional laparoscopic approaches. However, vNOTES is contraindicated in patients with severe adhesions, recto-vaginal fibrosis, endometriosis, or previous pelvic radiation, and requires careful patient selection and substantial operator experience with an estimated learning curve of 20–30 cases. While vNOTES shows promise for borderline ovarian tumors and early-stage endometrial cancer, it is inadequate for comprehensive staging of invasive epithelial ovarian cancer because it cannot accomplish paraaortic lymphadenectomy, which is required for proper oncologic staging. Current best practice reserves vNOTES primarily for stage I disease and borderline tumors in carefully selected patients, while emphasizing that the procedure should not serve as an independent staging tool for invasive ovarian malignancy without lymphadenectomy capability.

title: Concomitant Hysterectomy and vNOTES-Assisted Sacrocolpopexy: A Feasible and Safe Scarless Approach for Apical Prolapse Repair
summary:
# Summary

Pelvic organ prolapse causes significant morbidity including pelvic pressure, vaginal bulge, and urinary and bowel dysfunction, with uterine prolapse resulting specifically from loss of apical support. This study demonstrates that concomitant hysterectomy with vNOTES-assisted sacrocolpopexy—a scarless vaginal approach using high-definition endoscopy without abdominal incisions—provides effective apical prolapse repair while avoiding the visceral injury and adhesion risks associated with traditional abdominal sacrocolpopexy. At 12 months, the procedure achieved 73.3% objective anatomical success and 93.3% clinical success, with significant improvements across all pelvic organ prolapse quantification parameters, particularly in the apical compartment. The technique was associated with low morbidity, with all complications graded as minor (Clavien-Dindo Grade I–II), mesh exposure in only 6.7% of patients (successfully managed with topical estrogen), and de novo dyspareunia in 10%—rates comparable to laparoscopic and robotic approaches. Patients achieving anatomical success experienced lower postoperative pain, and age showed weak-to-moderate predictive ability for outcomes, while operative duration and blood loss did not significantly impact pain trajectories. The procedure offers a safe, feasible scarless alternative combining the precision of minimally invasive surgery with rapid recovery and durable apical support restoration.

title: Surgical Anatomy of Corona Mortis: A Literature Review and Its Significance in Minimally Invasive Surgery
summary:
# Summary

The corona mortis is a vascular anastomosis between the obturator and external iliac or inferior epigastric vessels located in the retropubic pelvic space, present in approximately 40-60% of the population for venous variants and 20-40% for arterial variants. Injury to these small vessels (mean diameter 2.8 mm) during minimally invasive procedures—including laparoscopic removal of deep infiltrating endometriosis of the obturator internus muscle, laparoscopic hernia repair, gynecological procedures, and mid-urethral sling placement—can cause severe or massive hemorrhage that may require conversion to open surgery. The anatomical diversity of the corona mortis, driven by variable origins of the obturator artery, has led to multiple classification systems describing arterial and venous subtypes. Prevention strategies include meticulous dissection of the retropubic region, reducing pneumoperitoneum pressure to enhance vessel visualization, and placing mesh tacks closer to the symphysis pubis to minimize vascular injury risk. Preoperative imaging with contrast-enhanced CT or MRI angiography can identify vascular variations when clinically indicated, though notably, the presence of corona mortis does not correlate with increased bleeding or mortality in pelvic trauma cases.

title: Abdominal wall blocks in robotic hysterectomy for endometrial cancer are associated with a modest reduction in the frequency of patients receiving post-operative intravenous opioids
summary: Robotic hysterectomy is an established safe and effective approach for treating endometrial cancer and can be combined with regional anesthesia techniques to optimize perioperative pain management. This study evaluated whether abdominal wall blocks reduce postoperative opioid requirements in patients undergoing robotic hysterectomy, finding that blocks were associated with a 15% lower risk of intravenous opioid use and a 17% reduction in IV hydromorphone administration. Patients receiving blocks were more frequently treated with multimodal analgesia, including non-opioid analgesics such as ibuprofen and oral opioid formulations like oral hydromorphone and hydrocodone/acetaminophen, consistent with enhanced recovery protocols in minimally invasive surgery. However, the modest reduction in IV opioid use did not translate to shorter hospital stays or reduced direct procedural costs, and some patients in the block group received increased fentanyl during surgery. These findings support abdominal wall blocks as part of a multimodal pain management strategy for robotic hysterectomy, though the clinical benefit appears limited in the perioperative setting.

title: Surgical outcome of benign cases with pelvic adhesions undergoing robotic total hysterectomy
journal: Journal of the Chinese Medical Association : JCMA
summary: This study demonstrates that robotic-assisted total hysterectomy with uterine artery ligation is safe and effective for treating benign gynecological conditions, even in patients with pelvic adhesions previously considered poor candidates for minimally invasive surgery. While patients with adhesions experienced longer docking times, uterine artery ligation times, and higher postoperative pain scores compared to those without adhesions, there were no significant increases in blood loss, intraoperative or postoperative complications, or conversion to laparotomy. Pelvic adhesions most commonly involved the posterior uterine wall and bilateral ovaries, with bowel-to-bowel and periumbilical adhesions being less frequent. Adhesion severity directly correlated with operative difficulty—patients with adhesion score 3 and multiple adhesion areas required significantly longer operative times. The findings support robotic surgery as a viable approach for complex hysterectomies in patients with stage III-IV endometriosis and severe adhesions, particularly as pelvic adhesions are multifactorial in origin, stemming from previous abdominal surgeries, infections, and endometriosis-related procedures such as myomectomy, cystectomy, and electrofulguration.

title: An audit of hysterectomy in a teaching hospital in India: Story of a decade
journal: Journal of the Chinese Medical Association : JCMA
summary: This decade-long audit of hysterectomy procedures at an Indian teaching hospital examines surgical indications, techniques, and long-term health outcomes. Symptomatic leiomyoma and pelvic organ prolapse were the leading indications for hysterectomy, with approximately 92% of procedures performed for benign gynecological conditions including adenomyosis. The study found that laparoscopic and vaginal approaches resulted in shorter hospital stays and lower complication rates compared to abdominal approaches, with hemorrhage requiring transfusions being the most frequent complication. Beyond immediate surgical risks, hysterectomy was associated with significant long-term health consequences including increased risks of cardiovascular disease, dementia, osteoporosis, incontinence, sexual dysfunction, and metabolic disorders such as diabetes and hypertension when oophorectomy was performed. The analysis also identified unexpected malignancies in a small percentage of cases originally diagnosed as benign, highlighting diagnostic challenges. The findings underscore the importance of exploring conservative treatment alternatives such as uterine artery embolization, endometrial ablation, and high-intensity focused ultrasound therapy before pursuing hysterectomy for benign uterine diseases.

title: Comparing outcomes of ovarian cystectomy by vaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery versus laparoendoscopic single-site surgery: A retrospective cohort study
journal: Journal of the Chinese Medical Association : JCMA
summary: This retrospective cohort study compared vaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery (vNOTES) with laparoendoscopic single-site surgery (LESS) for treating ovarian cysts, which commonly cause abdominal pain, menorrhagia, and risk of ovarian torsion. Endometriosis-associated ovarian cysts, which do not resolve spontaneously, require surgical removal as standard treatment. The vNOTES group demonstrated superior perioperative outcomes, including significantly shorter surgical duration (70 versus 100 minutes), reduced blood loss (64 versus 163 mL), and zero conversion rates compared to LESS's 8.5% conversion rate, largely attributed to severe pelvic adhesions. The LESS group had larger baseline cyst diameter, more endometriotic cysts, and greater pelvic adhesions requiring adhesiolysis, factors that correlated with prolonged surgical time. Both minimally invasive approaches provided superior pain reduction and cosmetic outcomes compared to open surgery, though both techniques were associated with limited visualization. These findings suggest vNOTES is a more efficient and safer approach for ovarian cystectomy in appropriately selected patients with endometriosis-related ovarian pathology.

title: Robotic assisted versus conventional laparoscopic ovarian suture reapproximation in ovarian cystectomy of ovarian endometriomas in preserving ovarian reserve
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian endometriomas (OMA) are cysts containing endometrial-like tissue that cause pelvic pain, dyspareunia, infertility, and reduced ovarian reserve. This study evaluated robot-assisted laparoscopic reapproximation of ovarian stroma (ROS-RAL), a novel suturing technique that preserves ovarian tissue by targeting only the vascular-rich stroma while avoiding the cortex, thereby preventing thermal and ischemic damage caused by traditional cauterization methods. The ROS-RAL approach demonstrated superior preservation of ovarian reserve compared to conventional laparoscopy (CL), as evidenced by significantly higher anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) levels at 1, 12, and 36 months postoperatively and a substantially lower decline in AMH over time. At 36 months, the ROS-RAL group exhibited a notably lower incidence of diminished ovarian reserve (27.54% versus the CL group), with multivariate analysis confirming ROS-RAL as a strong protective factor against postoperative ovarian reserve loss. Additionally, the ROS-RAL group showed zero recurrence of endometriomas compared to a 5.88% recurrence rate in the CL group, suggesting that this robotic-assisted approach offers both superior preservation of fertility potential and improved long-term surgical outcomes.

title: Feasibility of Laparoscopic Radical Colpectomy in Locally Advanced Vaginal Cancer: A Case Report and Literature Review
summary:
# Summary

Primary vaginal carcinoma, predominantly presenting as squamous cell carcinoma (85% of cases), is associated with persistent high-risk HPV infection, repeated mechanical trauma, and chronic inflammation, with clinical presentations including postcoital or postmenopausal vaginal bleeding and pelvic pain. This case report demonstrates that laparoscopic en bloc resection, combining bilateral pelvic lymphadenectomy, radical hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, ICG-guided sentinel lymph node dissection, and vulvectomy with clitoral preservation, is a feasible and minimally invasive surgical approach for selected patients with locally advanced vaginal cancer. The laparoscopic technique successfully achieved complete R0 resection while reducing postoperative morbidity and preserving pelvic floor function compared to open approaches, with laparoscopic closure of the vaginal cuff particularly reducing complications such as cuff dehiscence and infection. Prognostic factors including advanced FIGO stage and tumor size greater than 2-4 cm predict poor survival outcomes, underscoring the importance of primary surgical treatment, which demonstrates a survival advantage over radiotherapy alone. Emerging evidence supports neoadjuvant chemotherapy combined with radical surgery for locally advanced disease, positioning minimally invasive laparoscopic techniques as a promising alternative for comprehensive oncologic management of vaginal carcinoma.

title: Safety of Transvaginal Specimen Retrieval in Total Laparoscopic Hysterectomy for Nulliparous Women: A Retrospective Study
summary: Total laparoscopic hysterectomy with transvaginal specimen retrieval is a safe and effective minimally invasive approach for treating benign gynecologic diseases in nulliparous women, despite anatomical challenges including narrower vaginal dimensions and less distensible tissues in this population. Transvaginal retrieval using contained in-bag morcellation techniques significantly reduces operative and retrieval times compared to transabdominal approaches, while maintaining low complication rates with only minor vaginal wall lacerations and cuff infections observed. The use of protective devices such as the Smart Retractor Free Access, combined with standardized morcellation techniques including rotating the uterus within the bag and initiating incisions from the cervix, enhances both procedural safety and retrieval efficiency for larger uteri. These technical refinements allow successful extraction of substantially larger specimens than traditionally considered feasible through the vaginal route, with no conversions to abdominal retrieval required in the study cohort. Although transabdominal retrieval demonstrated slightly higher retrieval efficiency per minute and handled larger median uterine weights, the benefits of shorter operative times and comparable safety profiles make transvaginal retrieval a viable option when appropriate surgical experience and technique standardization are employed.

title: Healthcare utilisation across five years among adolescents following metabolic-bariatric surgery; a prospective observational study
summary:
# Summary

Metabolic and bariatric surgery effectively treats adolescent severe obesity but generates substantial healthcare utilization over a five-year follow-up period. Early postoperative complications within 30 days of surgery strongly predict both related and unrelated healthcare utilization events in subsequent years, indicating that perioperative complications have lasting consequences beyond immediate recovery. The most frequent surgery-related healthcare events involve gastrointestinal symptoms, skin or wound problems, hepatobiliary events, and nutritional complications, with Roux-en-Y gastric bypass specifically associated with oesophagogastric complications and gastrointestinal strictures. Baseline clinical factors including higher BMI, hypertension, and female sex correlate with increased admission rates and healthcare utilization, while paradoxically, baseline type 2 diabetes is associated with lower rates of subsequent healthcare events. Between the two primary surgical approaches, Roux-en-Y gastric bypass and vertical sleeve gastrectomy yield comparable rates of both related and unrelated healthcare utilization events overall, though sleeve gastrectomy patients without baseline diabetes experience higher unrelated healthcare utilization.

title: Physician-level variability in adopting opportunistic salpingectomy for ovarian cancer prevention at the time of hysterectomy
journal: International journal of gynecological cancer : official journal of the International Gynecological Cancer Society
summary: This study examines the adoption of opportunistic salpingectomy—the removal of fallopian tubes during hysterectomy—as a preventive strategy for reducing ovarian cancer risk. The analysis reveals substantial variability in physician adoption of this procedure, with institutional and geographic factors emerging as significant predictors. Physicians practicing at teaching hospitals and those located in the northeast or west regions demonstrated significantly higher adoption rates of opportunistic salpingectomy. Conversely, physicians serving populations with higher proportions of Medicaid patients exhibited lower adoption of this preventive approach. These findings suggest that implementation of ovarian cancer prevention strategies during hysterectomy is influenced not only by clinical evidence but also by institutional setting and patient population demographics, highlighting potential disparities in access to cancer prevention interventions.

title: Effects of hysteroscopic cold knife technique on the treatment of endometrial polyps and its protective effect on the endometrium
summary: Hysteroscopic cold knife technology effectively treats endometrial polyps through a minimally invasive surgical approach that significantly outperforms conventional methods across multiple clinical parameters. The technique reduces operative time, intraoperative blood loss, postoperative vaginal bleeding duration and volume, and hospital stay duration compared to control treatments. Beyond direct symptom reduction, the cold knife technology provides protective endometrial effects by suppressing inflammatory markers (C-reactive protein, TNF-α, and IL-6) measured one month post-operatively, and improving uterine vascular perfusion through decreased resistance and pulsatility indices at three months. Notably, the procedure reduces estrogen and progesterone receptor expression while limiting postoperative endometrial thickening, resulting in a substantially lower recurrence rate of 3.77% compared to controls. These findings demonstrate that hysteroscopic cold knife technology combines surgical efficacy with endometrial preservation, offering both immediate procedural advantages and long-term protective benefits for patients undergoing polyp treatment.

title: Ureteral stricture: current treatment algorithm and key surgical principles in the robotic upper urinary tract reconstruction era
summary:
# Summary

Robotic-assisted surgical management of ureteral strictures represents a significant advance in upper urinary tract reconstruction, offering multiple advantages over open and laparoscopic approaches including reduced operative times, shorter hospital stays, and decreased blood loss. Treatment selection depends on stricture characteristics: endoscopic management with balloon dilation achieves success rates exceeding 50% for simple strictures less than 2 cm, but complex, iatrogenic, or radiation-associated strictures require surgical intervention using techniques such as ureteroureterostomy, ureteroneocystostomy, psoas hitch, Boari flap, or ileal ureter reconstruction. Key surgical principles include using healthy tissue with minimal dissection to preserve vascular supply, creating tension-free anastomoses, ensuring adequate drainage, and employing intraoperative ureteroscopy for quality control and stricture delineation. Indocyanine green imaging enhances tissue visualization and vascularity assessment to prevent ischemic complications and stricture recurrence, while preoperative ureteral stent removal reduces inflammation and edema, improving intraoperative visualization and lowering recurrence rates. The robotic platform's high reproducibility and shorter learning curve facilitate widespread adoption while maintaining the precision needed for complex reconstructions in previously irradiated or densely scarred tissues.

title: The Role of Natural Orifice Specimen Extraction in Modern Surgery: A Narrative Review
summary:
# Summary: Natural Orifice Specimen Extraction in Modern Surgery

Natural orifice specimen extraction (NOSE) represents a hybrid minimally invasive surgical technique that combines established laparoscopic resection with specimen retrieval through natural body orifices, thereby eliminating the need for additional abdominal incisions. By avoiding abdominal wall trauma, NOSE significantly reduces postoperative pain, wound complications including infection and incisional hernia formation, and hospital stay length compared to conventional laparoscopic approaches, while accelerating return of bowel function and improving patient body image scores. NOSE has been successfully applied in colorectal surgery for conditions such as rectal and sigmoid cancer, diverticulitis, and inflammatory bowel disease, as well as in gynecologic procedures including hysterectomy and adnexal mass removal. Despite theoretical concerns about bacterial contamination during transanal or transvaginal extraction, large meta-analyses of over 3,000 patients demonstrate that NOSE achieves equivalent lymph node harvest, resection margins, and long-term oncologic outcomes without increased anastomotic leakage rates. While potential complications of natural orifice extraction include anal sphincter injury with transanal retrieval and vaginal injury or dyspareunia with transvaginal approaches, these occur at acceptable rates. NOSE overcomes the technical limitations that restricted adoption of the earlier NOTES concept and represents an important advance in minimally invasive surgical practice.

title: Technical proficiency assessment of robotic intracorporeal single-stapling colorectal anastomosis using video-based RA-CUSUM
summary: The robotic intracorporeal single-stapling anastomosis (RiSSA) technique was developed to overcome limitations of the conventional double-stapling method, which carries risks of anastomotic leakage and stricture formation due to overlapping staple lines. RiSSA enables fully intracorporeal anastomosis with tension-free reconstruction, particularly advantageous in challenging pelvic cases, while robotic platforms provide enhanced visualization, improved instrument articulation, and superior ergonomics to facilitate this technically demanding procedure. Using video-based RA-CUSUM learning curve analysis, this study demonstrated that RiSSA has a definable learning trajectory, with technical efficiency stabilizing after approximately 17 cases as measured by console time, pure RiSSA interval, and total purse-string suture duration. Notably, purse-string suturing skills were acquired earlier than overall procedural fluency, suggesting differential skill acquisition during the learning phase. However, the occurrence of one anastomotic leak in the late phase underscores that technical proficiency does not entirely eliminate procedural risks, indicating the importance of ongoing vigilance even after mastering the technique. The pure RiSSA interval emerged as a reproducible metric for evaluating anastomotic proficiency and supporting objective skill assessment in robotic colorectal surgery training programs.

title: Does Retroperitoneal vNOTES Sentinel Lymph Node Mapping Represent a Feasible Staging Option in Presumed Early-Stage Endometrial Cancer?
summary: Retroperitoneal vaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery (vNOTES) represents a feasible and safe alternative to systematic lymphadenectomy for sentinel lymph node (SLN) mapping in early-stage endometrial cancer staging. This minimally invasive approach provides direct retroperitoneal access to pelvic lymph node basins while avoiding abdominal incisions, resulting in reduced postoperative pain, shorter hospitalization (median 2 days), and minimal blood loss (median 60 mL). Using methylene blue as the tracer agent, the technique achieved bilateral SLN mapping in 87.8% of 98 patients, with predominant nodal involvement in the obturator fossa (43.9%), and successfully identified nodal metastases in 12.8% of carcinoma cases, all prompting appropriate adjuvant therapy including chemotherapy and radiotherapy. The procedure demonstrated excellent safety outcomes with a 6.1% overall complication rate (5.1% major morbidity), including no disease recurrences at 12-month follow-up and zero conversions to open surgery. For the 12.2% of patients with unilateral mapping requiring side-specific lymphadenectomy, no additional metastatic non-sentinel nodes were identified, validating the algorithm's effectiveness. These findings establish retroperitoneal vNOTES as a superior staging strategy for presumed early-stage endometrial cancer, combining the oncologic accuracy of SLN mapping with the recovery benefits of minimally invasive surgery.

title: Overcoming the Three-Dimensional Complexity of Vulvar Defects: A Stepwise, Multi-Flap Approach
summary:
# Summary

This surgical article describes a stepwise, multi-flap reconstruction approach for managing complex three-dimensional vulvar and perineal defects arising from malignancies such as extramammary Paget's disease and squamous cell carcinoma. The authors employ a hierarchical flap strategy using the gluteal fold VY advancement flap as the primary workhorse, augmented by gracilis muscle flaps, mons pubis rotation flaps, medial thigh VY advancement flaps, and other local tissue transfers tailored to specific anatomical defects. Key advantages of the multi-flap approach include reduced infection risk, improved tissue padding and pliability, and superior functional outcomes compared to single large flap reconstruction. Perioperative management emphasizes urinary and fecal diversion via catheter and colostomy to maintain wound cleanliness, topical negative pressure wound therapy to promote flap adherence, and systematic management of post-operative infections with topical antifungals and intravenous antibiotics. Postoperative rehabilitation incorporates biofeedback exercises and self-digital dilatation to optimize anal sphincter coordination and tissue functionality following colostomy reversal.

title: Health-related Quality of Life in Patients Undergoing Ileal Ureter Replacement for Extensive Ureteral Stricture: A Prospective Multi-institutional Study
summary:
# Summary

Ileal ureter replacement (IUR) is the definitive surgical treatment for extensive ureteral strictures, which commonly result from radiotherapy, iatrogenic injury during lithotripsy, or urinary tuberculosis and cause significant morbidity including chronic flank pain, recurrent urinary tract infections, and renal dysfunction. Patients with extensive ureteral strictures present with substantially diminished health-related quality of life (HRQoL) and heightened psychological distress, particularly due to long-term dependence on nephrostomy tubes and ureteral stents. This prospective multi-institutional study demonstrated that IUR produces significant improvements across multiple HRQoL domains within 12 months of surgery, including physical function, bodily pain, general health perception, vitality, social function, mental health, and role limitations, with 96.3% of patients reporting overall improvements. Additionally, IUR restored renal function as evidenced by significant reductions in serum creatinine levels and reduced the prevalence of anxiety symptoms from 73.2% preoperatively to substantially lower postoperative levels. The procedure was associated with acceptable safety, with Clavien-Dindo grade III–IV complications occurring in 9.8% of patients, and benefits were sustained through minimally invasive surgical approaches including robotic and laparoscopic techniques.

title: Fluorescence-Guided Surgery in a Latin American Population: Patient Experiences in a Private Academic Hospital in Panama
summary:
# Summary

Fluorescence-guided surgery utilizing indocyanine green (ICG) represents a significant advancement in intraoperative visualization, with demonstrated benefits across multiple surgical disciplines including cholecystectomy, colorectal, and breast cancer procedures. ICG functions by binding to albumin in the bloodstream and undergoing hepatic uptake and biliary excretion, enabling real-time fluorescent visualization of critical anatomical structures including the biliary tract, bile ducts, cystic ducts, blood vessels, and ureters. This study of a Latin American patient population documented that fluorescence-guided surgery substantially reduces surgical complications through enhanced anatomical identification, preventing serious adverse events such as bile duct injuries, ureteral injuries, and anastomotic leaks. Notably, the technique improved intraoperative confidence and decision-making, though inflammatory conditions such as acute cholecystitis with elevated white blood cell counts and C-reactive protein levels occasionally impaired anatomical identification. Expert consensus supported the use of near-infrared fluorescent cholangiography and multi-fluorophore approaches for comprehensive vessel and anatomical visualization, positioning fluorescence-guided surgery as a valuable tool in contemporary surgical practice.

title: Serous Ovarian Cancer Following Opportunistic Bilateral Salpingectomy
summary: This study demonstrates that opportunistic bilateral salpingectomy (OBS)—surgical removal of the fallopian tubes during gynecologic procedures—substantially reduces the risk of serous ovarian cancer by approximately 80% compared to standard hysterectomy alone. High-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC), which accounts for roughly 70% of ovarian cancers, originates primarily in the fallopian tubes rather than the ovaries themselves, establishing the fallopian tube epithelium as a critical site of malignant transformation. Among patients without fallopian tubes following OBS, only 23% of identified ovarian carcinomas were HGSC compared to 68% in a historical cohort with intact fallopian tubes, providing robust evidence for the preventive mechanism. These findings support OBS as an effective primary prevention strategy for the most common and lethal form of ovarian cancer, suggesting that removal of fallopian tubes should be considered during routine hysterectomy procedures, particularly in patients undergoing surgery for benign gynecologic conditions such as endometriosis. The data indicate that preserving the fallopian tubes carries a substantially elevated risk for serous ovarian malignancy, fundamentally shifting the risk-benefit analysis for this anatomic preservation approach.

title: Day‐Case Laparoscopic Hysterectomy: A Successful Pilot in Regional New Zealand
journal: The Australian & New Zealand Journal of Obstetrics & Gynaecology
summary: This pilot study demonstrates that day-case total laparoscopic hysterectomy is a viable surgical approach for managing severe endometriosis in a regional New Zealand setting. The procedure successfully reduced severe menstrual symptoms and substantially improved patients' quality of life, with participants reporting restoration of normal activities and reduced work absenteeism that had previously been debilitating. The surgical protocol incorporated enhanced recovery after surgery (ERAS) principles, which facilitated rapid patient discharge and recovery. The cohort included patients with diverse body mass indices and comorbidities alongside significant intraoperative pathology, demonstrating the technique's applicability across varied patient populations. These findings support day-case laparoscopic hysterectomy as an effective and efficient treatment option for endometriosis, potentially reducing healthcare burden while delivering meaningful improvements in patient outcomes.

title: Tunnel method in laparoscopic single-position nephroureterectomy for women: preserving the uterine round ligament during distal ureter management and bladder cuff excision
summary:
# Summary

This article describes the tunnel method for laparoscopic nephroureterectomy in women with upper urinary tract urothelial carcinoma, emphasizing preservation of the uterine round ligament during distal ureter and bladder cuff management. Transection of the uterine round ligament causes uterine retroversion, which predisposes women to retrograde menstruation and subsequent endometriosis, while also increasing risks of pelvic organ prolapse, adhesions, and infertility through fallopian tube compression and cervical elevation. The tunnel method circumvents these complications by preserving the round ligament through a natural anatomical plane, thereby preventing retroversion-associated pathology while maintaining effective oncological resection of the tumor and reducing postoperative adhesions. Additionally, performing single-position transperitoneal laparoscopic nephroureterectomy improves surgical efficiency and field exposure compared to prone positioning, avoiding cardiopulmonary stress. This approach is particularly critical for women of reproductive age, as it achieves both cancer control and preservation of reproductive and pelvic function.

title: Comparison of the effects of total laparoscopic hysterectomy and total abdominal hysterectomy on ovarian reserve and sexual function: a non-randomised prospective study
summary:
# Summary

This prospective study compared the effects of total abdominal hysterectomy (TAH) and total laparoscopic hysterectomy (TLH) on ovarian reserve and sexual function in women with benign uterine conditions including fibroids, abnormal uterine bleeding, and adenomyosis. Both surgical approaches caused comparable reductions in ovarian reserve over six months, characterized by increased follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH: 6.57–7.73 mIU/mL for TAH, 5.89–6.96 mIU/mL for TLH) and decreased estradiol levels (TAH: 184.05–157.20 pmol/L; TLH: 163.60–122.17 pmol/L), with changes attributed to disrupted ovarian blood supply from cauterization of the mesosalpinx during surgery. Both procedures also significantly reduced sexual function scores on the Female Sexual Function Index (FSFI), with TAH declining from 25.77 to 24.46 and TLH from 22.87 to 21.14, despite hormonal changes not being significantly associated with sexual function outcomes. While laparoscopic approach offered advantages including shorter hospital stays and better wound healing, it did not provide superior protection of ovarian function or sexual wellbeing compared to open surgery. These findings underscore that hysterectomy fundamentally compromises ovarian function and sexual health regardless of surgical technique, leading to earlier menopause and reduced quality of life measures in the postoperative period.

title: Oblique subcostal transversus abdominis plane block versus transmuscular quadratus lumborum block for postoperative analgesia and quality of recovery in laparoscopic gynecological surgery: a prospective randomized controlled double-blind trial
summary:
# Summary

Postoperative pain following laparoscopic gynecological surgery arises from multiple sources, including trocar incisions, pneumoperitoneum, abdominal wall distention, and pelvic dissection, which can lead to inadequate analgesia, prolonged recovery, and chronic pain if not properly managed. This randomized controlled trial compared two regional anesthesia techniques—the transmuscular quadratus lumborum (TQL) block and the oblique subcostal transversus abdominis plane (OSTAP) block—both administered with bupivacaine as part of a multimodal pain management strategy alongside paracetamol, tenoxicam, and tramadol as needed. The TQL block demonstrated superior postoperative analgesia by significantly reducing tramadol requirements (29.4% relative difference), paracetamol consumption, and visual analog scale scores during movement compared to the OSTAP block, likely due to its broader dermatome coverage from T4 to L1. However, both blocks produced comparable pain scores at rest and similar quality of recovery scores at 24 hours postoperatively, indicating equivalent overall functional recovery despite differences in analgesic consumption. These findings support the use of regional anesthesia techniques, particularly the TQL block, as effective components of multimodal analgesia for reducing opioid requirements and improving pain control in laparoscopic gynecological procedures.

title: Recovery After Transcervical Fibroid Ablation Versus Minimally Invasive Myomectomy for Symptomatic Uterine Fibroids: A Randomised Controlled Trial
summary: This randomized controlled trial compared transcervical fibroid ablation (TFA) using radiofrequency energy with minimally invasive myomectomy (MIM) for treating symptomatic uterine fibroids. TFA demonstrated substantial recovery advantages, with patients returning to normal activities in 5.5 days compared to 13 days for MIM, while achieving discharge readiness in approximately one-third the time (22.9 versus 58.9 hours). The procedure time for TFA was roughly half that of MIM (51 versus 95 minutes), and patients experienced significantly less postprocedural pain requiring fewer opioids (25.9% versus 49.2% opioid utilization). Recovery benefits extended across nine measured metrics including household tasks, work, driving, and sexual function. Although MIM remains a reasonable option for specific fibroid subtypes where fertility preservation through surgical removal is prioritized, and both techniques have been associated with successful pregnancy outcomes, TFA offers a less invasive incisionless alternative that substantially reduces recovery burden and productivity loss while maintaining comparable safety profiles.

title: Non-placement of nasogastric tube versus early removal of nasogastric tube in the enhanced recovery after surgery of hepatocellular carcinoma: a case control study
summary:
# Summary

This case control study evaluated nasogastric tube (NGT) management strategies in hepatocellular carcinoma patients undergoing hepatectomy within an enhanced recovery after surgery (ERAS) protocol. Non-placement of NGT significantly reduced postoperative complications including atelectasis and pneumonia, while substantially decreasing patient discomfort through lower rates of nasopharyngeal irritation, nausea, vomiting, and sore throat compared to early removal strategies. Longer operative time, history of prior abdominal surgery, and open surgical approaches were independent risk factors for requiring NGT reinsertion, with right hepatectomy showing higher reinsertion rates than left hepatectomy or central bisectionectomy. Minimally invasive approaches including laparoscopic and robotic surgery had substantially lower reinsertion requirements compared to open procedures. The study demonstrates that routine NGT avoidance aligns with ERAS principles and improves patient comfort and clinical outcomes following hepatic resection for cancer.

title: Endoscopic management of ureteral injuries arising from gynecologic procedures
summary: Ureteral injuries during gynecologic procedures, particularly hysterectomies and laparoscopic surgeries, occur in 0.1–2.5% of cases and represent a significant clinical challenge in endometriosis management. Risk factors include prior pelvic surgery, adhesions, endometriosis, and anatomical anomalies, with distal ureteral injuries accounting for 91% of cases. While delayed diagnosis and management lead to serious complications including strictures, hydronephrosis, fistulas, and chronic inflammation requiring surgical reconstruction, early endoscopic intervention offers substantially superior outcomes with an 80% success rate compared to 33% with delayed treatment. Early retrograde endoscopic stent placement significantly reduces stricture incidence and the need for invasive procedures while minimizing operative time and hospital stays compared to surgical repair. Intraoperative recognition and prompt endoscopic management are critical, as 87% of injuries initially go unrecognized, leading to sepsis, urinoma, and fibrotic remodeling that mandates later reconstruction. The timing of intervention outweighs stent design in determining success, making rapid diagnosis and treatment within three months of injury essential for optimal patient outcomes.

title: Urological Injuries Following Gynecologic and Obstetric Surgery: Incidence, Diagnosis, and Outcomes from a 10-Year Retrospective Cohort
summary:
# Summary

This 10-year retrospective cohort study of 16,100 gynecologic and obstetric procedures identified 223 urological injuries (1.3% incidence), with bladder injuries being the most frequent complication (62.3%), predominantly associated with obstetric procedures and cesarean sections, while ureteral injuries (28.7%) occurred more frequently during malignant gynecologic surgeries. Key risk factors for urinary tract injuries include surgical technique, prior operations with adhesions, anatomical distortion from malignancy, and deep endometriosis. Critically, bladder injuries demonstrated high intraoperative recognition rates (98.6%), whereas ureteral injuries and genitourinary fistulas were frequently missed intraoperatively, with many ureteral injuries becoming clinically apparent only 48–72 hours postoperatively. Early recognition and timely intervention significantly reduce morbidity, while delayed diagnosis leads to severe complications including vesicovaginal and ureterovaginal fistulas presenting with pelvic pain, urinary incontinence, and hematuria. Open surgical approaches carried higher complication rates than minimally invasive techniques, and management strategies included double-layer suture repair for bladder injuries and ureteroneocystostomy with percutaneous nephrostomy for ureteral injuries.

title: Safety and feasibility of concomitant ventral hernia repair and total laparoscopic hysterectomy: A single-centre experience of over 1000 cases
summary: This study demonstrates the safety and feasibility of performing concomitant ventral hernia repair using intraperitoneal onlay mesh (IPOM) technique alongside total laparoscopic hysterectomy in over 1000 patients. Laparoscopic ventral hernia surgery has become standard care, offering improved patient outcomes and reduced hospital stays compared to staged procedures, which expose patients to multiple anesthesia exposures and prolonged recovery. Women face a 3-5% higher lifetime risk of developing ventral hernias compared to men, primarily due to obesity and multiparity, with untreated hernias risking serious complications including obstruction, incarceration, and strangulation. The peritoneal immune response, mediated by macrophages, lymphocytes, and mesothelial cells producing interleukins, along with diaphragmatic lymphatic absorption, helps clear bacteria and prevents infection, though mesh infections caused by Staphylococcus and Streptococcus remain a consideration. The study achieved excellent clinical outcomes with composite mesh placement combined with mandatory sac excision and defect approximation, resulting in only a 0.55% hernia recurrence rate and 0.094% mesh infection rate through meticulous sterile technique. Proper painting and draping, instrument sterilization, and changing trocars and gloves between procedures are critical preventive measures that collectively support the safety profile of this combined laparoscopic approach.

title: Frozen embryo transfer outcomes in patients with prior cesarean delivery: a focus on endometrial lining parameters
summary:
# Summary

Prior cesarean delivery is associated with inferior frozen embryo transfer (FET) outcomes compared to vaginal delivery, with reduced clinical pregnancy rates (60.5% vs. 64.1%) and live birth rates (49.5% vs. 53.0%), despite comparable endometrial thickness measurements between groups. The mechanism underlying this reduced fertility appears related to cesarean scar complications, particularly isthmocele formation, which occurs in more than half of post-cesarean patients and significantly increases the risk of intracavitary fluid accumulation—a condition that occurs in approximately 40% of patients with isthmoceles and is associated with higher FET cycle cancellation rates (2.22-fold increase). Proposed mechanisms for the reduced implantation potential include altered endometrial microbiota, reduced uterine contractility due to scarring, and compromised endometrial function that manifests as fluid accumulation rather than endometrial thinning. The adverse effect of prior cesarean delivery on FET outcomes persists even when examining euploid embryos and across both programmed and natural cycle preparations, suggesting the cesarean scar itself fundamentally alters endometrial receptivity beyond simple thickness parameters.

title: Prospective cohort study: vascular clamping of uterine artery combined with sacral ligament plication in complex hysterectomy
summary: This prospective cohort study evaluates a combined surgical technique for complex laparoscopic hysterectomy in patients with endometriosis, adenomyosis, and uterine fibroids, comparing Hem-o-lok clip closure of the uterine artery trunk combined with uterosacral ligament plication versus conventional methods. The combined technique significantly reduces intraoperative blood loss, operative time, hospitalization costs, and postoperative drainage parameters while avoiding thermal complications associated with electrocoagulation and blind coagulation, which risk ureteral injury. Mechanistically, uterosacral ligament plication elongates and elevates the vaginal vault to prevent pelvic organ prolapse, with the combined group demonstrating superior long-term outcomes including lower pelvic organ prolapse rates (6% versus 24% at 3 years) and maintained vaginal length. At three-year follow-up, the combined group exhibited improved pelvic floor function (lower PFDI-20 scores) and significantly better sexual quality of life (higher FSFI scores: 25.2 ± 2.6 versus 20.3 ± 4.1). These findings demonstrate that the combined technique of vascular clip closure with uterosacral ligament plication provides superior surgical efficiency, faster recovery, and sustained improvements in pelvic floor and sexual function compared to conventional hysterectomy approaches for complex endometriosis cases.

title: Reproductive Outcome After Laparoscopic Ovarian Endometrioma Stripping With Volumetric Hydrodissection
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluates volumetric hydrodissection (HD), a fluid-based tissue separation technique using only saline, as an alternative to hybrid HD during laparoscopic enucleation of ovarian endometriomas. Volumetric HD preserves intact endometrial cysts and prevents removal of mature ovarian cortex tissue compared to classic stripping, thereby potentially protecting ovarian reserve. While both volumetric and classic stripping approaches achieved similar spontaneous conception rates (approximately 63%), volumetric HD resulted in fewer miscarriages (21% versus 36.3%) and reduced cauterization events, which are associated with ovarian damage. Although laparoscopic cystectomy improves spontaneous pregnancy rates compared to ablation, the procedure generally reduces ovarian follicle numbers and decreases anti-müllerian hormone (AMH) levels, particularly in bilateral endometrioma cases. The findings suggest that volumetric hydrodissection may provide a more tissue-sparing surgical approach to endometrioma treatment, balancing reproductive outcomes with preservation of ovarian function in infertile women seeking pregnancy.

title: Long-Term Outcomes After Laparoscopic vs Open Adhesiolysis for Small Bowel Obstruction
summary: This long-term comparative study evaluated laparoscopic versus open adhesiolysis for treating adhesive small bowel obstruction, which accounts for the majority of small bowel obstructions requiring surgical intervention. Both approaches effectively treat adhesive obstruction, but neither demonstrated clear superiority at five-year follow-up in terms of recurrence rates, incisional hernia incidence, or quality of life measures. While laparoscopic adhesiolysis offered faster initial recovery and reduced short-term complications including wound infections and hospital stay, the laparoscopic approach showed equivalent or slightly higher long-term small bowel obstruction recurrence rates, potentially due to occult adhesions remaining outside the visualized obstructed site. Notably, incisional hernias occurred exclusively in patients whose laparoscopic procedures were converted to open surgery, not in those completed entirely laparoscopically, suggesting the conversion itself carries increased hernia risk. The findings suggest that despite theoretical advantages from reduced tissue trauma during laparoscopy, open surgery remains a viable and potentially equally effective long-term treatment option for adhesive small bowel obstruction, with choice of approach depending on patient-specific factors and surgeon expertise.

title: Sahin transvaginal extracorporeal myomectomy
summary: Uterine myomas are benign tumors that commonly cause abnormal uterine hemorrhage, pelvic pain, abdominal swelling, infertility, and miscarriage, with significant impacts on patient quality of life including depression. This study presents the Sahin transvaginal extracorporeal myomectomy (STVEC) technique as a minimally invasive, uterus-preserving surgical alternative to treat myomas while preserving ovarian function and hormonal status. The STVEC technique achieved hemostasis through lidocaine-adrenaline injection, demonstrated acceptable operative times averaging 71.9 minutes with a mean hospital stay of 31.9 hours, and effectively treated the primary symptoms of heavy menstrual bleeding (68% of patients) and pelvic pain (40.5% of patients). Perioperative outcomes were favorable with low complication rates—only 9% of patients required blood transfusion, with minimal postoperative infections, fever, hematoma, or wound dehiscence. Key factors requiring conversion to open laparotomy included adenomyotic uterus, dense pelvic adhesions, prior vaginal surgery, and distorted cervical anatomy in 4% of cases. The STVEC technique represents a safe and effective minimally invasive option for fibroid management that maintains fertility potential while providing symptom relief comparable to other advanced surgical approaches.

title: Prognostic Factors of Advanced Ovarian Cancer in the Era of HIPEC: A Multicenter Retrospective Study from an ESGO-Certified Center and an ESPSO-Certified Center
summary:
# Summary

This multicenter retrospective study evaluated prognostic factors in advanced ovarian cancer patients treated with cytoreductive surgery (CRS) combined with hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy (HIPEC). While CRS plus HIPEC was associated with significantly improved overall and progression-free survival compared to CRS alone, the treatment also carried higher rates of major postoperative complications, with HIPEC perfusion, high peritoneal cancer index, and greater intraoperative blood loss independently increasing complication risk. Multivariable analysis identified systematic lymphadenectomy and serous histology as independent predictors of improved progression-free and overall survival, while prior surgery emerged as the sole independent predictor of local-regional recurrence and macroscopic residual disease (CC-3) substantially elevated mortality risk. Complete or near-complete cytoreduction (CC-0/1) was achieved in 85.6% of patients and remained a critical prognostic factor, highlighting the importance of aggressive surgical debulking in advanced disease management.

title: Perioperative Outcomes of Total Vaginal Hysterectomy Versus Total Abdominal Hysterectomy: A Study of the Enlarged Uterus with Uterine Prolapse from Romania
summary:
# Summary

Total vaginal hysterectomy (TVH) demonstrates superior perioperative outcomes compared to total abdominal hysterectomy (TAH) for treating conditions including endometriosis, uterine prolapse, and leiomyomas. TVH was associated with significantly shorter operative time (median 60.50 versus 80.50 minutes), lower intra-operative blood loss (median 102.50 versus 132.50 mL), and reduced hospital stay (median 4.00 versus 5.00 days). Post-operative complications were substantially more frequent in the TAH group, with adhesions occurring in 33.33% of TAH cases versus only 13.33% of TVH cases, and overall complications at 9.99% versus 3.33% respectively. TVH's minimally invasive approach preserves pelvic floor support structures and causes less postoperative pain without leaving abdominal scars, making it the preferred method for managing enlarged uteri with prolapse when patient factors such as BMI and uterine size permit vaginal access.

title: Robot-Assisted Hysterectomy Provides Higher Sentinel Node Detection and Lower Conversion Rates Compared to Laparoscopy in Endometrial Cancer
summary: Robot-assisted hysterectomy (RAH) demonstrates superior performance compared to traditional laparoscopic hysterectomy (TLH) for surgical management of endometrial cancer, particularly in sentinel lymph node (SLN) detection and conversion avoidance. RAH achieved significantly higher SLN detection rates (98% versus 90.2%) and eliminated conversion to open laparotomy (0% versus 11.5%), outcomes likely attributed to the da Vinci system's three-dimensional high-definition imaging, tremor filtration, and enhanced tissue handling capabilities. The clinical significance of improved SLN mapping extends beyond surgical success rates, as sentinel mapping reduces complications such as lymphedema and nerve injury while increasing identification of micrometastases for more precise staging. Conversion to laparotomy carries substantial morbidity including increased bleeding risk and longer hospital stays, making the robotic approach's lower conversion rate particularly valuable in challenging cases involving severe obesity or complicated pelvic anatomy. Both procedural approaches remain safe and effective for treating early-stage endometrial cancer, yet robotic surgery's technical advantages translate to measurable improvements in oncologic staging accuracy and reduced operative morbidity.

title: National implementation of vaginal Natural Orifice Transluminal Endoscopic Surgery for benign hysterectomies: A historical cohort study of Swedish data 2021–2023
summary:
# Summary

This Swedish national implementation study (2021–2023) demonstrates that vaginal Natural Orifice Transluminal Endoscopic Surgery (vNOTES) hysterectomy significantly reduces operating time, hospitalization duration, postoperative pain, and overall complications compared to traditional approaches. The procedure successfully treats benign gynecological conditions including dysfunctional menstrual bleeding, cervical dysplasia, and pelvic pressure symptoms, with 90% of patients reporting satisfaction at one-year follow-up. Key technical advantages include endoscopic visualization of the vesico-uterine pouch (reducing bladder injury risk compared to manual anterior colpotomy), use of the Alexis ring for ureteral protection, and feasibility even for large uteri exceeding 500 grams.
