Notable safety concerns include bladder injury in 1.28% of cases (potentially related to McCall suture placement) and higher urinary tract infection rates, while rectovaginal endometriosis remains a contraindication due to difficult pouch of Douglas access. Economically, vNOTES proves cost-neutral to standard vaginal hysterectomy and more cost-effective than total laparoscopic, robotic, or abdominal approaches, with patients achieving faster return to daily activities.

title: Risk factors for vaginal cuff dehiscence after robot‐assisted total laparoscopic hysterectomy: A retrospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective cohort study identifies vaginal cuff dehiscence (VCD) as a serious postoperative complication following robot-assisted total laparoscopic hysterectomy, with rates particularly elevated compared to open surgical approaches. The research identifies multiple independent risk factors for VCD development: lower body mass index (BMI) acts as a protective factor when increased, with each unit increase reducing odds by 13%, while higher uterine weight significantly elevates risk, with each 100-gram increase raising odds by 30%. Prolonged operative duration and younger age also contribute to VCD risk, likely through mechanisms involving increased tissue trauma, contamination, and impaired healing. Notably, vaginal intercourse triggered VCD in 82% of symptomatic cases, with acute abdominal pain and vaginal bleeding being the most common presenting symptoms, while 25% of patients experienced the serious complication of bowel evisceration. The study emphasizes that patient counseling should specifically address increased VCD risk in women with low BMI and large uteri, and highlights the importance of adequate wound healing time before resuming sexual activity to reduce VCD incidence following minimally invasive hysterectomy.

title: Surgical Outcomes and Learning Curve of Robotic Single-Port Vaginal Natural Orifice Transluminal Endoscopic Surgery (vNOTES) Hysterectomy Using the Da Vinci Single-Port System
summary:
# Summary

Robotic single-port vaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery (RSP-vNOTES) hysterectomy using the da Vinci single-port system represents a minimally invasive surgical approach for treating endometriosis along with uterine fibroids, abnormal uterine bleeding, and pelvic organ prolapse. In a cohort of 97 patients with endometriosis (88.7% with pathologic confirmation across all disease stages), the procedure demonstrated excellent safety and efficacy, with a 1.0% conversion rate, 14.4% complication rate comparable to other robot-assisted approaches, and a high same-day discharge rate of 93.8%. The articulated, wristed instruments of the RSP platform facilitated complex endometriotic excision and provided ergonomic advantages, though proficiency required approximately 30 cases according to cumulative sum analysis—a steeper learning curve than multiport robot-assisted systems. Surgical times were efficient, with median hysterectomy time of 35 minutes and minimal blood loss (25 mL median), while postoperative pain scores decreased significantly across weeks 1 through 6 compared to baseline. The da Vinci single-port system has successfully enabled true single-site surgery, establishing RSP-vNOTES as a viable advanced surgical option for benign gynecologic indications including endometriosis management.

title: Bladder flap creation during cesarean delivery. A systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials
summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials evaluates the clinical utility of bladder flap creation during cesarean delivery across multiple perioperative outcomes. The analysis of over 3,000 participants demonstrates that routine bladder flap creation significantly prolongs operative time by approximately 8 minutes and incision-to-delivery time while providing no meaningful benefit in preventing bladder injury, urinary tract infections, or urinary retention. Notably, bladder flap creation is associated with a 3.23-fold increased odds of postoperative microscopic hematuria, heightened postoperative pain, and delayed gastrointestinal recovery, likely reflecting mucosal microtrauma and devascularization during flap dissection. Histopathological evidence reveals that blade flap creation triggers chronic inflammatory, fibrotic, and neovascularization changes with higher adhesion rates, potentially predisposing to complications in repeat cesarean deliveries. The findings indicate that omission of bladder flap creation achieves comparable safety profiles while reducing operative time, postoperative pain, and hematuria, supporting a reassessment of routine flap creation as standard practice during cesarean delivery.

title: Worldwide research tendencies on natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery: a bibliometric analysis
summary: This bibliometric analysis examines global research trends in natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery (NOTES), a minimally invasive approach that accesses organs through natural body openings rather than external incisions. NOTES encompasses resection of multiple organs including the gallbladder, liver, appendix, ovaries, and uterus, with applications across digestive, urological, and gynecological disorders. Key procedures include transgastric and transvaginal approaches to cholecystectomy, appendectomy, hysterectomy, and nephrectomy, which reduce postoperative pain and accelerate recovery by minimizing tissue damage. Vaginal NOTES (VNOTES) has emerged as a feasible and safe treatment for benign gynecological diseases, including hysterectomy and ovarian cystectomy, though its application in complex cases such as endometriosis remains in early development. The United States leads NOTES research with 549 publications (25% globally), followed by China and Germany, with Harvard University and Mayo Clinic as leading research institutions. Recent research trends (2021-2025) show increased focus on VNOTES applications, hysterectomy, pelvic organ prolapse, and endometrial cancer, reflecting the expanding clinical relevance of minimally invasive approaches compared to traditional laparoscopic surgery.

title: Integration of Indocyanine Green Sentinel Lymph Node Mapping and Laparoscopic Uterosacral Nerve Ablation: Advancements in Minimally Invasive Gynecologic Surgery
summary: This article examines two complementary minimally invasive surgical techniques for gynecologic pathology: indocyanine green (ICG) sentinel lymph node mapping and laparoscopic uterosacral nerve ablation (LUNA). ICG-based SLN mapping improves staging precision in endometrial cancer while reducing surgical morbidity by decreasing lymphedema and vascular injury risk compared to traditional pelvic lymphadenectomy, demonstrating high sensitivity and negative predictive value despite challenges in obese patients. LUNA addresses endometriosis-associated chronic pelvic pain through nerve ablation, producing significant improvements in pain tolerance, quality of life, work capacity, and reductions in psychological distress and sexual dysfunction, though effectiveness varies depending on pain etiology. The integration of these techniques represents an advancement in minimally invasive gynecologic surgery, offering patients shorter recovery times and fewer complications than more radical alternatives such as presacral neurectomy. However, the benefits of LUNA for endometriosis-related pain demonstrate variable outcomes, with some patients experiencing limited pain relief, suggesting that careful patient selection and counseling remain essential for optimizing surgical outcomes.

title: Harmful incidents following gynaecological ambulatory surgery: A scoping review
journal: International Journal of Nursing Studies Advances
summary: This scoping review systematically identifies harmful incidents following gynaecological ambulatory surgery, with laparoscopic hysterectomy representing a key procedural focus. Postoperative infections, bleeding, and pain emerge as the most frequently reported physical complications, with postoperative infections—particularly urinary tract infections and surgical site infections at the vaginal cuff—reported across the highest number of studies. Additional serious complications include bleeding-related readmissions, wound problems, fistula formation, urinary retention, and thromboembolic disease, demonstrating that while minimally invasive surgical techniques and advancements in anaesthesiology have enhanced procedural safety, significant risks persist. Beyond physical harm, patients experience psychological consequences including anxiety, reduced social participation, and feelings of abandonment following ambulatory surgery. Nerve injury represents a notable complication specifically associated with laparoscopic hysterectomy, while oncologic indications carry elevated risks of lymphedema, pelvic fluid collection, and mortality. The review underscores that most harmful incidents result from physiological impacts and tissue damage, emphasizing the need for comprehensive perioperative monitoring and patient counseling regarding both physical and psychological recovery outcomes.

title: Patient-reported outcomes after hysterectomy due to pelvic organ prolapse versus other indications – a 10-year national follow-up study
summary:
# Summary

This 10-year national follow-up study compared patient-reported outcomes after hysterectomy for pelvic organ prolapse with urinary incontinence (POPUI) versus other indications such as myoma, abnormal uterine bleeding, dysmenorrhea, and endometriosis. Women undergoing hysterectomy for POPUI experienced significantly worse long-term satisfaction, with 86.7% reporting persistent pelvic floor symptoms and 20.7% requiring reoperations compared to 2.3% in the non-POPUI group. Multiple postoperative complications—including reoperation, wound infection, urinary tract infection, and inadequate pain relief—substantially increased dissatisfaction risk, with inadequate pain relief showing the strongest association (OR 4.0). Conversely, older age, symptomatic bulging, and urinary symptoms at follow-up predicted greater dissatisfaction, while higher BMI and parity with three or more children were protective factors. Although hysterectomy generally improves health-related quality of life by treating pelvic floor and urinary conditions, the study demonstrates that POPUI as the primary surgical indication carries substantially elevated risks for persistent symptoms and patient dissatisfaction at long-term follow-up, suggesting the need for careful patient counseling and optimized perioperative management.

title: Comparison of Surgical Complications in Staged Versus Combined Hysterectomy Approach in Masculinizing Bottom Surgery: A National Surgical Quality Improvement Program Analysis
summary: This study compared surgical outcomes between single-stage and staged approaches for masculinizing genitoplasty with hysterectomy using national surgical quality data. Single-stage genital affirming bottom surgery (GABS) combined with hysterectomy demonstrated significantly superior safety profiles, with staged procedures showing substantially higher complication rates including return to operating room (14.5% vs 0.0%), wound complications (22.9% vs 0.0%), and overall complications (27.7% vs 6.7%). The combined approach not only reduced surgical morbidity but also improved operational efficiency and patient satisfaction by consolidating procedures into a single anesthetic event. These findings suggest that coordinating hysterectomy with GABS in a single surgical session represents a safer and more efficient strategy for transgender patients requiring both procedures, potentially improving access to care while minimizing repeat surgical interventions.

title: Prioritizing vaginal hysterectomy: a stepped algorithm with vNOTES support for benign indications
summary:
# Summary

This study presents a stepped surgical algorithm prioritizing vaginal hysterectomy for benign gynecological indications, with vaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery (vNOTES) as a bridge technique to minimize abdominal conversion. Vaginal hysterectomy successfully reduces surgical complications compared to abdominal approaches, achieving vaginal completion in 95.2% of cases, while vNOTES support offers advantages including shorter operative times, reduced postoperative pain, and faster recovery compared to total laparoscopic hysterectomy. Uterine weight emerged as the sole independent predictor of pure vaginal hysterectomy failure, with each 100-gram increase elevating failure probability; conversely, surgeon experience significantly improved pure vaginal hysterectomy success rates (81.7–93.9%) while decreasing vNOTES utilization. Dense pelvic adhesions and stage 3–4 endometriosis represented contraindications requiring conversion, whereas adnexal pathology and inadequate uterine mobility were the primary indications for vNOTES transition, allowing safe management without full laparoscopic conversion. Notably, obesity and previous cesarean deliveries did not compromise success rates (93.1% and 96% respectively), demonstrating that a vaginal-first approach with vNOTES support optimizes minimally invasive benefits across diverse patient populations.

title: Advances of regional nerve blocks for postoperative analgesia in laparoscopic gynecological surgery: precision, innovation, and future directions
summary:
# Summary

Laparoscopic hysterectomy for endometriosis, despite its minimally invasive nature, produces complex and prolonged postoperative pain requiring optimized analgesia strategies. Regional nerve blocks have emerged as cornerstones of Enhanced Recovery After Surgery protocols, offering superior opioid-sparing analgesia compared to traditional systemic approaches. Ultrasound-guided fascial plane blocks targeting the abdominal wall—including the Transversus Abdominis Plane Block, Quadratus Lumborum Block, and Erector Spinae Plane Block—effectively reduce postoperative pain scores and analgesic consumption by blocking somatic innervation. The Quadratus Lumborum Block offers additional visceral analgesia through paravertebral spread toward sympathetic fibers, while blocks targeting the superior hypogastric plexus directly address pelvic visceral nociception, complementing somatic blockade. Although these techniques demonstrate efficacy, each carries distinct safety considerations—including femoral nerve weakness with transmuscular QLB and pneumothorax risk with ESPB—requiring careful patient selection and technical precision, particularly with ultrasound guidance, to optimize pain control while minimizing complications in laparoscopic gynecological procedures.

title: Attention to the Editor – Case series: “Laparoscopic robot-assisted extravesical repair of vesicovaginal fistula post-hysterectomy: a study of peritoneal flap inlay technique and outcomes”
summary:
# Summary

Vesicovaginal fistulas occur as a rare but significant complication in approximately one of 1,800 hysterectomy cases, causing considerable physical, emotional distress and declining quality of life. This case series demonstrates that robot-assisted laparoscopic repair using an extravesical approach with peritoneal flap interposition successfully closed fistulas in all 21 patients, achieving complete continence with no recurrence over a median 20-month follow-up period. The robot-assisted technique provides substantial advantages over conventional open repair, including reduced hospital stay, faster recovery, improved cosmetic outcomes, and enhanced surgical precision through high-definition three-dimensional visualization and articulated instrumentation. Postoperative assessments using standardized questionnaires revealed significant improvements in life satisfaction, psychological distress, and sexual function in all patients. The peritoneal flap interposition technique, combined with principles of adequate tissue vascularization, infection control, complete fistula tract removal, tension-free multilayer closure, and sustained bladder drainage, proved critical to successful outcomes. These findings establish robot-assisted extravesical repair as an effective and minimally invasive alternative to traditional approaches for managing this serious surgical complication.

title: Ruptured Tubal Ectopic Pregnancy Managed by Salpingectomy: vNOTES versus Conventional Laparoscopy
journal: JSLS : Journal of the Society of Laparoscopic & Robotic Surgeons
summary:
# Summary

Transvaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery (vNOTES) represents a feasible and advantageous minimally invasive approach for managing ruptured tubal ectopic pregnancy through salpingectomy, offering multiple clinical benefits compared to conventional laparoscopy. In a comparative analysis of 24 patients, vNOTES significantly reduced operative time (30 ± 10 minutes versus 41 ± 14 minutes), required lower insufflation pressure (8 mmHg versus 13 mmHg), and decreased postoperative pain across all measured timepoints, with substantially lower rates of shoulder tip discomfort (17% versus 83%) due to reduced diaphragmatic irritation. The vNOTES approach eliminated the need for abdominal trocar placement, resulting in shorter hospital stays (36 ± 13 hours versus 55 ± 14 hours), no requirement for pelvic drains, and the elimination of trocar-related complications such as visible scars, port-site hernias, and adhesions. Safety outcomes were equivalent between both techniques, with neither group experiencing intraoperative complications or requiring conversion to laparotomy, while both groups demonstrated comparable hematocrit variations and postoperative opioid requirements. These findings support vNOTES as an effective alternative to conventional laparoscopy for ectopic pregnancy management, particularly benefiting patient recovery and reducing hospital resource utilization through compatibility with Enhanced Recovery After Surgery protocols.

title: V-NOSE en bloc laparoscopic hysterectomy with segmental bowel resection for deep infiltrating endometriosis (#ENZIAN C3)
summary: Deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIE) with bowel involvement presents a complex surgical challenge, occurring in up to 16% of endometriosis cases and commonly accompanied by severe dysmenorrhea, deep dyspareunia, and intestinal symptoms. The V-NOSE en bloc laparoscopic hysterectomy technique represents an innovative surgical approach that combines complete en bloc resection of the uterus, appendix, and segmental bowel tissue to achieve comprehensive excision of DIE. This minimally invasive strategy integrates anterior colpotomy with the uterus attached to the rectovaginal nodule and employs vaginal specimen extraction (V-NOSE) to facilitate tissue removal while maintaining procedural efficiency. By enabling complete excision of deeply infiltrating lesions with bowel involvement through a unified en bloc resection, this laparoscopic approach potentially reduces operative time compared to traditional techniques. The technique demonstrates that careful integration of multiple laparoscopic steps—including appropriate colpotomy and specimen extraction methods—allows effective management of advanced endometriosis cases that would otherwise require more extensive open surgical approaches.

title: Surgical outcomes according to the degree of parametrial dissection with hysterectomy for deep endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluated surgical outcomes of hysterectomy with varying degrees of parametrial dissection for parametrial deep endometriosis, a complex disease form associated with severe pelvic pain and bladder dysfunction. All surgical approaches—extended hysterectomy with superficial parametrectomy and three types of deep parametrectomy (EHDP1-3)—significantly improved pain symptoms, bowel function, and gastrointestinal quality of life. However, deeper parametrial dissection increased postoperative bladder voiding dysfunction risk, particularly with EHDP1 and EHDP3 (31.6% incidence), and deeper dissection beyond the presacral fascia and middle rectal artery risked neurovegetative injury. Extended hysterectomy for deep endometriosis required ureterolysis in nearly 90% of cases and frequently involved segmental bowel resection, though serious complications were rare. The findings suggest that while aggressive parametrial dissection achieves excellent symptom relief, less extensive approaches like EHDP3 may optimize functional outcomes by improving urinary and sexual function while reducing morbidity from neural and urinary tract injury.

title: Port placement and patient-specific docking strategies for robotic hysterectomy with the Hugo™ RAS system: an international Delphi consensus
summary:
# Summary

This international Delphi consensus study established expert-driven recommendations for port placement and docking strategies during robotic hysterectomy using the Hugo RAS system, a newly approved platform with four independent robotic arm carts offering substantial flexibility compared to boom-based alternatives. The strongest area of expert agreement identified the need to modify port placement based on patient anthropometric characteristics, particularly elevated BMI (requiring increased inter-port distance), large uterine size (requiring cranial port displacement), and short umbilico-pubic distance, while small pelvic anatomy did not yield consensus on a single modification strategy. The modified bridge configuration, which combines elements of three-arm and standard four-arm setups with standardized spacing parameters, achieved the highest expert support (93.7%), followed by the standard hysterectomy setup as the most preferred overall configuration. Notably, no single docking configuration reached consensus as universally optimal, reflecting the heterogeneity of current clinical practice and underscoring that optimal use of the Hugo RAS system requires flexibility and case-specific adaptation to individual patient anatomies. Key modifications identified included positioning the surgical assistant at Palmer's point and employing a compact cart configuration, endorsed by the majority of experts who were predominantly experienced surgeons from academic settings with median clinical experience of 20 years.

title: Effect of adhesions on laparoscopically-assisted vaginal hysterectomy outcome: a 10-year retrospective, comparative study of 1683 consecutive cases
summary:
# Summary

This 10-year retrospective study of 1,683 laparoscopically-assisted vaginal hysterectomy (LAVH) cases demonstrates that peritoneal adhesions significantly complicate surgical outcomes, increasing operation time by approximately 50 minutes and raising intraoperative complication rates from 0.7% to 2%, particularly through bowel and urinary bladder injuries. The procedure was primarily performed for myoma (71%) and adenomyosis (14.9%), with adhesiolysis required in 88% of patients presenting with adhesions. Despite these challenges, LAVH remains a minimally-invasive treatment option that offers substantial advantages over abdominal hysterectomy, including reduced operative time, blood loss, postoperative pain, and faster recovery, while facilitating treatment of adhesions and additional pelvic pathology including endometriosis removal. The study confirms that laparoconversion occurred more frequently in patients with extensive adhesions or large uterine size (1.6% overall), and that surgical expertise and preoperative assessment of adhesion severity are critical for optimizing patient safety and informed consent. Overall, LAVH maintains a low postoperative complication rate (3.4% in the adhesion group) and should remain the preferred approach for hysterectomy in appropriately selected candidates.

title: Total versus subtotal hysterectomy for benign gynaecological conditions
journal: The Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews
summary:
# Summary

This Cochrane systematic review compares total hysterectomy (removal of uterus and cervix) versus subtotal hysterectomy (removal of uterine body only) for benign gynecological conditions, including recto-vaginal endometriosis. Subtotal hysterectomy demonstrates significant operative advantages, reducing operating time by approximately 11 minutes, blood loss by 57 milliliters, and substantially lowering postoperative fever and urinary retention risks. The procedure theoretically preserves nerve supply to pelvic structures by requiring less tissue dissection, potentially reducing risks of bladder damage, ureteral injury, and pelvic hematoma. However, subtotal hysterectomy carries a major disadvantage of substantially increased cyclical vaginal bleeding after surgery. The two approaches show no significant differences in functional outcomes including urinary, bowel, and sexual function, nor in recovery time, symptom relief, or transfusion requirements. The laparoscopic approach has increased subtotal hysterectomy utilization, particularly for endometriosis cases where cervical conservation may be beneficial, though concerns remain regarding cervical stump malignancy risk.

title: Surgical Approaches to Vaginal Agenesis: The Past, Present and Future
summary:
# Summary

Vaginal agenesis, most commonly caused by Mayer-Rokitansky-Küster-Hauser syndrome or Andersen Insensitivity Syndrome, requires surgical intervention when nonsurgical approaches like dilator therapy prove insufficient. Multiple surgical techniques have been developed to create functional neovaginas, including the McIndoe procedure with split-thickness grafts, the Vecchietti procedure with progressive traction, the Davydov procedure using peritoneal inlay, and intestinal neovagina creation using sigmoid colon—each with distinct advantages and complication profiles. Emerging innovations such as tissue engineering-supported approaches, decellularized basement membrane products, and minimally invasive techniques like single-port laparoscopy represent promising advancements in reducing morbidity while improving functional outcomes. Long-term complications remain common across all techniques and include vaginal stenosis, loss of length, fistula formation, and prolapse, necessitating lifelong surveillance and maintenance through continued dilation or coitus. The choice of surgical approach must be individualized based on patient factors, surgeon expertise, and consideration of serious complications such as HPV-related dysplasia and, rarely, malignant transformation in intestinal neovaginas.

title: Perioperative outcomes and anesthetic challenges of robotic-assisted gynecologic surgery: a systematic review
journal: Journal of Anesthesia, Analgesia and Critical Care
summary:
# Summary

Robotic-assisted gynecologic surgery represents a feasible and safe alternative to laparoscopic and open approaches for gynecologic procedures, including endometriosis treatment, offering enhanced precision, ergonomics, and visualization compared to traditional techniques. This approach demonstrates significant perioperative advantages including shorter hospital stays, faster return to daily activities and ambulation, reduced blood loss, lower postoperative pain intensity, reduced opioid consumption, and lower conversion rates compared to open surgery and sometimes laparoscopic approaches. Oncologic outcomes including lymph node yield and R0 resection are comparable across all three surgical modalities. However, robotic-assisted surgery introduces distinct anesthetic challenges related to patient positioning, particularly steep Trendelenburg positioning and prolonged pneumoperitoneum, which impair pulmonary compliance, reduce functional residual capacity, increase peak inspiratory pressures and end-tidal CO₂ levels, and may increase intracranial pressure through optic nerve sheath diameter expansion. Despite these physiological demands, pulmonary complication rates remain similar to laparoscopic approaches, and major hemodynamic complications are infrequent, though intolerance to Trendelenburg positioning occasionally necessitates conversion to laparotomy.

title: Association between hysterectomy and dementia risk in Korean women aged 40–59: a nationwide retrospective cohort study
summary: This nationwide Korean cohort study examined whether hysterectomy increases dementia risk in women aged 40–59, focusing on the mechanistic role of estrogen depletion. Estrogen normally supports neuroprotection through multiple pathways: maintaining synaptic plasticity, preserving cerebral perfusion, reducing beta-amyloid accumulation, and limiting oxidative stress. However, the study found no significant association between hysterectomy and all-cause dementia, Alzheimer's disease, or vascular dementia, even when restricted to laparoscopic procedures or comparing surgeries with and without ovarian removal. The key distinction emerged that shorter lifetime estrogen exposure, rather than hysterectomy itself, drives dementia risk—a finding supported by evidence that early surgical menopause with bilateral oophorectomy before age 50 substantially elevates dementia risk. The authors conclude that hysterectomy without premature ovarian failure does not meaningfully increase cognitive decline in midlife women, though menopausal hormone therapy after surgery may help mitigate hormonal effects on brain health when clinically appropriate.

title: EHS Guidelines on the Management of Primary Ventral and Incisional Hernias Under Emergency Conditions
summary:
# Summary

Primary ventral and incisional hernias (PVIHs) represent a common surgical emergency with serious complications including bowel obstruction, strangulation, and peritonitis, and carry substantially higher mortality rates when repaired emergently compared to elective procedures. Emergent incisional hernia repair has become increasingly prevalent in older men and is associated with significant morbidity, particularly surgical site infections. Current guidelines recommend a damage control approach that prioritizes physiological stability, with mesh-based repair (via laparoscopic intraperitoneal or open onlay placement using large-pore synthetic meshes) preferred over primary suture repair for amenable defects. For large defects that cannot be closed primarily, a staged approach utilizing temporary abdominal closure is recommended to avoid immediate mesh placement. Adherence to these evidence-based management strategies can standardize PVIH treatment and improve patient outcomes in this high-risk emergency population.

title: Pain after laparoscopic surgery: Focus on shoulder-tip pain after gynecological laparoscopic surgery
journal: Journal of the Chinese Medical Association : JCMA
summary:
# Summary

Laparoscopic surgery remains the preferred treatment for endometriosis due to minimal incisional trauma; however, up to 80% of patients experience significant postoperative pain, particularly shoulder-tip pain resulting from pneumoperitoneum-induced phrenic neuropraxia. Three primary mechanisms explain postlaparoscopic shoulder-tip pain: carbon dioxide conversion to carbonic acid damages peritoneal and diaphragmatic nerves; residual CO₂ gas pockets create traction on hepatic ligaments through loss of negative pressure; and pneumoperitoneum stretches the peritoneum and diaphragm, causing phrenic nerve injury and inflammatory mediator release, with pain severity correlating directly to stretching degree. Multiple interventions effectively reduce pain outcomes: low-pressure pneumoperitoneum and active gas aspiration decrease CO₂-related irritation, while warmed and humidified CO₂ prevents hypothermia-associated pain amplification. Pharmacological approaches including nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, local anesthetic infiltration, and carbonic anhydrase inhibitors complement surgical modifications, offering clinicians evidence-based strategies to minimize postoperative morbidity in laparoscopic gynecological procedures.

title: Vaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery versus laparoscopy for ovarian cystectomy: a prospective, open-label, randomized, non-inferiority pilot trial
summary: Vaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery (vNOTES) demonstrates non-inferiority to laparoscopy for ovarian cystectomy in selected patients, with distinct advantages in postoperative outcomes. Compared to traditional laparoscopy, vNOTES achieved significantly lower postoperative pain scores, shorter time to first postoperative flatus, and superior cosmetic results through scarless, vaginal specimen retrieval. Operating times for specimen retrieval were notably shorter with vNOTES, and the procedure maintained a low complication rate comparable to laparoscopy with minimal infection risk. However, estimated blood loss was greater in the vNOTES group, and the technique is unsuitable for patients with severe endometriosis due to associated pelvic adhesions, as evidenced by one case requiring conversion to laparoscopy. These findings support vNOTES as a feasible and safe alternative for carefully selected patients with benign ovarian tumors who desire fertility preservation and rapid recovery with minimal postoperative discomfort.

title: Laparoscopic vs. laparotomy tubal recanalization for fertility restoration after tubal sterilization: a retrospective analysis
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective analysis compared laparoscopic and laparotomy approaches for tubal recanalization in women seeking fertility restoration after tubal sterilization, demonstrating superior outcomes with the minimally invasive technique. Laparoscopic recanalization achieved significantly higher intrauterine pregnancy rates (70.49% versus 42.62%), lower postoperative pain, reduced blood loss, and shorter hospital stays compared to open laparotomy. Key predictors of pregnancy success included patient age at recanalization and reconstructed tubal length, with patients aged 35 years or younger and those with tubal length exceeding 8 centimeters achieving the highest pregnancy rates (80% and 100%, respectively). The type of ligation technique and ligation location influenced outcomes, with isthmus-isthmus ligation showing superior results (72.46% pregnancy rate) compared to ampulla ligation (52.94%). Microsurgical techniques, including complete scar resection and atraumatic suturing, enhanced fertility potential while reducing complications, with laparoscopic patients experiencing no postoperative infections compared to wound site infections in the laparotomy group.

title: “I had a feeling the period did not have an opening to come out:” A qualitative assessment of Type III Female Genital Mutilation/Cutting and menstrual health among Somali communities in Kenya
summary:
# Summary

This qualitative study examines how infibulation (Type III female genital mutilation/cutting) affects menstrual health among Somali women in Kenya, revealing that infibulation causes significant menstrual complications including pain, irregular bleeding, and menstrual flow obstruction. Beyond menstrual effects, FGM/C is associated with immediate and long-term health consequences such as excessive bleeding, scarring, urinary tract infections, sexual dysfunction, and mental health disorders. The research identifies critical barriers to adequate care, including healthcare providers' lack of training and preparation to address menstrual health concerns in women with FGM/C, women's distrust of formal healthcare systems, and male-centered family decision-making that limits women's healthcare access. Knowledge about menstruation and infibulation is primarily transmitted through informal discussions among female relatives and community women, often leading to confusion and fear during menarche, though some women report managing menstrual discomfort with over-the-counter pain analgesics. The study underscores that menstrual health requires comprehensive access to education, appropriate medical care, and menstrual management resources—elements currently unavailable to many Somali women with FGM/C histories.

title: Complex vaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery hysterectomy for symptomatic giant polymyomatous uteri in nulliparous women
summary: Vaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery (vNOTES) hysterectomy effectively treats symptomatic giant polymyomatous uteri, which present surgical challenges due to limited pelvic space and increased vascularity. This minimally invasive approach provides superior perioperative outcomes compared to conventional laparotomy and laparoscopy-assisted techniques, including reduced blood loss, shorter operative times, and minimal hemoglobin decrease achieved through early uterine artery control and stepwise debulking. The avoidance of abdominal incisions and trocar-related complications contributes to significantly reduced postoperative pain, earlier mobilization, and faster recovery, with patients achieving hospital discharge in less than one day. Enhanced endoscopic vision via the vaginal route minimizes intraoperative complications and permits efficient specimen extraction even in cases of large uteri, with the entire series achieving completion without conversion to alternative approaches. The overall complication rate remained low at 8%, with only isolated cases of transfusion and fever reported, though careful consideration is warranted in patients with prior cesarean sections or endometriomal cysts. These advantages translate to improved patient satisfaction and reduced healthcare resource utilization, establishing vNOTES as a feasible and safe option for managing symptomatic giant fibroids in nulliparous women.

title: Trends and comparative study of robotic-assisted benign hysterectomy among morbidly obese patients in united States
summary:
# Summary

This comparative analysis demonstrates that robotic-assisted hysterectomy provides significant advantages for morbidly obese patients undergoing benign gynecologic procedures, including endometriosis treatment. Compared to conventional laparoscopy, robotic-assisted hysterectomy substantially reduced perioperative bleeding (2.8% vs. 4.0%), conversion to open surgery (3.5% vs. 7.3%), and operative time (179 vs. 186 minutes), with these benefits extending even to superobese patients (BMI >50 kg/m²). The superiority of robotic platforms in this high-risk population is attributed to improved surgeon ergonomics, better surgical field exposure, and fixed robotic arms that may reduce intra-abdominal pressure and associated pulmonary complications. Between 2013 and 2020, robotic-assisted hysterectomy became the predominant minimally invasive approach in morbidly obese women (40.8%), facilitating broader adoption of minimally invasive surgery across the obesity spectrum while maintaining low complication rates and enabling outpatient procedures. These findings support the expanding use of robotic technology for benign indications such as endometriosis, fibroids, and chronic pelvic pain in obese populations where technical challenges typically favor more invasive approaches.

title: Incidence of surgical site infection surveillance in hysterectomies and related risk factors: a prospective cohort study
summary:
# Article Summary

This prospective cohort study examined surgical site infection (SSI) rates and risk factors in hysterectomy patients, including those undergoing surgery for endometriosis. The overall SSI incidence was 2.5%, with vaginal hysterectomy showing higher infection rates (3.4%) compared to abdominal hysterectomy (1.9%), while laparoscopic approaches were associated with shorter operative times. Independent risk factors for SSI included diabetes mellitus (OR: 2.9) and cancer (OR: 3.7), along with higher ASA scores and elevated NHSN risk indices. Escherichia coli was the predominant causative organism (41%), followed by Enterococcus faecalis and other gram-negative bacteria, with prophylactic antibiotic use and chlorhexidine skin preparation identified as effective preventive measures. SSIs significantly prolonged hospital stays and increased healthcare costs, demonstrating the clinical and economic importance of perioperative infection prevention strategies in hysterectomy patients.

title: Perioperative Outcomes of Robotic Versus Conventional Total Laparoscopic Hysterectomy in Surgically Complex Cases: A Propensity Score-Matched Study
summary:
# Summary

This propensity score-matched study compared robotic versus conventional total laparoscopic hysterectomy in surgically complex cases involving endometriosis and associated pelvic adhesions. While robotic surgery required longer operative times, it achieved significantly lower estimated blood loss compared to conventional laparoscopy, likely due to the da Vinci platform's enhanced three-dimensional visualization, articulated instruments, and improved hemostasis capabilities. These advantages are particularly valuable in complex cases with large uteri and extensive pelvic adhesions, where conventional rigid instruments face mechanical limitations and increased risk of adjacent structure injury. Despite longer operative times with robotic approach, postoperative hemoglobin decline, hospital length of stay, and complication rates remained comparable between groups, with no conversions to open surgery in either cohort. The findings suggest that robotic-assisted hysterectomy offers meaningful intraoperative benefits in surgically complex endometriosis cases without compromising short-term clinical outcomes.

title: Surgically Relative Risk Factors for Lower Colorectal Anastomotic Dehiscence and Rectovaginal Fistulas in Complex Deep Endometriosis Cases: A Single-Center Retrospective–Prospective Cohort Study
summary:
# Summary

Deep endometriosis frequently involves the bowel, particularly the rectum and sigmoid colon, necessitating surgical management through segmental resection, discoid resection, or shaving procedures depending on lesion size and depth. Anastomotic leakage and rectovaginal fistula formation represent the most serious postoperative complications, occurring in 2.8% of cases overall but associated with specific risk factors including very low anastomoses (<6 cm), levator ani infiltration, prolonged operative time, concomitant hysterectomy or vaginal opening, and segmental resection type. Protective measures including fibrin glue (88.2% of cases), omental flaps, reinforcing sutures, and particularly protective stomas prevent anastomotic complications, with protective stomas achieving 100% success in ultra-low anastomoses. Discoid resection emerged as a safer alternative to segmental resection for appropriate lesions, while intraoperative indocyanine green mapping enhanced surgical precision in bowel transection. Comprehensive perioperative management including prehabilitation, antibiotic prophylaxis, thromboprophylaxis, and probiotic supplementation supported optimal recovery outcomes in this complex surgical population.

title: Risk factors for surgical site infections after hysterectomy: A systematic review and meta-analysis
summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis identified multiple significant risk factors for surgical site infections (SSIs) following hysterectomy, a procedure commonly used to treat endometriosis, uterine fibroids, and gynecologic malignancies. Obesity (BMI >30 kg/m²) emerged as the strongest modifiable risk factor, increasing SSI risk more than threefold (OR 3.34), followed by diabetes mellitus (OR 1.82) and smoking (OR 1.76), each operating through distinct mechanisms including impaired vascularity, immune suppression, and delayed wound healing. Surgical and procedural factors were equally important: open abdominal approaches carried nearly threefold higher SSI risk compared to minimally invasive methods, while prolonged operative time (>2 hours), bowel resection, and prolonged drainage duration all substantially increased infection rates. Patient comorbidity burden, reflected in ASA scores >3 and advanced disease stages in oncologic cases, further elevated SSI risk, underscoring how complex pathology compounds surgical stress. The analysis demonstrates that SSIs significantly prolong hospitalization and increase readmission rates, highlighting the clinical importance of preoperative optimization for obese and diabetic patients, smoking cessation, and careful surgical technique selection to minimize infection complications.

title: Clinical Outcomes of Robotic-Assisted Gynecologic Surgery: A Single-Center 70-Case Series Experience
summary:
# Summary

This single-center series of 70 robotic-assisted gynecologic procedures demonstrates that robotic surgery significantly improves clinical outcomes across multiple benign gynecologic conditions, including endometriosis-related pathology. Robotic platforms provide superior three-dimensional high-definition visualization, tremor elimination, and enhanced instrument articulation, enabling precise dissection and accurate suturing that are particularly valuable in complex pelvic cases. Robotic cystectomy for endometriotic cysts achieved the shortest operative time (60.3 minutes) with minimal blood loss (28.3 mL) and brief hospital stays, while robotic hysterectomy and myomectomy effectively treated abnormal uterine bleeding, adenomyosis, and uterine fibroids with acceptable perioperative outcomes. Compared to conventional open gynecologic surgery, which carries substantial risks of increased blood loss and pain, robotic-assisted approaches achieved minimal perioperative blood loss, short hospital stays averaging 2.3-2.6 days, and notably, zero intraoperative or postoperative complications. These findings support robotic-assisted surgery as a safe and effective minimally invasive option for managing benign gynecologic diseases with superior visualization and surgical precision while preserving critical pelvic anatomy and fertility potential.
